title: Homotopically Non-Trivial Maps with Small k-Dilation
overall_summary: The paper studies when bounds on k-dimensional volume expansion force a map between spheres to be homotopically trivial, and shows that for k at least 3 the answer can be very different from the 2-dilation case. Its core construction embeds a highly anisotropic rectangle in a sphere, maps one factor with large Lipschitz constant and another factor with very small Lipschitz constant, and uses a singular-value count to make every k-dimensional Jacobian small while preserving a suspended homotopy class. The paper also surveys lower-bound mechanisms for k-dilation, especially Tsui-Wang and Gromov results for small 2-dilation and Gromov's Hopf-invariant estimate via differential forms and Hodge theory. For the original rectangle problem, the most useful extracted content is the anisotropic product-map estimate, the exterior-power singular-value bookkeeping, and the contrast between rank-collapse constructions and degree or cohomological lower bounds.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Basic singular-value estimates for k-dilation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a C^1 map f between Riemannian manifolds, the k-dilation is the supremum over the domain of the operator norm |Λ^k df|. If the domain dimension is m and the singular values of df at a point are ordered as 0 ≤ s_1 ≤ ... ≤ s_m, then |Λ^k df| at that point is s_{m-k+1}...s_m, the product of the k largest singular values. Consequently, for 1 ≤ k < m, one has the pointwise inequality |Λ^{k+1} df| ≤ |Λ^k df|^{(k+1)/k}; hence if f has k-dilation D, then f has (k+1)-dilation at most D^{(k+1)/k}. Also, if F is L-Lipschitz, then the k-dilation of F ∘ f is at most L^k times the k-dilation of f.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: At a point, choose orthonormal bases diagonalizing df in singular-value form. On a decomposable basis k-vector e_{i_1} ∧ ... ∧ e_{i_k}, the exterior derivative Λ^k df multiplies lengths by s_{i_1}...s_{i_k}; the operator norm is therefore the largest such product, namely the product of the k largest singular values. If P = s_{m-k+1}...s_m, then s_{m-k} ≤ P^{1/k}, because each of the k factors in P is at least s_{m-k}. Thus |Λ^{k+1} df| = s_{m-k}P ≤ P^{(k+1)/k}. The composition estimate follows from Λ^k d(F ∘ f) = Λ^k dF ∘ Λ^k df and |Λ^k dF| ≤ L^k.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1, suspension classes with arbitrarily small k-dilation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a homotopy class a in π_m(S^n). Let Σ^p a denote its p-fold suspension, an element of π_{m+p}(S^{n+p}). If k is an integer satisfying k > n + (n/m)p, then the suspended class Σ^p a can be represented by maps S^{m+p} → S^{n+p} with arbitrarily small k-dilation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Represent a by a C^1 map f_1: [0,1]^m → S^n that sends the boundary of the cube to the basepoint. Choose a C^1 degree-one map f_2: [0,1]^p → S^p that sends the boundary to the basepoint. Let L be larger than the Lipschitz constants of both f_1 and f_2. For small ε > 0, the paper uses a quasi-isometric embedding, with ε-independent quasi-isometry constant, of the rectangle R_ε = [0,ε]^m × [0,ε^{-m/p}]^p into the unit sphere S^{m+p}. Define F = F_1 × F_2: R_ε → S^n × S^p by rescaling the first factor [0,ε]^m to [0,1]^m and applying f_1, and rescaling the second factor [0,ε^{-m/p}]^p to [0,1]^p and applying f_2. Thus F_1 has Lipschitz constant at most Lε^{-1}, while F_2 has Lipschitz constant at most Lε^{m/p}. Since the first target factor has dimension n, at most n singular directions can contribute the large factor Lε^{-1}; when k > n, any k-dimensional Jacobian must include at least k-n directions from the second, shrunken factor. The paper obtains the estimate Dil_k(F) < (Lε^{-1})^n(Lε^{m/p})^{k-n} = L^k ε^{-n + (m/p)k - (m/p)n} = L^k ε^{(m/p)(k - n - (n/m)p)}. The assumed inequality makes the exponent of ε positive, so the k-dilation tends to zero as ε → 0. The boundary of R_ε maps into S^n ∨ S^p. Composing with a degree-one smash map S^n × S^p → S^{n+p} that sends S^n ∨ S^p to the basepoint, and extending by the basepoint outside R_ε in S^{m+p}, gives a map S^{m+p} → S^{n+p}. The resulting map represents the p-fold suspension Σ^p a, and the fixed quasi-isometry and smash-map constants do not prevent the k-dilation from tending to zero.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Example after Proposition 1, nontrivial S^4 to S^3 maps with arbitrarily small 3-dilation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The nontrivial element of π_4(S^3), represented by the suspension of the Hopf fibration, can be represented by maps S^4 → S^3 with arbitrarily small 3-dilation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Proposition 1 to the Hopf fibration class in π_3(S^2) with p = 1, m = 3, n = 2, and k = 3. The numerical condition is 3 > 2 + (2/3)·1, which holds. The proposition therefore produces maps in the suspended Hopf class, i.e. the nontrivial element of π_4(S^3), whose 3-dilation tends to zero.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1, infinitely many nontrivial sphere maps with arbitrarily small 3-dilation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every integer N ≥ 2, there are infinitely many integers M such that there exist homotopically nontrivial maps S^M → S^N with arbitrarily small 3-dilation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The topological input used in the paper is as follows. For i = 8j + 1, π_i(SO) is Z_2. Adams proved that the stable J-homomorphism is injective on these groups, producing nontrivial stable homotopy classes. The paper uses results of Curtis, and the formulation in Davis-Mahowald, to choose for each such i a class a_i in π_{i+2}(S^2) whose p-fold suspension is nontrivial for every p. Fix N ≥ 2 and choose i = 8j + 1 with i > 2N - 6. Consider the class Σ^{N-2}a_i in π_{i+N}(S^N), and put M = i + N. This class is nontrivial by the topological input. Apply Proposition 1 with p = N - 2, m = i + 2, n = 2, and k = 3. The condition in Proposition 1 becomes 3 > 2 + (2/(i+2))(N-2), which is exactly i > 2N - 6. Therefore each such nontrivial class has representatives with arbitrarily small 3-dilation, and there are infinitely many allowable i.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Cited theorem of Tsui and Wang, small 2-dilation implies nullhomotopy between spheres
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f: S^m → S^n be a C^1 map between round spheres, with m ≥ 2. If the 2-dilation of f is less than 1, then f is nullhomotopic.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper does not prove the theorem, but summarizes the method of Tsui and Wang. They consider the graph of f as a submanifold of S^m × S^n and evolve it by mean curvature flow. Under the hypothesis that the 2-dilation is less than 1, the flow remains graphical, so at each time it is the graph of a map f_t. The flow converges to the graph of a constant map, and the maps f_t provide a homotopy from f to a constant map.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Cited Gromov small-2-dilation nullhomotopy estimates
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The paper recalls Gromov's earlier lower-bound results for 2-dilation. First, for each pair of dimensions m and n, there is ε(m,n) > 0 such that every C^1 map S^m → S^n with 2-dilation less than ε(m,n) is nullhomotopic. Second, Gromov proves stronger variants: if |Λ^2 df| has sufficiently small L^{m-1+δ} norm, with threshold ε(m,n,δ), then f: S^m → S^n is nullhomotopic; and for any compact simply connected Riemannian manifolds M and N, there is ε(M,N) > 0 such that any map M → N with 2-dilation less than ε(M,N) is nullhomotopic.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper only sketches Gromov's argument for the sphere case. Gromov treats a map S^m → S^n as a family of maps S^2 → S^n. If the 2-dilation is less than ε, each image S^2 has area less than 4πε. By the Riemann mapping theorem, one changes coordinates on the domain spheres so that each map in the family has Dirichlet energy bounded by Cε. The borderline Sobolev inequality then bounds the BMO norm of each map by Cε. From the BMO bound, Gromov constructs a homotopy from the original family to a family of constant maps. The paper cites these as special cases of Gromov's corollary on page 230 of reference [5].

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Elementary degree-volume lower bound for n-dilation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If f: S^n → S^n is a C^1 map that is homotopically nontrivial, then its n-dilation is at least 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If the n-dilation were at most 1 - ε for some ε > 0, then the image of S^n under f would have n-dimensional volume at most (1 - ε)Vol(S^n). Hence f could not be surjective. A non-surjective map into S^n has image contained in S^n minus a point, which is contractible, so f would be nullhomotopic. This contradicts homotopical nontriviality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Gromov Hopf-invariant lower bound for 2n-dilation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f: S^{4n-1} → S^{2n} be a sufficiently smooth map with 2n-dilation D. Then the absolute value of the Hopf invariant of f is bounded above by C(n)D^2, where C(n) depends only on n. Since the Hopf invariant is an integer, any map with nonzero Hopf invariant has 2n-dilation at least C(n)^{-1/2}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose a 2n-form ω on S^{2n} with ∫_{S^{2n}}ω = 1 and pointwise norm bounded by a constant C depending on n. The pullback f^*ω is a closed 2n-form on S^{4n-1}; because H^{2n}(S^{4n-1}) = 0, it is exact. Let P f^*ω be a primitive, so d(P f^*ω) = f^*ω. The Hopf invariant is ∫_{S^{4n-1}} P f^*ω ∧ f^*ω. The pointwise norm |f^*ω| is bounded by C times |Λ^{2n}df|, hence by CD, so the L^2 norm of f^*ω is at most CD after absorbing the fixed volume of the sphere into C. By Hodge theory, choose the primitive P f^*ω perpendicular to all exact (2n-1)-forms. For this choice, a Poincare-type estimate gives ||P f^*ω||_{L^2} ≤ λ^{-1/2}CD, where λ > 0 is the smallest eigenvalue of the Laplacian on exact 2n-forms on S^{4n-1}. Finally, Cauchy-Schwarz bounds |∫ P f^*ω ∧ f^*ω| by the product of the two L^2 norms, giving |Hopf(f)| ≤ C(n)D^2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Formal properties of the filtration V_kπ_m
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a finite simplicial complex W equipped with the piecewise Riemannian metric in which each simplex is equilateral of edge length 1, define V_kπ_m(W) to be the set of homotopy classes in π_m(W) that can be represented by maps S^m → W with k-dilation tending to zero. The paper proves that V_kπ_m(W) is a subgroup of π_m(W), that V_kπ_m(W) ⊂ V_{k+1}π_m(W), and records the filtration 0 = V_1π_m(W) ⊂ V_2π_m(W) ⊂ ... ⊂ V_mπ_m(W) ⊂ V_{m+1}π_m(W) = π_m(W). It also proves naturality: if F: W → V is a continuous pointed map between finite simplicial complexes, then F_* maps V_kπ_m(W) into V_kπ_m(V). For an arbitrary space X, the paper defines a class a in π_m(X) to lie in V_kπ_m(X) if a is the image of an element of V_kπ_m(W) for some finite simplicial complex W and some map W → X; with this definition the filtration is functorial and homotopy invariant.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For additive inverses, if f_i represents a class a with k-dilation tending to zero and I: S^m → S^m is a reflection of degree -1 fixing the basepoint, then f_i ∘ I represents -a and still has k-dilation tending to zero up to a fixed multiplicative constant. For sums, if f_i represents a and g_i represents b with k-dilations tending to zero, take a fixed map I: S^m → S^m ∨ S^m of degree (1,1). Define h_i on the wedge by f_i on the first sphere and g_i on the second. Then h_i ∘ I represents a + b, and its k-dilation tends to zero because I is fixed and h_i has k-dilation controlled by the maximum of the two pieces. The inclusion V_k ⊂ V_{k+1} follows from the singular-value inequality |Λ^{k+1}df| ≤ |Λ^kdf|^{(k+1)/k}. Also, any map from S^m has zero (m+1)-dilation, giving V_{m+1} = π_m. For naturality, approximate F by a PL map with finite Lipschitz constant L; then Dil_k(F ∘ f_i) ≤ L^k Dil_k(f_i), so k-dilation tending to zero is preserved under F_*. The definition for arbitrary spaces is then arranged so that the same naturality statement is automatic, and homotopy invariance follows from functoriality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2, computation of V_4π_7(S^4)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The group V_4π_7(S^4) is exactly the torsion subgroup of π_7(S^4). Since π_7(S^4) is isomorphic to Z ⊕ Z_12, this says V_4π_7(S^4) is the Z_12 summand; in particular it is a proper nonzero subgroup.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let H: π_7(S^4) → Z be the Hopf invariant. The Gromov Hopf-invariant estimate with n = 2 says that any map S^7 → S^4 with nonzero Hopf invariant has 4-dilation bounded below by a positive constant. Therefore any class representable with arbitrarily small 4-dilation must lie in ker H, so V_4π_7(S^4) ⊂ ker H. The paper recalls that ker H is isomorphic to Z_12 and that the suspension map π_6(S^3) → π_7(S^4) is an isomorphism onto ker H, as follows from the long exact sequence of the Hopf fibration S^3 → S^7 → S^4. Hence every element of ker H is a suspension. Applying Proposition 1 with p = 1, m = 6, n = 3, and k = 4 gives arbitrarily small 4-dilation for every such suspension class, because 4 > 3 + (3/6). Thus ker H ⊂ V_4π_7(S^4), proving equality.

proof_techniques.name: Anisotropic rectangular product construction
proof_techniques.description: The paper's main construction maps a rectangular block R_ε = [0,ε]^m × [0,ε^{-m/p}]^p into S^n × S^p. On the short m-dimensional factor, the map is a rescaled representative of a homotopy class and has Lipschitz scale ε^{-1}; on the long p-dimensional factor, it is a rescaled degree-one map to S^p and has Lipschitz scale ε^{m/p}. Because the first target has only n dimensions, a k-dimensional Jacobian with k > n must include some of the shrunken directions from the second factor. The resulting estimate is a competition between n large singular values and k-n small singular values.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is directly relevant to rectangle problems because it shows how anisotropic side lengths and rank limitations can make k-dimensional volume expansion small even when some directions are greatly stretched. For a degree-one 4-rectangle map with 2-dilation control, an analogous singular-value analysis can help identify which rank-collapse patterns must be ruled out by the boundary and degree hypotheses.

proof_techniques.name: Exterior-power singular-value bookkeeping
proof_techniques.description: A recurring estimate in the paper is that |Λ^k df| is the product of the k largest singular values of df. This allows the author to estimate k-dilation by counting how many large singular values can occur and how many small singular values must appear in every k-fold product. The same calculation also yields monotonicity of the small-dilation condition: if k-dilation tends to zero, then all higher dilations tend to zero as well.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For maps between 4-dimensional rectangles, Dil_2(f) ≤ 1 means the product of the two largest singular values is bounded by 1 almost everywhere. This singular-value viewpoint is a natural local input for deriving global constraints involving products of side lengths such as R_3R_4 or total volume.

proof_techniques.name: Boundary collapse and smash product to encode relative topology
proof_techniques.description: In Proposition 1, maps from cubes that send their boundary to a basepoint are used as representatives of maps from spheres. The product map sends the boundary of the product rectangle to the wedge S^n ∨ S^p, and a smash map S^n × S^p → S^{n+p} collapses the wedge to the basepoint. Extending by the basepoint outside the embedded rectangle gives a sphere map in the desired suspended homotopy class.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem also concerns maps of pairs (R,∂R) → (S,∂S), so collapsing boundaries to obtain sphere-level or quotient-level topology is a natural maneuver. The paper's construction indicates how relative boundary conditions can preserve nontrivial topology while the differential is highly anisotropic.

proof_techniques.name: Differential-form lower bounds for homotopy invariants
proof_techniques.description: For the Hopf invariant, the paper presents Gromov's method: pull back a normalized volume form, choose a controlled primitive using Hodge theory, and estimate the defining integral for the invariant by L^2 norms. The k-dilation enters because the pullback of a k-form is bounded pointwise by the k-dilation times the form norm.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A degree-one rectangle map has a cohomological or integral invariant analogous in spirit to the Hopf invariant, although simpler. This suggests trying to pair pulled-back coordinate or volume forms with chains or slices and use Dil_2 bounds plus geometry of R and S to force quantitative lower bounds.

proof_techniques.name: Small 2-dilation as a topological obstruction
proof_techniques.description: The paper recalls two types of 2-dilation lower bounds: Tsui-Wang's sharp theorem that 2-dilation less than 1 for maps S^m → S^n implies nullhomotopy, and Gromov's earlier theorem that sufficiently small 2-dilation, or even sufficiently small L^{m-1+δ} norm of |Λ^2df|, implies nullhomotopy. These results are topological obstructions specifically tied to two-dimensional area contraction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem assumes a 2-dilation bound and a nonzero degree condition. One possible strategy is to normalize, double, or quotient the rectangle map into a sphere or compact-manifold map where a small-2-dilation nullhomotopy theorem would contradict the degree.

proof_techniques.name: Functorial control of k-dilation under Lipschitz postcomposition
proof_techniques.description: The filtration argument uses the simple but important estimate that postcomposition by an L-Lipschitz map multiplies k-dilation by at most L^k. This lets the paper pass from maps into a finite complex to maps into another complex, and shows that the property of having representatives with arbitrarily small k-dilation is stable under fixed Lipschitz operations.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In rectangle comparisons one often rescales source and target rectangles to normalized boxes or composes with coordinate projections and inclusions. This estimate tracks how the Dil_2 bound changes under such normalizations and fixed geometric maps.

proof_techniques.name: Hodge-theoretic primitive estimate
proof_techniques.description: In the Hopf-invariant argument, after writing f^*ω as an exact form, the primitive is not chosen arbitrarily. It is chosen perpendicular to exact forms so that a spectral gap for the Hodge Laplacian gives an L^2 bound on the primitive in terms of the L^2 norm of f^*ω. This converts pointwise k-dilation control into a bound on a global integral invariant.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the rectangle problem is attacked through forms on a doubled or quotient manifold, a similar primitive or Poincare-type estimate may help convert area-dilation bounds into inequalities involving side lengths and volumes.

other_useful_info: The paper points to Gromov's Metric Structures for Riemannian and Non-Riemannian Spaces, pages 358-359, for the Hopf-invariant estimate, and to Gromov's Carnot-Caratheodory spaces as seen from within, especially pages 179 and 220-230, for small-2-dilation and related lower bounds. Section 4 gives additional examples of the filtration: every element of π_m(S^2) lies in V_3 because maps to S^2 have zero 3-dilation; the nontrivial elements of π_4(S^3) and π_5(S^3) lie in V_3; one nontrivial element of π_6(S^3) lies in V_3; the nontrivial elements of π_5(S^4) and π_6(S^4) are shown to lie in V_4; and one nontrivial element of π_7(S^4) lies in V_3. The paper notes that lower bounds for k-dilation with k > 2 are much rarer, and specifically says it does not know a way to lower-bound the 3-dilation of maps representing torsion homotopy classes; for example, the 100-fold suspension of the Hopf map in π_103(S^102) is known from Proposition 1 to have representatives with arbitrarily small 69-dilation, but the paper leaves open whether it can have arbitrarily small 3-dilation.
why_relevant: This paper gives key constructions showing how small k-dilation can coexist with nontrivial topology in some dimensions. Even though it is mainly constructive, it helps identify which features of the degree-one rectangle problem must be used to rule out rank-collapse/Kaufman-type behavior.

title: Area-expanding embeddings of rectangles
overall_summary: The paper proves sharp-up-to-constant necessary and sufficient conditions for the existence of k-expanding embeddings between n-dimensional rectangles. More generally, it proves lower bounds for the k-dilation of any nonzero-degree map of pairs Φ:(U,∂U)->(S,∂S), where U is an open subset of a rectangle R and S is a rectangle. The core proof combines a rectangle-specific relative isoperimetric profile, Almgren-Gromov complexes of cycles, a sweepout/null-homotopy lemma, and a skeleton-by-skeleton tightening construction at an anisotropic scale. For the original problem, the most directly useful results are Estimate 1 and Estimate 2, because they turn a degree-one 2-contracting map between 4-rectangles into product inequalities involving the side lengths; the proof machinery is also useful for deriving variants with mixed products such as S_1 S_2^{1/2} S_3^{3/2} S_4.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Estimate 1 (main k-dilation lower bound)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For each dimension n there is a constant c(n)>0 with the following property. Let R=[0,R_1]×...×[0,R_n] and S=[0,S_1]×...×[0,S_n] be n-dimensional rectangles with R_1≤...≤R_n and S_1≤...≤S_n. Put Q_i=S_i/R_i. Let U be an open subset of R, and let Φ:(U,∂U)->(S,∂S) be a map of pairs of positive degree D>0. If 0≤j<k≤l≤n, then the k-dilation of Φ satisfies dil_k(Φ) ≥ c(n) Q_1...Q_j (Q_{j+1}...Q_l)^((k-j)/(l-j)), with the convention that an empty product is 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by homogeneity reduced to the case dil_k(Φ)≤1, called k-contracting. The case j=0 is proved first by a sweepout argument: subdivide R into blocks [0,R_1]×...×[0,R_k]×[0,ε]^{n-k}; the associated complex of cycles C_Φ has very small p-dimensional chains if R_1...R_k is too small compared with S_1...S_k, and the sweepout lemma then makes C_Φ null-homotopic, contradicting nonzero degree. Proposition 4.2 upgrades this to l≥k and gives the j=0 part. For 0<j<k<l, Section 5 rewrites the desired estimate for a k-contracting map as [(R_1...R_j)/(S_1...S_j)]^((l-k)/(k-j)) R_1...R_l ≥ c(n) S_1...S_l, and reduces to the case where the anisotropic subdivision scale L, defined by R_1...R_j L^{k-j}=δ(n) S_1...S_j S_j^{k-j}, satisfies L≤R_{j+1}. Then R is subdivided into fine rectangular blocks of dimensions R_1×...×R_j×L×...×L. The chain map C_0 associated to Φ is tightened to a new chain map C_1: C_1 agrees with C_0 on the k-skeleton, while each higher-dimensional face is replaced by a filling of its already-defined boundary using the relative isoperimetric inequality for S. This gives |C_1(F^p)|≤Cδ S_1...S_j S_j^{p-j} for every fine p-face. The key lemma proves that C_1 has the same degree as C_0, hence D. Next one glues fine faces into a coarser decomposition with blocks R_1×...×R_l×L×...×L. For a p-face F^p of this coarse complex, the glued complex C_1^+ obeys the volume bound |C_1^+(F^p)|≤C(n,δ)[(R_1...R_j)/(S_1...S_j)]^((l-k)/(k-j)) R_1...R_l S_j^{p-l}. Since C_1^+ has nonzero degree, the sweepout lemma implies that some p-face has volume at least c(n)S_1...S_p. Comparing the two bounds and using S_j≤S_i for i≥l gives the desired inequality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Estimate 2 (degree-refined k-dilation lower bound)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: With R, S, Q_i, U, and Φ as in Estimate 1, for every integer j with 0≤j<k, the k-dilation satisfies dil_k(Φ) ≥ c(n) D^((k-j)/(n-j)) Q_1...Q_j (Q_{j+1}...Q_n)^((k-j)/(n-j)), where D>0 is the degree of Φ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Again one reduces to k-contracting maps. For j=0, the n-dilation of a degree D map is at least D Vol(S)/Vol(R)=D Q_1...Q_n, and the linear-algebra monotonicity dil_n^{k/n}≤dil_k gives the bound. For 0<j<k, the same anisotropic subdivision and tightening construction used for Estimate 1 is applied with l=n. After gluing, the coarser complex has only the full n-dimensional rectangle R as its top-dimensional face. The tightened and glued n-chain C_1^+(R) represents degree D in H_n(S,∂S), so its volume is at least D S_1...S_n. On the other hand the tightening/gluing estimate gives |C_1^+(R)|≤C(n,δ)[(R_1...R_j)/(S_1...S_j)]^((n-k)/(k-j)) R_1...R_n. Comparing these two inequalities yields [(R_1...R_j)/(S_1...S_j)]^((n-k)/(k-j)) R_1...R_n ≥ c(n)D S_1...S_n, which is equivalent to the stated dilation lower bound after undoing the normalization.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1 (necessary conditions for k-expanding embeddings of rectangles)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For each dimension n there is a constant c(n)>0 such that the following holds. Let R and S be n-dimensional rectangles with ordered side lengths R_1≤...≤R_n and S_1≤...≤S_n. If there is a k-expanding embedding I:S->R, meaning that every k-dimensional surface in S has image volume at least its original volume, then for all integers j,l with 0≤j<k≤l≤n one has (R_1...R_j)^((l-j)/(k-j)) R_{j+1}...R_l ≥ c(n) (S_1...S_j)^((l-j)/(k-j)) S_{j+1}...S_l.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This theorem is obtained from Estimate 1 by taking U=I(S)⊂R and Φ=I^{-1}:U->S. Since I is k-expanding, its inverse Φ is k-contracting, so dil_k(Φ)≤1. The map Φ has degree 1 as a map of pairs. Substituting Q_i=S_i/R_i into Estimate 1 and rearranging gives exactly the displayed product inequality. The cases j=0 are the sweepout/cross-sectional volume inequalities, while j>0 are obtained by the tightening construction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3 (isoperimetric profile of a rectangle)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R=[0,R_1]×...×[0,R_n] be an n-dimensional rectangle with R_1≤...≤R_n. For a q-dimensional relative integral cycle z in R, define its filling volume to be the least volume of a relative integral (q+1)-chain y in R with ∂y=z, and let I_R^q(V) be the largest filling volume among q-dimensional relative integral cycles in R of volume at most V. For 1≤q≤n-1 there are constants c(n)>0 and C(n)<∞ such that if V≤c(n)R_1...R_q and V is written uniquely in the form V=c(n) R_1...R_j ρ^{q-j} for some 0≤j≤q-1 and some ρ in the appropriate interval R_j≤ρ≤R_{j+1} (with empty product convention and the lower endpoint vacuous when j=0), then I_R^q(V)≤C(n) R_1...R_j ρ^{q-j+1}. In all cases, I_R^q(V)≤C(n) R_{q+1} V. The proof also establishes the base case that a relative 0-cycle of volume V can be filled with length at most R_1 V.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof uses the Federer-Fleming deformation theorem and induction on q. For q=0, each point p=(p_1,...,p_n) is joined to the face x_1=0 by the segment [0,p_1]×{p_2}×...×{p_n}, giving filling length at most R_1 times the total multiplicity. For q≥1, let z be a q-cycle of volume V. If V≤cR_1^q, choose a rectangular lattice of side length L≈V^{1/q}≤R_1, with ∂R in its (n-1)-skeleton. The deformation lemma moves z to a homologous q-cycle z' in the q-skeleton with |z'|≤CV and filling cost ≤CLV. With constants chosen so that |z'| is less than the volume of one q-cell, integrality forces z'=0, so z is filled with volume O(V^{(q+1)/q}). If V≥cR_1^q, use a lattice of scale L=R_1 arranged so every lattice vertex has first coordinate 0 or R_1. The deformation lemma moves z to a cycle z' in the q-skeleton with filling cost ≤CR_1V. Because every interior q-face contains the full [0,R_1] factor, z' has the form [0,R_1]×z_1, where z_1 is a relative (q-1)-cycle in [0,R_2]×...×[0,R_n] with volume ≤CV/R_1. The induction hypothesis fills z_1 in the lower-dimensional rectangle, and multiplying the filling by [0,R_1] fills z'. Substituting V=cR_1...R_jρ^{q-j} gives the small-volume bound C R_1...R_jρ^{q-j+1}; the universal bound C R_{q+1}V follows similarly, with the deformation cost absorbed since R_1≤R_{q+1}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1.1 (relative Federer-Fleming deformation on a rectangular lattice)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let z be a relative k-cycle in a rectangle R. Suppose R is equipped with a rectangular lattice whose side lengths are all comparable to L, up to a factor of 2, with L≤R_1, and suppose ∂R lies in the (n-1)-skeleton of the lattice. Then there is a relative k-cycle z' contained in the k-skeleton of the lattice such that |z'|≤C(n)|z| and the relative cycle z'-z has filling volume at most C(n)L|z|.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The Federer-Fleming deformation is performed one skeleton at a time. Starting with z=z_n, construct cycles z_p lying in the union of the p-skeleton and ∂R, ending at z_k=z'. For each interior p-face F, choose a point x∈F and radially project F\{x} to ∂F while fixing ∂F. Averaging over x shows that one can choose x so that the projection stretches p-dimensional volume by at most C(n); the homology swept out has volume at most C(n)L times the mass being projected. Repeating over all p-faces and descending from p=n to p=k gives the stated mass and filling-volume bounds.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Useful special case after Theorem 3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R=[0,R_1]×...×[0,R_n] be an ordered rectangle. If z is a relative p-cycle in R with volume V≤c(n) R_1...R_j R_j^{p-j} for some 1≤j≤p, then z bounds a relative (p+1)-chain y in R with |y|≤C(n) R_j V.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Theorem 3 to z. In the notation V≈R_1...R_iρ^{p-i} determined by the theorem, the hypothesis forces the relevant scale ρ to be at most a constant multiple of R_j. The theorem gives filling volume at most C R_1...R_iρ^{p-i+1}=CρV, hence at most C R_j V. This is the form used repeatedly in the tightening construction, where boundaries of cells are small enough to be filled at the scale S_j in the target rectangle S.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.1 (sweepout/null-homotopy lemma for complexes of cycles)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: There is a constant c(n)>0 such that the following holds. Let S=[0,S_1]×...×[0,S_n] be an ordered n-rectangle. Let C_0 be a complex of relative integral cycles in S parametrized by a polyhedral complex X; that is, C_0 is a chain map from the cellular chain complex of X to the complex of relative integral Lipschitz chains in S. Suppose C_0(v)=0 for every vertex v of X, and suppose that for every p-dimensional face F^p of X with 1≤p≤n, the chain C_0(F^p) has volume at most c(n)S_1...S_p. Then C_0 is null-homotopic as a complex of cycles.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let C_1 be the zero complex and build a homotopy C on X×[0,1] from C_0 to C_1 skeleton by skeleton. On vertical edges v×[0,1], set C=0, which is compatible with the vertex hypothesis. Assume C has been extended up to dimension p-1 while maintaining small-volume bounds. For a p-face, the already-defined boundary is a relative (p-1)-cycle in S of volume at most a small multiple of S_1...S_{p-1}; Theorem 3 fills it with a p-chain of volume at most cS_1...S_p after choosing c(n) sufficiently small. This extends through the n-skeleton. For an (n+1)-face, the boundary is a relative n-cycle of volume less than S_1...S_n, hence represents zero in H_n(S,∂S) because any nonzero multiple of the fundamental class has mass at least Vol(S). Higher-dimensional extensions have no obstruction since H_p(S,∂S)=0 for p≥n+1. Thus the original complex is homotopic to zero.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.1 (basic product lower bound)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R and S be ordered n-rectangles, let Q_i=S_i/R_i, let U⊂R be open, and let Φ:(U,∂U)->(S,∂S) be a map of nonzero degree D. Then dil_k(Φ)≥c(n) Q_1...Q_k.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By scaling, assume dil_k(Φ)≤1 and suppose for contradiction that R_1...R_k<cS_1...S_k. Subdivide R into blocks congruent to [0,R_1]×...×[0,R_k]×[0,ε]^{n-k}, using a fine grid only in the last n-k directions. Let C_Φ be the associated complex of cycles, C_Φ(F)=Φ(F∩U). Any face of dimension p<k lies in ∂R and maps to zero. Each k-face has volume at most R_1...R_k, so its image has volume less than cS_1...S_k. For p>k, the linear algebra corollary implies Φ is also p-contracting, so the image of a p-face has volume at most R_1...R_k ε^{p-k}; choosing ε sufficiently small makes these volumes less than cS_1...S_p. Lemma 4.1 then makes C_Φ null-homotopic, hence degree zero, contradicting the hypothesis.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.2 (j=0 estimates and degree-volume bound)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let l≥k. If U⊂R and Φ:(U,∂U)->(S,∂S) has nonzero degree D, then dil_k(Φ)≥c(n)(Q_1...Q_l)^{k/l}. Also dil_k(Φ)≥(|D|Q_1...Q_n)^{k/n}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Proposition 4.1 applied with k replaced by l, the l-dilation is at least c(n)Q_1...Q_l. Lemma 8.1 gives dil_l(Φ)^{k/l}≤dil_k(Φ), proving the first inequality. For the second, the n-dilation must be at least |D|Vol(S)/Vol(R)=|D|Q_1...Q_n, since the image n-volume counted with multiplicity has degree |D|. Applying the same linear-algebra monotonicity from n to k gives dil_k(Φ)≥(|D|Q_1...Q_n)^{k/n}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 5 algebraic reduction and choice of tightening scale L
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the nontrivial cases of Estimates 1 and 2, one may assume Φ is k-contracting, 0<j<k<l, and it suffices to prove [(R_1...R_j)/(S_1...S_j)]^((l-k)/(k-j)) R_1...R_l ≥ c(n)S_1...S_l and [(R_1...R_j)/(S_1...S_j)]^((n-k)/(k-j)) R_1...R_n ≥ c(n)|D|S_1...S_n. For fixed j define L by R_1...R_j L^{k-j}=δ(n)S_1...S_j S_j^{k-j}, where δ(n)>0 is small. It is enough to prove the estimates under the additional assumption L≤R_{j+1}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The inequalities can be rewritten as [(R_1...R_j)/(S_1...S_j)]^{1/(k-j)} ≥ c(n)[(S_1...S_l)/(R_1...R_l)]^{1/(l-k)} and [(R_1...R_j)/(S_1...S_j)]^{1/(k-j)} ≥ c(n)[|D|(S_1...S_n)/(R_1...R_n)]^{1/(n-k)}. The right-hand sides do not depend on j, so one chooses j, among 1≤j≤k-1, minimizing the left-hand side. If j=k-1, then L=δ S_1...S_{k-1}S_{k-1}/(R_1...R_{k-1})≤δ S_1...S_k/(R_1...R_{k-1}); Proposition 4.1 gives R_1...R_k≥cS_1...S_k, so choosing δ small yields L≤R_k=R_{j+1}. If j<k-1, minimality compared with j+1 gives [(R_1...R_j)/(S_1...S_j)]^{1/(k-j)}≤[(R_1...R_{j+1})/(S_1...S_{j+1})]^{1/(k-j-1)}. Raising to the product of exponents and rearranging gives S_1...S_j S_{j+1}^{k-j}≤R_1...R_j R_{j+1}^{k-j}. Since S_j≤S_{j+1} and R_1...R_jL^{k-j}=δS_1...S_jS_j^{k-j}, it follows that L≤R_{j+1} after absorbing δ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 6 tightening construction and volume estimate (V)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume Φ:(U,∂U)->(S,∂S) is k-contracting, 0<j<k<l≤n, and the scale L defined by R_1...R_jL^{k-j}=δS_1...S_jS_j^{k-j} satisfies L≤R_{j+1}. Subdivide R into fine blocks of dimensions R_1×...×R_j×L×...×L and construct the tightened complex C_1 by filling higher-dimensional boundaries in S. If the fine complex is glued into a coarser complex B^+ whose blocks have dimensions R_1×...×R_l×L×...×L, then for every p-face F^p of B^+ with p≥l the glued chain satisfies |C_1^+(F^p)|≤C(n,δ)[(R_1...R_j)/(S_1...S_j)]^((l-k)/(k-j)) R_1...R_l S_j^{p-l}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The fine k-faces have volume at most R_1...R_jL^{k-j}=δS_1...S_jS_j^{k-j}; because Φ is k-contracting, their images have this same upper bound. Define C_1=C_0 on the k-skeleton. For a (k+1)-face, C_1 of its boundary has volume O(δS_1...S_jS_j^{k-j}); by the special isoperimetric bound in S it fills with volume O(δS_1...S_jS_j^{k+1-j}). Inductively, if all p-faces have volume O(δS_1...S_jS_j^{p-j}), then the boundary of a (p+1)-face has the same order of volume and fills with volume O(δS_1...S_jS_j^{p+1-j}). Thus every fine p-face obeys |C_1(F^p)|≤CδS_1...S_jS_j^{p-j}. A coarse p-face is a union of N=(R_{j+1}/L)...(R_l/L) fine p-faces, so the glued mass is at most CδN S_1...S_jS_j^{p-j}. Substituting L^{k-j}=δS_1...S_jS_j^{k-j}/(R_1...R_j) and simplifying gives the displayed bound, with powers of δ absorbed into C(n,δ).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.1 (homotopy between small complexes agreeing on a skeleton)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: There is ε(n)>0 such that the following holds. Let C_0 and C_1 be chain maps from the cellular chain complex of a polyhedral complex X to the relative integral Lipschitz chains in an ordered n-rectangle S. Suppose C_0 and C_1 agree on the k-skeleton of X. Suppose further that for every p-face F^p of X with k+1≤p≤n, both |C_0(F^p)| and |C_1(F^p)| are at most ε(n)S_1...S_p. Then C_0 and C_1 are homotopic as complexes of cycles.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Construct a homotopy C on X×[0,1]. For p≤k set C(F^p×[0,1])=0, using the fact that C_0 and C_1 agree on those faces. Inductively, suppose C has been constructed on lower-dimensional faces with bounds |C(F^p×[0,1])|≤cS_1...S_{p+1}. For a p-face F^p with p≥k+1, the boundary to be filled is C((∂F^p)×[0,1])+C_1(F^p)-C_0(F^p), a p-cycle whose volume is at most a small multiple of S_1...S_p. Theorem 3 fills it by a (p+1)-chain of volume at most cS_1...S_{p+1}. This extends to the n-skeleton. For n-faces, the boundary n-cycle has volume less than Vol(S) and is therefore null-homologous; higher extensions have no obstruction because H_q(S,∂S)=0 for q≥n+1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Key Lemma in Section 6 (tightening preserves degree)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the Section 6 setup, let C_0 be the complex of cycles associated to Φ on the fine subdivision B of R, so C_0(F)=Φ(F∩U). Let C_1 be the tightened complex that agrees with C_0 on faces of dimension at most k and whose higher-dimensional faces are chosen by the isoperimetric filling procedure described above. Then degree(C_1)=degree(Φ)=degree(C_0).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The issue is that C_1 is obtained by noncanonical fillings and need not visibly be homotopic to C_0. Guth proves degree preservation by gradual tightening. Let B_s be the finer subdivision with block dimensions R_1×...×R_j×2^{-s}L×...×2^{-s}L. Define Γ_s:B_s->I_rel(S) by setting Γ_s(F)=Φ(F∩U) for dim F≤k and filling all higher faces by the same isoperimetric tightening rule. Thus Γ_0=C_1. Let Γ_{s+1}^+ be Γ_{s+1} glued back to the coarser subdivision B_s. The complexes Γ_s and Γ_{s+1}^+ agree on the k-skeleton, and both have sufficiently small higher-dimensional face volumes of order δS_1...S_jS_j^{p-j}≤δS_1...S_p; Lemma 6.1 gives a homotopy, so their degrees are equal. Since gluing does not change degree, degree Γ_s is independent of s. For large s, define β_s(F)=Φ(F∩U) on every face of B_s. Because p-faces with p≥k+1 have volumes R_1...R_jL^{p-j}2^{-(p-j)s}, choosing s large makes all β_s(F) small enough for Lemma 6.1. β_s and Γ_s agree on the k-skeleton and have small higher faces, so they are homotopic. But β_s is the original map-induced complex and has degree degree(Φ). Therefore Γ_0=C_1 has the same degree.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 8.1 and Corollary 8.1 (higher-dimensional dilation controlled by k-dilation)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a linear map between Euclidean spaces and let |Λ^mL| denote its m-dimensional dilation, the operator norm on m-vectors. If l>k, then |Λ^lL|^{k/l}≤|Λ^kL|. Consequently, if Φ is a smooth map with k-dilation D(k) and l-dilation D(l), then for l≥k one has D(l)^{k/l}≤D(k). In particular, a k-contracting map is also l-contracting for every l≥k.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write the singular values of L in nondecreasing order 0≤s_1≤...≤s_m, where m is the smaller dimension. Then |Λ^rL| is the product of the r largest singular values, s_{m-r+1}...s_m. The geometric mean of the l largest singular values is at most the geometric mean of the k largest singular values, which is exactly |Λ^lL|^{1/l}≤|Λ^kL|^{1/k}. Raising to the k-th power gives the lemma. Taking the supremum over points for dΦ gives the corollary.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 8.2 (linear expansion/contraction interpolation inequality)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If j≤k≤l and L is a linear map between Euclidean spaces, then |Λ^jL|^((l-k)/(l-j)) |Λ^lL|^((k-j)/(l-j)) ≤ |Λ^kL|.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let s_1≤...≤s_m be the singular values. Then |Λ^rL| is the product of the r largest singular values. Raising the desired inequality to the power l-j, the left-hand side becomes (s_{m-j+1}...s_m)^{l-k}(s_{m-l+1}...s_m)^{k-j}. Reorganizing separates the largest j singular values and the intermediate singular values. Since the singular values appearing among the largest k dominate those appearing farther down among the largest l, the product is bounded by (s_{m-k+1}...s_m)^{l-j}=|Λ^kL|^{l-j}. Taking (l-j)-th roots gives the result. Guth notes that this has no direct pointwise-to-global analogue for nonlinear maps, and the paper's tightening construction serves as a nonlinear substitute at the level of complexes of cycles.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2 (sufficient conditions for k-expanding embeddings; sharpness up to constants)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For each dimension n there is a constant C(n)>0 such that the following holds. Let R and S be ordered n-rectangles. If for every 0≤j<k≤l≤n one has (R_1...R_j)^((l-j)/(k-j)) R_{j+1}...R_l ≥ C(n)(S_1...S_j)^((l-j)/(k-j)) S_{j+1}...S_l, then there exists a k-expanding embedding from S into R.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The construction composes a linear k-expanding part with foldings. First, a folding lemma says that if all partial products R_1...R_p are sufficiently larger than S_1...S_p, then there is a 1-expanding embedding S->R; this is built by repeatedly using two-dimensional foldings in coordinate pairs, where a long thin rectangle is folded into a shorter wider one without contracting lengths. Second, an algebraic lemma says that the displayed inequalities imply the existence of a k-contracting linear diffeomorphism from R to a rectangle T such that T_1...T_p≥S_1...S_p for every p. The proof of the algebraic lemma iteratively modifies R by linear maps that enlarge the first q axes by λ_q and shrink the remaining axes by λ_q^{-q/(k-q)}, preserving k-contraction, until the first deficient partial product is corrected; then it uses induction on the remaining dimensions. Taking the inverse of the k-contracting linear map gives a k-expanding linear map T->R, and composing S->T with T->R gives the desired k-expanding embedding.

proof_techniques.name: Normalize by scaling the target metric
proof_techniques.description: The k-dilation estimates are homogeneous. In proofs Guth often assumes the map is k-contracting, dil_k≤1, and proves a side-length inequality; the general lower bound for dil_k follows by rescaling the target rectangle so that the normalized map becomes k-contracting and then undoing the scaling.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a hypothesis Dil_2(f)≤1 no rescaling is needed, so the paper's k-contracting inequalities can be applied directly with k=2. If one derives an intermediate estimate with another dilation normalization, scaling keeps track of the correct powers of side lengths.

proof_techniques.name: Rectangle-specific relative isoperimetric profile
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 3 estimates fillings of relative integral cycles in a rectangle in terms of the side lengths. The proof uses Federer-Fleming deformation at the smallest relevant scale and induction after splitting off the shortest interval factor. The profile reflects anisotropy: a cycle of size about R_1...R_jρ^{p-j} fills with size about R_1...R_jρ^{p-j+1}, and large cycles have a linear filling bound C R_{p+1}V.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The target in the original problem is a rectangle S, so this isoperimetric profile can be used to replace pieces of f-slices by cheaper fillings whose cost depends on S_1,S_2,S_3,S_4. The mixed exponents in the desired inequality are likely to come from choosing the filling scale between two target side lengths.

proof_techniques.name: Complexes of cycles as discrete sweepouts
proof_techniques.description: A complex of cycles is a chain map from the face complex of a parameter polyhedron X to the relative integral chains of S. It records, for each p-face of X, a p-chain in S, with boundaries matching. A map of pairs Φ:(U,∂U)->(S,∂S) and a polyhedral decomposition of R naturally produce such a complex by sending each face F to Φ(F∩U). The complex has a well-defined degree via the induced map on H_n.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Instead of working with a continuous family of preimage or image slices, one can decompose the domain rectangle and use the resulting chain map. This is robust under piecewise smooth maps and under noncontinuous choices of fillings during tightening.

proof_techniques.name: Small-volume sweepout null-homotopy
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 4.1 says that if every p-face in a complex of cycles has volume below cS_1...S_p, then the complex is null-homotopic. The proof constructs a homotopy to zero by filling boundaries skeleton-by-skeleton using the rectangle isoperimetric inequality; the only top-dimensional obstruction is avoided because an n-cycle of volume less than Vol(S) cannot represent the fundamental class.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A degree-one map gives a non-null complex. Therefore, after any tightening procedure that preserves degree, some face must still have volume at least cS_1...S_p. Comparing that forced lower bound with an upper bound from Dil_2≤1 is the main route to side-length inequalities.

proof_techniques.name: Anisotropic subdivision at a critical scale L
proof_techniques.description: For 0<j<k, Guth subdivides the domain rectangle into blocks R_1×...×R_j×L×...×L, where L is chosen by R_1...R_jL^{k-j}=δS_1...S_jS_j^{k-j}. This makes the k-dimensional faces small enough in the target to start the isoperimetric tightening process at the scale S_j. Section 5 gives algebraic conditions ensuring L≤R_{j+1}, so this subdivision fits the ordering of the domain side lengths.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For n=4 and k=2, the nontrivial choice is j=1, so one should examine blocks with one short R_1 direction and one adjustable L direction. This is the mechanism in the paper that produces inequalities involving powers of R_1/S_1.

proof_techniques.name: Skeleton-by-skeleton tightening
proof_techniques.description: After subdividing, C_1 is defined to equal the original map-induced complex on the k-skeleton. For each (k+1)-face, the already-defined boundary is a small k-cycle in S and is replaced by a filling from the rectangle isoperimetric inequality. Then the same step is repeated for (k+2)-faces and so on. The result is a new complex whose p-face volumes are bounded by CδS_1...S_jS_j^{p-j}, often much smaller than the original images of p-faces.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This can bypass the obstacle that Dil_2 controls only 2-dimensional areas directly: by tightening, one manufactures controlled 3- and 4-dimensional chains from the controlled 2-skeleton.

proof_techniques.name: Gradual tightening to preserve degree
proof_techniques.description: Because the chosen fillings in C_1 are not canonically related to the original chains, degree preservation is nontrivial. Guth introduces finer subdivisions B_s with scale 2^{-s}L and constructs tightened complexes Γ_s. Consecutive Γ_s are compared after gluing by a homotopy lemma for small complexes, and for very fine s the original map-induced complex β_s has small higher-dimensional faces and is homotopic to Γ_s. This proves the tightened complex has the same degree as the original map.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Any proof that replaces f-images of cells by cheaper fillings must include a degree-preservation argument. The gradual-tightening method is a ready-made way to justify that the resulting tightened sweepout still detects degree one.

proof_techniques.name: Gluing fine cells into coarser l-dimensional blocks
proof_techniques.description: After tightening on the fine subdivision, Guth regroups fine faces into coarser blocks R_1×...×R_l×L×...×L. A coarse p-face contains N=(R_{j+1}/L)...(R_l/L) fine p-faces, so its mass is bounded by N times the fine mass. Substituting the defining equation for L yields the key upper bound with factor [(R_1...R_j)/(S_1...S_j)]^((l-k)/(k-j)) R_1...R_l.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Different choices of l produce different product inequalities. In dimension 4 with k=2, gluing at l=3 and l=4 gives the paper's main anisotropic constraints, which can be combined with side ordering and additional hypotheses.

proof_techniques.name: Linear-algebra dilation monotonicity and interpolation
proof_techniques.description: The singular-value identities show that if a map is k-contracting then it is l-contracting for every l≥k, and more generally for linear maps |Λ^jL|^((l-k)/(l-j))|Λ^lL|^((k-j)/(l-j))≤|Λ^kL|. The nonlinear theorem is presented as an analogue of this expansion/contraction tradeoff after replacing the map by a tightened complex.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Dil_2≤1 automatically controls 3- and 4-dimensional volumes of individual smooth images, which is useful for the j=0 estimates. The interpolation inequality also explains why exponents such as 1/2 and 3/2 may arise when combining 2-dimensional control with lower-dimensional squeezing.

proof_techniques.name: Degree as a top-dimensional mass lower bound
proof_techniques.description: If a relative n-chain in S represents degree D times the fundamental class, then its mass is at least |D|Vol(S). This simple observation is used in Estimate 2 after tightening and gluing all the way to l=n.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a degree-one map between 4-rectangles, any tightened 4-chain still representing the fundamental class must have mass at least S_1S_2S_3S_4. This supplies a hard lower bound to compare against tightened upper bounds.

other_useful_info: The paper gives model examples showing Theorem 3 is close to sharp: cycles [0,R_1]×...×[0,R_j]×S^{q-j}(ρ) have volume about R_1...R_jρ^{q-j} and filling volume about R_1...R_jρ^{q-j+1}, while M copies of [0,R_1]×...×[0,R_q]×{p} have filling volume about R_{q+1} times their volume. Guth notes that the arguments depend mainly on knowing the target's relative isoperimetric profile, so analogous estimates can be attempted for other targets with known profiles. Appendix 2 states product-type generalizations and ellipsoid corollaries, while the main references for the sweepout technology are Almgren's complexes of cycles, Gromov's filling/sweepout estimates, and Federer-Fleming deformation theory.
why_relevant: Although stated for k-expanding embeddings, this paper contains Guth's rectangle-specific isoperimetric profile estimates, complexes of cycles, and tightening construction. These are exactly the tools needed to prove anisotropic inequalities involving products of side lengths such as S_1 S_2^{1/2} S_3^{3/2} S_4.

title: Directional Isoperimetric Inequalities and Rational Homotopy Invariants
overall_summary: The paper proves sharp, anisotropic estimates for a second-order linking invariant of maps from an n-dimensional ellipsoid to a wedge of three spheres, using direction-dependent isoperimetric inequalities and a direction-dependent coarea inequality. Its central technical contribution is a filling theorem that controls each coordinate-projection volume of a filling chain separately, first for absolute and relative cycles in rectangles and then for cycles in ellipsoids via a double-of-a-rectangle model. These estimates yield Theorem 1, an upper bound for the second-order linking invariant under a k1-dilation hypothesis, and Theorem 3, a lower bound for the k-dilation of degree-D maps between ellipsoids in terms of axis ratios. For the original rectangle problem, the most useful ingredients are the directional filling inequalities in rectangles, the directional coarea inequality, and the strategy of composing a degree-one map with a carefully constructed test map to a wedge of spheres and comparing a homotopy-invariant lower bound with a dilation-dependent upper bound.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Directional volume definition and comparison estimate
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let C be an integral Lipschitz m-chain in R^n. For an m-tuple J of distinct elements of {1,...,n}, let P(J) be the corresponding coordinate m-plane. The J-volume Vol_J(C) is the m-dimensional volume of the projection of C to P(J), counted with geometric multiplicity; equivalently Vol_J(C) = ∫_C |T C_x · P(J)| dvol(x), where T C_x is the oriented unit simple m-vector tangent to C at almost every point and P(J) is the oriented unit coordinate m-vector. Then Vol(C) ≤ Σ_J Vol_J(C) ≤ binom(n,m) Vol(C), where the sum ranges over all coordinate m-tuples J.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: At almost every point of C, expand the unit simple m-vector T C_x in the orthonormal basis of coordinate m-vectors. The squared coefficients sum to 1. Therefore the sum of the absolute values of the coefficients is at least 1 and at most the number binom(n,m) of coefficients. Integrating this pointwise inequality over C gives the two-sided comparison between total m-volume and the sum of directional m-volumes.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1 (coarea inequality, as stated in the introduction)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F : (M^n,g) → (N^q,h) be a C∞ map between Riemannian manifolds with Lipschitz constant L. Then F has a regular fiber whose (n−q)-dimensional volume is at most L^q Vol(M,g) / Vol(N,h).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply the coarea formula: the integral over N of the volumes of the fibers F^{-1}(y) equals the integral over M of the q-dimensional Jacobian of dF. Since F is L-Lipschitz, the q-Jacobian is bounded by L^q at every point. Hence the average fiber volume over N is at most L^q Vol(M)/Vol(N), so some regular fiber satisfies the same bound.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Loomis-Whitney directional estimate (quoted background)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If U is an open subset of R^n and, for each coordinate (n−1)-tuple J, A_J denotes the (n−1)-volume of the projection of U onto the coordinate hyperplane P(J), then Vol(U) ≤ (Π_J A_J)^{1/(n−1)}, where the product is over the n coordinate hyperplanes. As an immediate corollary, if H is a closed embedded hypersurface in R^n enclosing a region of volume V, then V ≤ (Π_J Vol_J(H))^{1/(n−1)}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes the Loomis-Whitney theorem rather than proving it. The hypersurface corollary follows by observing that the projection volume of the enclosed region onto a coordinate hyperplane is bounded by the corresponding directional volume of its boundary, up to the convention used in the paper for geometric multiplicity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Federer-Fleming isoperimetric inequality (quoted background)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If z is a closed k-dimensional integral cycle in R^n, then there is a (k+1)-dimensional integral chain y in R^n with ∂y = z and |y| ≤ C(n) |z|^{(k+1)/k}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this classical theorem as the non-directional model for filling cycles of arbitrary codimension. No proof is included in the paper; it is used as background motivation for the directional filling estimates developed later.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2 / Isoperimetric inequality in an ellipse (quoted from Guth [2])
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let E be an n-dimensional ellipse with principal axes E_0 ≤ E_1 ≤ ... ≤ E_n. If z is an integral m-cycle in E, then z bounds an integral (m+1)-chain y in E satisfying Vol(y) ≤ C(n) [E_{m+1} + E_{n−m}] Vol(z).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this result from Guth [2] and does not reprove it at this point. Later, after proving the directional isoperimetric inequality in an ellipse, the paper notes that summing the directional estimates recovers this ordinary volume bound because for an (m+1)-tuple I, its smallest element is at most n−m and the smallest missing element e satisfies e−1 ≤ m+1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.1 (definition, well-definedness, and homotopy invariance of L2)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume 2 ≤ k_1 ≤ k_2 ≤ k_3 and n+2 = k_1+k_2+k_3. For a smooth map F : S^n → S^{k_1} ∨ S^{k_2} ∨ S^{k_3} and regular non-basepoint values q_i ∈ S^{k_i}, define z_1 = F^{-1}(q_1), choose an integral chain y_1 with ∂y_1 = z_1, define z_2 = y_1 ∩ F^{-1}(q_2) after putting y_1 in general position, choose y_2 with ∂y_2 = z_2, and let π be the retraction of the wedge to S^{k_3}. Since π∘F maps ∂y_2 to the basepoint, it has a well-defined degree on y_2; this integer is L2(F). For arbitrary continuous F, define L2(F) by first homotoping F to a smooth map for which the chosen q_i are regular. Proposition 1.1 states that L2(F) is independent of y_1, y_2, the smoothing/homotopy used to define it, and the choices of q_i; moreover L2 is a homotopy invariant.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Independence of y_2: if y_2 and y_2' both fill z_2, their difference is a k_3-cycle in S^n; it is exact, so the degree of π∘F on that difference is zero. Independence of y_1: if y_1−y_1' bounds A, then B = A ∩ F^{-1}(q_2) has boundary z_2−z_2'. Choosing y_2' = y_2−B gives fillings for the two choices, and the degree difference is the degree of π∘F on B; but B lies in F^{-1}(q_2), and q_2 lies in the S^{k_2} summand, so π∘F(B) is the basepoint and contributes zero. Homotopy invariance: for a homotopy F : S^n×[0,1] → wedge, use the fiber F^{-1}(q_1) as a homology between the endpoint fibers, fill the resulting cycle by a chain y_1 in S^n×[0,1], intersect y_1 with F^{-1}(q_2), fill again by y_2, and observe that π∘F(y_2) gives a homology between the endpoint k_3-cycles in S^{k_3}; hence their degrees agree. Independence of q_i follows by using a diffeomorphism of the wedge, homotopic to the identity, carrying one set of non-basepoint choices to another.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Unlabelled product calculation for the second-order linking invariant
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f : S^n → S^{k_1} ∨ S^{n−k_1+1} and g : S^{n−k_1+1} → S^{k_2} ∨ S^{k_3} be continuous maps, with n+2 = k_1+k_2+k_3 and 2 ≤ k_1 ≤ k_2 ≤ k_3. Let g_+ : S^{k_1} ∨ S^{n−k_1+1} → S^{k_1} ∨ S^{k_2} ∨ S^{k_3} be the map equal to the identity on S^{k_1} and equal to g on S^{n−k_1+1}. Then L2(g_+∘f) = L(f) L(g), where L denotes the ordinary first-order linking invariant.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let F = g_+∘f. The first fiber z_1 = F^{-1}(q_1) is f^{-1}(q_1), and choose y_1 with ∂y_1 = z_1. Let w = g^{-1}(q_2) in S^{n−k_1+1} and choose v with ∂v = w. Then z_2 = y_1 ∩ f^{-1}(w), and a convenient filling is y_2 = y_1 ∩ f^{-1}(v). The final zero-cycle is z_3 = y_2 ∩ F^{-1}(q_3) = y_1 ∩ f^{-1}(v ∩ g^{-1}(q_3)). The intersection v ∩ g^{-1}(q_3) is a signed set of L(g) points, and for each such point p, the signed count y_1 ∩ f^{-1}(p) is L(f). Thus the total signed count is L(f)L(g).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.2 (bounding L2 by isoperimetric constants and k1-dilation)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume 2 ≤ k_1 ≤ k_2 ≤ k_3 and n = k_1+k_2+k_3−2. Let F : (S^n,g) → (S^{k_1} ∨ S^{k_2} ∨ S^{k_3}, h_1∨h_2∨h_3) be a map whose k_1-dilation is at most L^{k_1}. Let Iso_j(g) denote the least constant I such that every integral j-cycle in (S^n,g) bounds an integral (j+1)-chain of volume at most I times the volume of the cycle. Then |L2(F)| ≤ Iso_{n−k_1}(g) Iso_{k_3−1}(g) Vol(g) L^{n+2} Vol(h_1)^{-1} Vol(h_2)^{-1} Vol(h_3)^{-1}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the coarea inequality to choose q_1 ∈ S^{k_1} so that z_1 = F^{-1}(q_1) has volume at most L^{k_1} Vol(g)/Vol(h_1). Fill z_1 by y_1 with volume at most Iso_{n−k_1}(g) times that. Apply coarea to F restricted to y_1 and the S^{k_2} summand to choose q_2 so that z_2 = y_1 ∩ F^{-1}(q_2) has volume at most Iso_{n−k_1}(g) Vol(g) L^{k_1+k_2} Vol(h_1)^{-1}Vol(h_2)^{-1}. Fill z_2 by y_2 using Iso_{k_3−1}(g). Finally, the degree of π∘F on y_2 is bounded in absolute value by L^{k_3} Vol(y_2)/Vol(h_3). Multiplying these estimates gives the stated bound. The use of L^{k_i} for i ≥ 1 is justified by the hypothesis on k_1-dilation together with k_i ≥ k_1, which controls higher-dimensional Jacobians in the required directions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition on isoperimetric constants of ellipses (quoted from Guth [2])
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let E be an n-dimensional ellipse with principal axes E_0 ≤ ... ≤ E_n. For 0 ≤ k ≤ n−1, the k-dimensional isoperimetric constant Iso_k(E) is comparable, up to multiplicative constants depending only on n, to E_{k+1}+E_{n−k}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this proposition from Guth [2]. It is used by inserting the estimate into Proposition 1.2. The two terms correspond to the two different types of hard-to-fill cycles in an ellipse: for k large, small equators are hardest; for k small, large equators are hardest.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.3 (basic L2 bound on an ellipse from ordinary isoperimetry)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume 2 ≤ k_1 ≤ k_2 ≤ k_3 and n+2 = k_1+k_2+k_3. Let E be an n-dimensional ellipse with principal axes E_0 ≤ ... ≤ E_n. Let F : E → S^{k_1} ∨ S^{k_2} ∨ S^{k_3} be a map to a wedge of unit spheres with k_1-dilation at most L^{k_1}. If k_3 < (n+1)/2, then |L2(F)| ≤ C(n) E_{n−k_1+1} E_{n−k_3+1} Vol(E) L^{n+2}. If k_3 ≥ (n+1)/2, then |L2(F)| ≤ C(n) E_{n−k_1+1} E_{k_3} Vol(E) L^{n+2}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Proposition 1.2 to the ellipsoidal metric and use Iso_j(E) ≃ E_{j+1}+E_{n−j}. For the first filling, j = n−k_1, so Iso_{n−k_1}(E) ≃ E_{n−k_1+1}+E_{k_1}; since n−k_1+1 = k_2+k_3−1 is at least k_1, this is bounded by a dimensional constant times E_{n−k_1+1}. For the second filling, j = k_3−1, so Iso_{k_3−1}(E) ≃ E_{k_3}+E_{n−k_3+1}; this is controlled by E_{n−k_3+1} when k_3 < (n+1)/2 and by E_{k_3} when k_3 ≥ (n+1)/2. The target spheres have unit volume, so their volumes are absorbed into C(n).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.1 (directional filling of absolute cycles in a rectangle)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R = [0,R_1]×...×[0,R_n] be a rectangle with R_1 ≤ ... ≤ R_n. If z is an m-dimensional integral cycle in R, then there is an integral (m+1)-chain y in R with ∂y = z such that for every coordinate (m+1)-tuple I, if i is the smallest element of I and I−i denotes the m-tuple obtained by removing i, then Vol_I(y) ≤ R_i Vol_{I−i}(z).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Proceed by induction on n. Let π be projection to the face {x_1=0}. There is a straight-line homology from z to π(z) obtained by moving each point parallel to the x_1-axis; this homology has (1∪J)-volume at most R_1 Vol_J(z) for each J not containing 1 and has I-volume zero for I not containing 1. The projected cycle π(z) lies in the (n−1)-dimensional rectangle {0}×[0,R_2]×...×[0,R_n]. By the induction hypothesis, π(z) bounds y' there with Vol_I(y') ≤ R_i Vol_{I−i}(π(z)) ≤ R_i Vol_{I−i}(z) for coordinate tuples I not containing 1. Combining the straight homology with y' gives a filling y of z satisfying all the required directional estimates. The paper emphasizes the useful consequence that some directional volumes of z, namely those whose tuple contains 1, are not needed to bound the total volume of the constructed filling.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.1 (pushing a relative cycle that avoids the top face)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R = [0,R_1]×R' with R' = [0,R_2]×...×[0,R_n]. Suppose z is an m-chain in R with ∂z contained in ∂R, and suppose ∂z does not intersect the top face {R_1}×R'. Then there is an (m+1)-chain y in R and an m-chain B in ∂R such that ∂y = z+B and: (1) if I is an (m+1)-tuple containing 1, then Vol_I(y) ≤ R_1 Vol_{I−1}(z); (2) if I is an (m+1)-tuple not containing 1, then Vol_I(y)=0; (3) if J is an m-tuple not containing 1, then Vol_J(B) ≤ Vol_J(z).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let I : z → R be the identity map with coordinate functions I_1,...,I_n. Define f : z×[0,1] → R by f_1(x,t)=t I_1(x) and f_i(x,t)=I_i(x) for i≠1, and set y=f(z×[0,1]). The boundary of y consists of z, the projection f(z×{0}) into the bottom face, and the swept image f(∂z×[0,1]). Because ∂z lies in ∂R and avoids the top face, this swept boundary lies in the sides and bottom of R; hence ∂y=z+B with B⊂∂R. The chain y lies in [0,R_1]×π(z), where π is projection onto R', giving the stated estimates for Vol_I(y). The boundary chain B has one piece coming from f(∂z×[0,1]), whose J-volume is zero when J does not contain 1, and one piece equal to −π(z), whose J-volume is at most Vol_J(z).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.2 (directional filling/pushing of relative cycles in a rectangle)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R = [0,R_1]×...×[0,R_n] be a rectangle with R_1 ≤ ... ≤ R_n. Suppose z is an m-dimensional relative integral cycle in R, meaning ∂z⊂∂R. Then there is an (m+1)-chain y in R and an m-chain B contained in ∂R such that ∂y = z+B and the following estimates hold. For every coordinate (m+1)-tuple I, let e be the smallest number in {1,...,n} not contained in I; then Vol_I(y) ≤ C(n) Σ_{d=1}^{e−1} R_d Vol_{I−d}(z), where d<e implies d∈I. In addition, if J is an m-tuple not containing 1 and e is the smallest number in {2,...,n} not contained in J, then Vol_J(B) ≤ C(n) [Vol_J(z) + Σ_{d=1}^{e−1} R_1^{-1} R_d Vol_{(1∪J)−d}(z)], where (1∪J)−d means remove d from the tuple {1}∪J.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on n and uses Lemma 2.1 plus slicing. For h∈[0,R_1], let z_h = z∩{x_1=h}. By the coarea inequality, choose h such that for every (m−1)-tuple K in {2,...,n}, Vol_K(z_h) ≤ C(n) R_1^{-1} Vol_{1∪K}(z). Decompose z as z = z_1+z_2, where z_2=[0,R_1]×z_h and z_1=z−z_2; then split z_1 into an upper part z_+ and lower part z_− relative to the slice. Each of z_+ and z_− is a relative cycle avoiding one of the top/bottom faces, so Lemma 2.1 fills or pushes it to the boundary. The directional volumes of the artificial slice-cylinder [0,R_1]×z_h are controlled by the choice of h, so the resulting y_1 and B_1 satisfy the desired estimates. For z_2, apply the induction hypothesis in the (n−1)-rectangle [0,R_2]×...×[0,R_n] to fill z_h by y_h with boundary z_h+B_h and directional bounds. Set y_2=[0,R_1]×y_h. If an (m+1)-tuple I contains 1, Vol_I(y_2)=R_1 Vol_{I−1}(y_h), which is controlled by the inductive estimate and the chosen slice bounds; if I omits 1, Vol_I(y_2)=0. The boundary contribution B_2=[0,R_1]×B_h−{0}×y_h+{R_1}×y_h lies in ∂R, and its J-volumes for J not containing 1 are controlled by Vol_J(y_h). Adding y_1+y_2 and B_1+B_2 completes the induction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.3 (directional isoperimetric inequality in an ellipse)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let E be an n-dimensional ellipse with principal axes E_0 ≤ E_1 ≤ ... ≤ E_n. Directional volumes in E are defined by fixing a C(n)-bilipschitz identification of E with the double of the rectangle [0,E_1]×...×[0,E_n], splitting a chain into its northern and southern pieces, and adding the corresponding directional volumes in the two copies of the rectangle. If z is an m-dimensional integral cycle in E, then z bounds an integral (m+1)-chain y in E with the following estimates: for every coordinate (m+1)-tuple I, let i be the smallest element of I and let e be the smallest element of {1,...,n} not in I; then Vol_I(y) ≤ C(n) [E_i Vol_{I−i}(z) + Σ_{d=1}^{e−1} E_d Vol_{I−d}(z)].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write z=z_N+z_S according to the northern and southern halves of the double rectangle. Treat z_S as a relative cycle in the southern rectangle and apply Proposition 2.2 to get y_S with ∂y_S=z_S+B, where B lies on the equatorial boundary, and with directional estimates for y_S and for those directional volumes of B whose tuples omit 1. Then z_N−B is an absolute cycle in the northern rectangle. Apply Proposition 2.1 to fill it by y_N, giving Vol_I(y_N) ≤ E_i [Vol_{I−i}(z_N)+Vol_{I−i}(B)], where i is the smallest element of I. Since I−i never contains 1, the boundary estimates for B from Proposition 2.2 apply. If i>1, then I−i also misses 2, so the relevant estimate gives Vol_{I−i}(B) controlled by Vol_{I−i}(z_S), leading to Vol_I(y_N) ≲ E_i Vol_{I−i}(z). If i=1, the estimate for B gives Vol_{I−1}(B) ≲ E_1^{-1} Σ_{d=1}^{e−1} E_d Vol_{I−d}(z), and multiplication by E_1 gives the second term in the claimed bound. Finally set y=y_N+y_S; then ∂y=z and combining the bounds for y_N and y_S yields the proposition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.1 (exterior algebra estimate used in directional coarea)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be an m-dimensional plane in R^n, and let J be a coordinate k-tuple, also viewed as the corresponding unit coordinate k-vector. Then sup_{Q⊂P, dim Q=k} |J·Q| = (Σ_{J⊂I} |I·P|^2)^{1/2}, where Q ranges over oriented k-planes contained in P, I ranges over coordinate m-tuples containing J, and P and I are also viewed as unit simple m-vectors.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Both sides are invariant under rotations that fix the coordinate k-plane J. Use such a rotation to put P∩J^⊥ in coordinate position, say equal to the coordinate (m−k)-plane K disjoint from J. Let Q_0=P∩K^⊥, so P=Q_0⊕K. For any k-plane Q⊂P, write a unit k-vector for Q as a wedge of vectors v_i=u_i+w_i with u_i∈Q_0 and w_i∈K. When taking the inner product with J, all terms involving any w_i vanish, so |J·Q|≤|J·Q_0|, and equality is attained at Q_0. On the right-hand side, the only coordinate m-tuple I containing J with nonzero contribution is I=J∪K; hence the right-hand side equals |J∧K · Q_0∧K|=|J·Q_0|.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.1 (directional coarea inequality)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let y be a C∞ m-chain in R^n, and let F : y → (N^q,h) be a C∞ map to a q-dimensional Riemannian manifold whose q-dilation is at most Λ. Let k=m−q. Then there is a fiber z=F^{-1}(p), p∈N, such that for every coordinate k-tuple J, Vol_J(z) ≤ C(n) Λ Vol(N,h)^{-1} Σ_{J⊂I} Vol_I(y), where the sum is over all coordinate m-tuples I containing J.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the general coarea formula with a weight G. At points where Jac dF≠0, the kernel of dF is a k-plane; let V(F) be its unit k-vector. Choose G=|J·V(F)|. Then the left-hand side of coarea, integrated over N, is ∫_N Vol_J(F^{-1}(p)) dp. The right-hand side is ∫_y Jac(dF)|J·V(F)| dvol ≤ Λ ∫_y |J·V(F)| dvol. The k-plane V(F) lies inside the tangent m-plane Ty, so by Lemma 3.1, |J·V(F)|≤(Σ_{J⊂I}|I·Ty|^2)^{1/2}≤Σ_{J⊂I}|I·Ty|. Integrating gives ∫_N Vol_J(F^{-1}(p)) dp ≤ Λ Σ_{J⊂I} Vol_I(y). Since there are only finitely many J, choose one p for which all these inequalities hold simultaneously, losing only a dimensional constant.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1 (refined upper bound for L2 under k1-dilation)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume 2 ≤ k_1 ≤ k_2 ≤ k_3 and n+2 = k_1+k_2+k_3. Let E be an n-dimensional ellipse with principal axes E_0 ≤ ... ≤ E_n. Let F : E → S^{k_1} ∨ S^{k_2} ∨ S^{k_3} be a map to a wedge of unit spheres with k_1-dilation at most L^{k_1}. Then |L2(F)| ≤ C(n) E_{n−k_1+1} E_{k_3} Vol(E) L^{n+2}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First choose q_1∈S^{k_1} by coarea so that z_1=F^{-1}(q_1) has volume at most C(n)Vol(E)L^{k_1}; consequently every directional volume of z_1 is bounded by the same quantity. Fill z_1 by y_1 using Proposition 2.3. For an (n−k_1+1)-tuple I, with i the smallest element of I and e the smallest missing element, this gives Vol_I(y_1) ≤ C(n)Vol(E)L^{k_1}(E_i+E_{e−1}). Next choose q_2∈S^{k_2} using the directional coarea inequality applied to F restricted to y_1. If J is a coordinate tuple of dimension n−k_1+1−k_2, define f to be the (k_2+1)-st smallest element not in J. Since each relevant I is obtained from J by adding k_2 elements, the smallest missing element e of I is at most f, and since |I|=n−k_1+1 the smallest element i of I is at most k_1. Thus z_2=y_1∩F^{-1}(q_2) satisfies Vol_J(z_2) ≤ C(n)Vol(E)L^{k_1+k_2}(E_{k_1}+E_{f−1}). Fill z_2 by y_2 using Proposition 2.3. For a k_3-tuple K, let k be its smallest element and g its smallest missing element. Substitution of the z_2 estimate yields terms of the four forms E_k E_{k_1}, E_k E_{f(K−k)−1}, E_c E_{k_1}, and E_c E_{f(K−c)−1} for c<g. The paper checks each using only the ordering of the axes and the relations among k_i: k≤n−k_3+1 for the first term; for the second, f≤n−k_1+2 and either k≤k_3 or else k≥k_3+1≥k_2+1 implies f=k_2+1; for the third, g≤k_3+1 so c≤k_3; for the fourth, K−c has k_3−1 elements so f≤k_2+k_3=n−k_1+2 and again c≤k_3. In all cases the product is bounded by E_{n−k_1+1}E_{k_3}. Therefore Vol(y_2) ≤ C(n)Vol(E)L^{k_1+k_2}E_{n−k_1+1}E_{k_3}. Finally choose q_3∈S^{k_3} by coarea; the resulting zero-dimensional cycle z_3=y_2∩F^{-1}(q_3) has at most C(n)Vol(E)L^{n+2}E_{n−k_1+1}E_{k_3} points counted with multiplicity, and its signed count is L2(F).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.1 (Lipschitz map from an ellipse to a glued two-sphere wedge with linking invariant 1)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let E be an n-dimensional ellipse with axes E_0≤...≤E_n. Let d,e≥2 satisfy n+1=d+e. Construct a space X by taking S^d and S^e and identifying two antipodal points p,q on S^d with a basepoint * on S^e. Put on S^d the ellipsoidal metric E[d] with axes E_0≤...≤E_d, with p and q the two tips in the E_d-direction. Put on S^e the metric obtained by one-point compactifying the rectangle with side lengths E_{n−e+1},...,E_n, with the compactification point as basepoint. Then there is a map F:E→X with linking invariant 1 and Lipschitz constant at most C(n), and F sends the two tips of E to the basepoint of X.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Identify E up to C(n)-bilipschitz equivalence with the double of the rectangle [0,E_1]×...×[0,E_n]. Define an open set U shaped like S^{n−e}×B^e as the double of Π_{i=1}^{n−e}[0,E_i]×Π_{i=n−e+1}^{n}[(1/3)E_i,(2/3)E_i]. On U, project to the last e coordinates and map the resulting e-rectangle by a degree-one Lipschitz map to the compactified S^e, sending the boundary to the basepoint. Define V using the annular region in the last e coordinates between a larger rectangle Π[(1/10)E_i,(9/10)E_i] and the inner rectangle used for U; topologically V≃S^{e−1}×S^{d−1}×[0,1], and up to C(n)-bilipschitz equivalence it is Core×E[d−1]×[0,E_d]. On V, project to E[d−1]×[0,E_d] and map degree one and C(n)-Lipschitz to E[d]=S^d, taking the two boundary components to the two tips p,q, which become the basepoint in X. Map the complement of U∪V to the basepoint. A generic point in S^e has preimage a core S^{n−e} in U, and a generic point in S^d has preimage a core S^{e−1} in V; these core spheres link once in E, so the linking invariant is 1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2 (existence of maps with large second-order linking invariant)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume 2 ≤ k_1 ≤ k_2 ≤ k_3 and n+2 = k_1+k_2+k_3. Let E be an n-dimensional ellipse with principal axes E_0 ≤ ... ≤ E_n. If L > C(n) E_1^{-1}, then there is a map Φ : E → S^{k_1} ∨ S^{k_2} ∨ S^{k_3} to a wedge of unit spheres with Lipschitz constant L, up to changing the dimensional constants, and L2(Φ) ≥ c(n) E_{n−k_1+1} E_{k_3} Vol(E) L^{n+2}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Lemma 5.1 first with e=k_1, so the target X is obtained by gluing a sphere with ellipsoidal metric E[n−k_1+1] to an S^{k_1} carrying the compactified rectangle metric with sides E_{n−k_1+1},...,E_n. Apply Lemma 5.1 a second time to the ellipsoid E[n−k_1+1] with e=k_2; the resulting space X' glues an S^{k_3} with metric E[k_3] to an S^{k_2} carrying the compactified rectangle metric with sides E_{n−k_1−k_2+2},...,E_{n−k_1+1}, i.e. E_{k_3},...,E_{n−k_1+1}. Because the second map sends tips to the basepoint, it extends over X by acting as the identity on the S^{k_1} summand. The composition Φ_1:E→Y=X'∨S^{k_1} has L2(Φ_1)=1 by the product calculation for L2. Now construct a map α:Y→S^{k_1}∨S^{k_2}∨S^{k_3} to unit spheres, with Lipschitz constant L, that on the S^{k_i} summand has degree D_i. The paper asserts such α can be chosen with D_1 ≥ c(n) E_{n−k_1+1}...E_n L^{k_1}, D_2 ≥ c(n) E_{k_3}...E_{n−k_1+1} L^{k_2}, and D_3 ≥ c(n) E_1...E_{k_3} L^{k_3}. Then Φ=α∘Φ_1 has Lipschitz constant at most C(n)L and L2(Φ)=D_1D_2D_3. The product of the three displayed degree lower bounds is c(n) E_{k_3}E_{n−k_1+1}(E_1...E_n)L^{n+2}, and E_1...E_n is comparable to Vol(E) up to C(n), giving the theorem after adjusting constants.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3 (k-dilation lower bound for degree-D maps between ellipses)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let E and E' be n-dimensional ellipses with principal axes E_0≤...≤E_n and E'_0≤...≤E'_n, respectively. Set Q_i=E'_i/E_i. Suppose Φ:E→E' has degree D. If 2≤k_1≤k_2≤k_3, n+2=k_1+k_2+k_3, and k≤k_1, then Dil_k(Φ) > c(n) [|D| Q_{n−k_1+1} Q_{k_3} Q_1...Q_n]^{k/(n+2)}. In the special case n=4 and k=k_1=k_2=k_3=2, this gives Dil_2(Φ) ≳ |D|^{1/3} Q_1^{1/3} Q_2^{2/3} Q_3^{2/3} Q_4^{1/3}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use Theorem 2 on the target ellipse E' to choose, for large L, a map F:E'→S^{k_1}∨S^{k_2}∨S^{k_3} with Lipschitz constant L and L2(F) ≥ c(n) E'_{n−k_1+1} E'_{k_3} E'_1...E'_n L^{n+2}. Since Φ has degree D, the composition F∘Φ has |L2(F∘Φ)| at least |D| times this lower bound. On the other hand, if δ=Dil_k(Φ), then because k≤k_1, the k_1-dilation of F∘Φ is at most (δ^{1/k}L)^{k_1}; this is the singular-value monotonicity that converts a k-dilation bound into a higher-dimensional dilation bound. Apply Theorem 1 to F∘Φ as a map from E, with effective parameter δ^{1/k}L, to get |L2(F∘Φ)| ≤ C(n) E_{n−k_1+1} E_{k_3} E_1...E_n L^{n+2} δ^{(n+2)/k}. Comparing lower and upper bounds, canceling L^{n+2}, and rewriting the ratio of primed to unprimed axes as Q_i gives the stated inequality.

proof_techniques.name: Directional volume bookkeeping by coordinate projections
proof_techniques.description: Instead of estimating only the total volume of a chain, the paper decomposes volume according to coordinate directions: Vol_J(C) is the mass of the projection of C to the coordinate plane P(J). Because total volume is comparable to the sum of all directional volumes, a proof can track anisotropic information without losing control of ordinary volume. The main benefit is that fillings can be large in a few directions but small in most others, and these refined bounds survive subsequent coarea slicing.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The rectangle problem is explicitly side-length-sensitive, so tracking coordinate 2-, 3-, and 4-directional volumes is a natural way to exploit the ordered side lengths R_i and S_i rather than collapsing everything to total volume.

proof_techniques.name: Inductive absolute filling in rectangles by projection to a face
proof_techniques.description: For an absolute m-cycle in a rectangle, Proposition 2.1 fills it by first projecting to the face x_1=0 and using the straight-line homology between the cycle and its projection. The homology costs R_1 only in directions that include x_1, and the projected cycle is filled inductively in one lower dimension. The resulting estimate Vol_I(y) ≤ R_i Vol_{I−i}(z), where i is the smallest coordinate in I, is sharply anisotropic and has no unnecessary dependence on long sides.
