proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This construction can be transplanted directly to rectangular domains or to slices/fibers inside them. It is especially relevant for producing fillings whose cost is controlled by the smallest side appearing in a given coordinate direction.

proof_techniques.name: Relative boundary-pushing in rectangles via slicing
proof_techniques.description: For a relative cycle z with ∂z⊂∂R, Proposition 2.2 constructs a chain y with ∂y=z+B and pushes the leftover boundary B into ∂R, while controlling directional volumes of both y and B. The proof slices z by a coordinate hyperplane chosen by coarea, subtracts a cylinder over the slice, fills the upper and lower pieces by a simple push-to-bottom/top homotopy, and handles the cylinder by induction on dimension. This is a robust way to combine relative topology with anisotropic metric estimates.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original map is a map of pairs (R,∂R)→(S,∂S), so relative cycles and boundary terms are unavoidable. This boundary-pushing mechanism is a likely ingredient for adapting closed-manifold linking arguments to rectangles.

proof_techniques.name: Directional coarea inequality
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 3.1 refines the usual coarea inequality by bounding each directional volume of a fiber in terms of directional volumes of the chain being sliced. The proof inserts the weight |J·ker dF| into the coarea formula and uses an exterior algebra identity to compare the possible direction of the kernel with the directional components of the ambient tangent plane. This lets one choose a fiber whose projections are simultaneously small in all coordinate directions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A 2-dilation bound controls Jacobians for maps to 2-dimensional targets and, via singular-value monotonicity, higher-dimensional Jacobians. Directional coarea can turn those dilation hypotheses into anisotropic bounds for fibers or intersections inside a rectangle.

proof_techniques.name: Iterated coarea-filling-coarea scheme for second-order linking
proof_techniques.description: The proof of Theorem 1 estimates L2(F) by choosing a small first fiber z_1, filling it by y_1 with directional control, choosing a small second fiber z_2 inside y_1 with directional control, filling it by y_2, and finally counting a third fiber z_3. The key improvement over ordinary isoperimetry is that the first filling y_1 concentrates directional volume in short-axis directions, which forces z_2 to have favorable directional distribution and makes the second filling cheaper.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The desired rectangle inequality resembles an obstruction obtained by testing a degree-one map against a higher-order linking invariant. The same iterative structure could produce lower bounds involving mixed products of side lengths.

proof_techniques.name: Composition with a test map and homotopy-invariant comparison
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 3 proves dilation lower bounds by composing a degree-D map Φ:E→E' with a carefully built map F:E'→a wedge of spheres having large L2(F). Homotopy invariance and degree imply L2(F∘Φ)=D L2(F) up to sign, while Theorem 1 bounds L2(F∘Φ) from above in terms of the dilation of Φ and the source axes. Comparing the lower and upper bounds forces a quantitative dilation lower bound.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a degree-one rectangle map f:R→S, one can try to construct an anisotropic test map on S with a nonzero linking invariant, compose with f, and use a source-side upper bound to force inequalities among R_i and S_i.

proof_techniques.name: Construction of large-linking test maps from thick linked spheres
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 5.1 builds a Lipschitz map from an ellipsoid to a glued pair of spheres by placing two thick, linked core spheres inside the double-rectangle model of the ellipsoid. One region maps by projection to a compactified rectangle sphere, and an annular linked region maps by projection to an ellipsoidal sphere. Generic fibers over the two target summands are linked once, giving linking invariant 1. Theorem 2 iterates this construction and then composes with high-degree maps on each summand to amplify L2.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Rectangles contain natural products and annular regions analogous to the thick linked spheres used here. A rectangle-specific proof may need to build test maps on S whose fiber geometry reflects side lengths S_1,...,S_4.

proof_techniques.name: Double-of-a-rectangle model for ellipsoids
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly uses that an n-dimensional ellipsoid with axes E_0≤...≤E_n is C(n)-bilipschitz to the double of the rectangle with side lengths E_1,...,E_n. This allows rectangular directional estimates to be transferred to closed ellipsoids by treating northern and southern halves as two copies of a rectangle and managing the equatorial boundary with relative filling estimates.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A map of pairs between rectangles can often be doubled to a map between closed doubles, converting relative boundary behavior into closed topology. Conversely, the paper's closed ellipsoid estimates can suggest what relative estimates should hold directly in rectangles.

proof_techniques.name: Singular-value monotonicity from k-dilation to higher dilations
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 3 uses the fact that if a map has k-dilation δ and j≥k, then its j-dimensional Jacobians are controlled by δ^{j/k}, in the form needed to say that a composition with an L-Lipschitz map has k_1-dilation at most (δ^{1/k}L)^{k_1}. This follows from ordering singular values: once the product of the largest k is bounded, the remaining singular values beyond the kth are bounded by δ^{1/k}.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original hypothesis is Dil_2(f)≤1, but arguments may require controlling 3- or 4-dimensional Jacobians of composed maps or restrictions. This monotonicity is the standard bridge.

proof_techniques.name: Axis-order combinatorics in anisotropic product estimates
proof_techniques.description: In Theorem 1, after substituting directional coarea bounds into the second directional filling, many possible products of axes appear. The proof bounds all of them by the single product E_{n−k_1+1}E_{k_3} using only the ordering E_1≤...≤E_n and the dimension identities n+2=k_1+k_2+k_3. This kind of ordered-index bookkeeping is essential for extracting clean mixed-axis inequalities from many directional terms.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The target statement involves ordered side lengths and a dichotomy depending on products such as R_3R_4 and mixed powers of S_2,S_3. Similar ordered-index estimates may be needed after applying directional filling/coarea bounds in dimension 4.

other_useful_info: The paper uses constants C(n), c(n), and the notation ≲ for inequalities up to a factor depending only on n; all main estimates are uniform in the eccentricities of the ellipses. It explicitly notes that the analogous problem of sharp lower bounds for the 2-dilation of degree-one maps between 4-dimensional rectangles was solved in Guth [3], described as complicated and involving nonlinear near-optimal maps; that cited work is likely the closest source for the original rectangle theorem. The paper also states, as a conjectural Euclidean analogue, that every closed k-cycle z in R^n should bound y with Vol_I(y) ≤ C(n)(Π_{J⊂I} Vol_J(z))^{1/k} for every coordinate (k+1)-tuple I; this conjecture is not proved in the paper but motivates the directional viewpoint. For n=4, k=k_1=k_2=k_3=2, Theorem 3 gives the explicit ellipsoid lower bound Dil_2(Φ) ≳ |D|^{1/3} Q_1^{1/3}Q_2^{2/3}Q_3^{2/3}Q_4^{1/3}, which is the paper's simplest stated application to dilation.
why_relevant: This paper proves anisotropic, direction-dependent isoperimetric inequalities and applies them to k-dilation lower bounds. The directional nature of the estimates is highly relevant to the side-length-sensitive rectangle inequality in the problem.

title: Isoperimetric Inequalities and Rational Homotopy Invariants
overall_summary: The paper estimates isoperimetric constants of n-dimensional ellipses E with principal axes E0 ≤ ... ≤ En, proving that Iso_k(E) is comparable up to dimension-dependent constants to E_{k+1}+E_{n-k}. The core technique is to replace an ellipse by a bilipschitz double of a rectangle and prove anisotropic directional filling inequalities in rectangles, tracking projected volumes in coordinate directions rather than only total volume. These filling estimates are combined with Gromov's coarea-plus-filling method to bound Hopf and linking invariants of maps whose relevant k-dilation is controlled. The paper also constructs maps from ellipses to spheres or wedges of spheres with large Hopf/linking invariant using linked thick tubes, and uses these constructions to derive lower bounds for k-dilation of degree-nonzero maps between ellipses. For the original rectangle problem, the most relevant ingredients are the directional rectangle filling lemmas, the mechanism of composing a degree-one map with a high-linking test map, and the way k-dilation bounds topological invariants through fiber-volume and filling-volume estimates.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1.1 (coarea estimate for a small fiber)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F:(M^d,g) → (N^q,h) be a C∞ smooth map between finite-volume Riemannian manifolds, with d ≥ q. Suppose the q-dilation of F is at most Λ, meaning that the q-dimensional Jacobian Jac[dF(x)] is at most Λ at every point. Then there is a regular value y in N such that the (d−q)-dimensional volume of the fiber F^{-1}(y) is at most Vol_d(M,g) Λ / Vol_q(N,h). The paper also notes that for C1 maps the same coarea formula gives a fiber with bounded Hausdorff measure, and that a C1 map with q-dilation less than Λ can be smoothed within its homotopy class while keeping q-dilation less than Λ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply the coarea formula: ∫_M Jac[dF(x)] dvol_g = ∫_N Vol_{d−q}(F^{-1}(y)) dvol_h(y). Since Jac[dF(x)] ≤ Λ everywhere, the left-hand side is at most Λ Vol(M,g). Therefore the average fiber volume over N is at most Λ Vol(M,g)/Vol(N,h), so some regular fiber satisfies the stated bound. Sard's theorem gives regularity for almost every fiber in the smooth case. The comments on C1 maps use the version of coarea involving Hausdorff measure and then a smoothing argument.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.1 (Gromov Hopf bound from 2-dilation)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F:(S^3,g) → (S^2,h) be a C1 map with 2-dilation at most L^2. Let Iso_1(g) be the supremum of FillVol(z)/Vol(z) over all closed integral 1-cycles z in (S^3,g), where FillVol(z) is the least area of an integral 2-chain bounding z. Then the Hopf invariant satisfies |Hopf(F)| ≤ Iso_1(g) Vol(S^3,g) Area(S^2,h)^{-2} L^4.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: After smoothing, use Lemma 1.1 with q=2 to find a regular fiber F^{-1}(y) of length at most Vol(g)L^2/Area(h). This fiber is a closed 1-cycle in S^3, so it bounds a 2-chain C with area at most Iso_1(g) Vol(g)L^2/Area(h). The Hopf invariant can be computed as the degree of the map F restricted to the filling, F:(C,∂C) → (S^2,y). A degree d map from C to S^2 must satisfy |d| Area(h) ≤ area(F(C)); the 2-dilation bound gives area(F(C)) ≤ L^2 area(C). Hence |Hopf(F)| ≤ L^2 area(C)/Area(h), and substituting the filling-area bound gives the stated estimate.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.1 (Gromov linking bound from k1-dilation)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let k1 and k2 be integers with 2 ≤ k1 ≤ k2 and k1+k2=n+1. Let F:(S^n,g) → (S^{k1}∨S^{k2}, h1∨h2) be a smooth map to the wedge of two spheres, where h_i is a Riemannian metric on S^{ki}. Write A_i=Vol_{ki}(S^{ki},h_i), denoted Area(h_i) in the paper. Suppose F has k1-dilation at most L^{k1}. Let Iso_{k2−1}(g) be the filling-volume isoperimetric constant for exact integral (k2−1)-cycles in (S^n,g). Then the linking invariant L(F), defined as the linking number of the fibers over generic points in the two wedge summands, obeys |L(F)| ≤ Iso_{k2−1}(g) Vol(S^n,g) A_1^{-1} A_2^{-1} L^{n+1}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let U=F^{-1}(S^{k1}) inside S^n. Since Vol(U)≤Vol(S^n,g), Lemma 1.1 applied to F:U→S^{k1} gives a regular fiber F^{-1}(y1), y1∈S^{k1}, of dimension k2−1 and volume at most Vol(g)L^{k1}/A_1. Fill this fiber by a k2-chain C in S^n with Vol(C) ≤ Iso_{k2−1}(g) Vol(g)L^{k1}/A_1. Let π:S^{k1}∨S^{k2}→S^{k2} collapse S^{k1} to the basepoint and be the identity on S^{k2}. The linking invariant is the degree of π∘F:(C,∂C)→(S^{k2},basepoint). Since k2≥k1, the singular-value monotonicity of dilation implies that k1-dilation ≤ L^{k1} gives k2-dilation ≤ L^{k2}. Thus |deg(π∘F)| ≤ Vol(C)L^{k2}/A_2. Substitution yields the bound with exponent L^{k1+k2}=L^{n+1}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3 directional volume comparison
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let C be an m-dimensional rectifiable or integral chain in a rectangle R⊂R^n. For an m-tuple J of distinct coordinate indices, let P(J) be the coordinate m-plane spanned by those axes and let Vol_J(C) be the m-volume, counted with geometric multiplicity, of the orthogonal projection of C to P(J). Then Vol(C) ≤ ∑_{|J|=m} Vol_J(C) ≤ binomial(n,m) Vol(C). The paper defines e(J) to be one less than the smallest element of J; thus if J={3,6,8}, e(J)=2, and always 0≤e(J)≤n−m.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: At almost every point of C, the tangent m-vector has coordinate minors indexed by m-tuples J. The projected volume density in direction J is the absolute value of the corresponding coordinate minor. The Euclidean norm of the tangent m-vector is bounded above by the sum of the absolute values of all its coordinate minors, and this sum is bounded above by the number of minors times the Euclidean norm. Integrating these pointwise inequalities over the chain with multiplicity gives the stated comparison.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3, Lemma 1 (absolute rectangle filling with directional volumes)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R=[0,R1]×...×[0,Rn] be an n-dimensional rectangle with R1≤...≤Rn. Let z be an integral k-cycle in R with ∂z=0. For every k-tuple J of coordinate directions, let Vol_J(z) be the directional projected volume and let e(J) be one less than the smallest coordinate index in J. Then z bounds an integral (k+1)-chain y in R satisfying Vol(y) ≤ C(n) ∑_{|J|=k, e(J)≥1} R_{e(J)} Vol_J(z). In particular, directional volumes of z involving the shortest coordinate x1, i.e. e(J)=0, do not enter the estimate.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Project z to the hyperplane x1=0. The cylinder between z and its projection has volume at most R1 times the sum of those directional k-volumes of z whose directions do not contain x1, equivalently those with e(J)≥1. The projected cycle has the same J-volume as z for directions J not containing x1 and zero J-volume for directions containing x1. Repeat: project successively to x1=x2=0, then x1=x2=x3=0, and so on until reaching the coordinate k-plane x1=...=x_{n−k}=0. A k-cycle lying in this k-plane bounds a (k+1)-chain of zero volume. The cost at the projection to codimension c is bounded by R_c ∑_{e(J)≥c} Vol_J(z). Summing over c gives ∑_J (R1+...+R_{e(J)})Vol_J(z), which is at most C(n)∑_J R_{e(J)}Vol_J(z) because the side lengths are nondecreasing.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3, Lemma 2 (relative rectangle filling with controlled boundary directions)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R=[0,R1]×...×[0,Rn] be an n-dimensional rectangle with R1≤...≤Rn. Let z be an integral k-chain in R whose boundary is contained in ∂R, i.e. z is a relative k-cycle. Then there is an integral (k+1)-chain y in R and a k-chain B contained in ∂R such that ∂y=z+B, with the following estimates. First, Vol(y) ≤ C(n) R_{k+1} Vol(z). Second, for every (k+1)-tuple J with e(J)≥1, Vol_J(y)=0, so y has directional volume only in directions involving x1. Third, for every k-tuple J with e(J)>1, Vol_J(B) ≤ C(n) Vol(z). Fourth, for every k-tuple J with e(J)=1, Vol_J(B) ≤ C(n)(R_{k+1}/R1) Vol(z).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on k. For k=0 it is immediate. For k>0, slice z by hyperplanes x1=h and choose h so that the slice z_h=z∩{x1=h} has (k−1)-volume at most Vol(z)/R1. Decompose z as z1+z2, where z2=[0,R1]×z_h and z1=z−z2. The chain z1 splits into relative cycles z+ and z− above and below the slicing plane. Fill z− by pushing it straight down to the face x1=0, using F(p,t)=(t x1(p),x2(p),...,xn(p)); fill z+ similarly by pushing to x1=R1. These fillings have volume O(R1 Vol(z))≤O(R_{k+1}Vol(z)) and all their nonzero directional (k+1)-volumes involve x1. Their boundary contributions in ∂R have controlled projected volume: the projection pieces have volume O(Vol(z)), while the side pieces may be large only in directions containing x1, hence do not affect J-volumes with e(J)>0. For z2, apply the induction hypothesis to z_h inside the (n−1)-rectangle with side lengths R2,...,Rn. This gives a filling y_h of volume at most C R_{k+1}Vol(z_h) and boundary B_h. Set y2=[0,R1]×y_h. Then Vol(y2)≤R1 R_{k+1}Vol(z_h)≤R_{k+1}Vol(z), and all its directional volumes involve x1. The new boundary B2={R1}×y_h−{0}×y_h+[0,R1]×B_h has no directional contribution for k-tuples with e(J)>1; for e(J)=1 the two copies of y_h contribute at most O(R_{k+1}Vol(z_h))≤O((R_{k+1}/R1)Vol(z)). Adding the fillings of z+, z−, and z2 gives y and B with the asserted estimates.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.1 (upper bound for isoperimetric constants of ellipses)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let E be the n-dimensional ellipse in R^{n+1} with principal axes E0≤E1≤...≤En. For every k for which the isoperimetric constant Iso_k(E) is defined, namely the supremum over exact integral k-cycles z of FillVol(z)/Vol(z), one has Iso_k(E) ≤ C(n)(E_{k+1}+E_{n−k}).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The ellipse E is C(n)-bilipschitz equivalent to the double of the n-rectangle R with side lengths R_i=E_i for i=1,...,n. Split a k-cycle z in E into its parts z_N and z_S in the northern and southern copies of R. Apply Lemma 2 to the relative cycle z_S in the southern rectangle. This gives a chain y_S with ∂y_S=z_S+B, where B lies on the equator, Vol(y_S)≲E_{k+1}Vol(z), and B has directional estimates: for k-tuples J with e(J)=1, Vol_J(B)≲(E_{k+1}/E1)Vol(z), while for e(J)>1, Vol_J(B)≲Vol(z). View B as a chain in the northern rectangle. Then z_N−B is an absolute k-cycle there. Apply Lemma 1 to fill it by y_N with Vol(y_N) bounded by ∑_{e(J)≥1} E_{e(J)} Vol_J(z_N−B). For e(J)>1 the directional volumes are O(Vol(z)), and since e(J)≤n−k their total contribution is O(E_{n−k}Vol(z)). For e(J)=1 the contribution is E1·O((E_{k+1}/E1)Vol(z))=O(E_{k+1}Vol(z)). Thus y=y_S+y_N fills z with volume O((E_{k+1}+E_{n−k})Vol(z)), giving the proposition after absorbing the bilipschitz constants.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.1 (Hopf invariant upper bound for maps from 3-dimensional ellipses)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let E be a 3-dimensional ellipse with principal axes E0≤E1≤E2≤E3. Let F:E→(S^2,h) be a map with 2-dilation at most L^2. Then |Hopf(F)| ≤ C E2 Vol(E) L^4 / Area(S^2,h)^2. In particular, if h is the unit round metric, then |Hopf(F)| ≤ C E1 E2^2 E3 L^4.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Proposition 1.1 to get |Hopf(F)|≤Iso_1(E)Vol(E)L^4/Area(h)^2. Proposition 3.1 with n=3 and k=1 gives Iso_1(E)≤C(E2+E_{3−1})=C E2. Also Vol(E)≤C E1E2E3. Substitution gives the first bound, and for the unit sphere Area(h) is a dimensional constant, yielding the stated product E1E2^2E3.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.2 (linking invariant upper bound for maps from ellipses)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let E be an n-dimensional ellipse with principal axes E0≤E1≤...≤En. Let k1,k2 be integers with 2≤k1≤k2 and k1+k2=n+1. Let F:E→(S^{k1}∨S^{k2},h1∨h2) be a map with k1-dilation at most L^{k1}. Write A_i=Vol_{ki}(S^{ki},h_i). Then the linking invariant satisfies |L(F)| ≤ C(n) E_{k2} Vol(E) L^{n+1} A_1^{-1} A_2^{-1}. In particular, for unit sphere metrics on both target summands, |L(F)| ≤ C(n) E_{k2} Vol(E) L^{n+1}, and Vol(E) is comparable up to C(n) to E1...En.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Start from Proposition 2.1: |L(F)|≤Iso_{k2−1}(E)Vol(E)A_1^{-1}A_2^{-1}L^{n+1}. Proposition 3.1 gives Iso_{k2−1}(E)≤C(n)(E_{k2}+E_{n−k2+1}). Since k1+k2=n+1, n−k2+1=k1. The side lengths are nondecreasing and k1≤k2, so E_{k1}≤E_{k2}; hence Iso_{k2−1}(E)≤C(n)E_{k2}. Substitution yields the estimate.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.1 construction (large Hopf invariant on a 3-dimensional ellipse)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a 3-dimensional ellipse E with principal axes E0≤E1≤E2≤E3 and for L>C E1^{-1}, the construction in the proof gives a map F:E→S^2 to the unit sphere with Lipschitz constant at most L and Hopf invariant at least c E1 E2^2 E3 L^4, equivalently at least c E2 Vol(E)L^4 up to dimensional constants.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Model E as the double of the rectangle E1×E2×E3. Let U be the double of the central slab [0,E1]×[E2/3,2E2/3]×[E3/3,2E3/3], and project U to the 2-rectangle U'=[E2/3,2E2/3]×[E3/3,2E3/3]. Choose a map f1:(U',∂U')→(S^2,*) with Lipschitz constant L and degree at least cE2E3L^2. Let V be a region in the northern hemisphere surrounding the complementary annulus in the E2,E3 directions; it is bilipschitz to S^1(E3)×[0,E1]×[0,E2]. Project V to V'=[0,E1]×[0,E2], and choose f2:(V',∂V')→(S^2,*) with Lipschitz constant L and degree at least cE1E2L^2. Define F=f1∘projection on U, F=f2∘projection on V, and send the rest of E to the basepoint. For a generic y∈S^2, F^{-1}(y) consists of about E2E3L^2 parallel copies of the core of U and about E1E2L^2 parallel copies of the core of V. The two cores link once, while parallel copies of the same core have zero mutual linking. Comparing two generic fibers gives Hopf(F) at least the product c(E2E3L^2)(E1E2L^2)=cE1E2^2E3L^4.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.2 construction (large linking invariant on an n-dimensional ellipse)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let E be an n-dimensional ellipse with principal axes E0≤E1≤...≤En. Let k1,k2 satisfy 2≤k1≤k2 and k1+k2=n+1. If L>C(n)E1^{-1}, the construction in the proof gives a map F:E→S^{k1}∨S^{k2}, with unit metrics on the target spheres and Lipschitz constant at most L, whose linking invariant is at least c(n) E_{k2} Vol(E) L^{n+1}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Model E as the double of the rectangle with side lengths E1,...,En. Let U be a thick neighborhood of the double of [0,E1]×...×[0,E_{k2−1}], so its core is a standard S^{k2−1}. Geometrically U is bilipschitz to U1×[0,E_{k2}]×...×[0,En]. Project U to U'=[0,E_{k2}]×...×[0,En], whose dimension is n−k2+1=k1, and choose f1:(U',∂U')→(S^{k1},*) with Lipschitz constant L and degree at least c(n)E_{k2}...En L^{k1}. Let V be a thick neighborhood, in the upper hemisphere, of a (k1−1)-dimensional ellipse given by x1=...=x_{k2−1}=0 and ∑_{i=k2}^n ((x_i−E_i/2)^2/E_i^2)=1. This V is bilipschitz to V1×V', with V'=[0,E1]×...×[0,E_{k2}]. Choose f2:(V',∂V')→(S^{k2},*) with Lipschitz constant L and degree at least c(n)E1...E_{k2}L^{k2}. Define F to be f1 on U, f2 on V, and the wedge basepoint elsewhere. The fibers over generic points in the two wedge summands are many parallel copies of the two linked cores, whose linking number is 1. Hence the linking invariant is at least c(n)(E_{k2}...En L^{k1})(E1...E_{k2}L^{k2})=c(n)E_{k2}Vol(E)L^{n+1}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.3 (lower bound and sharpness for ellipse isoperimetric constants)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let E be an n-dimensional ellipse with principal axes E0≤E1≤...≤En. Then Iso_k(E) ≥ c(n)(E_{k+1}+E_{n−k}). Together with Proposition 3.1, this gives Iso_k(E) comparable to E_{k+1}+E_{n−k} up to dimension-dependent constants.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the E_{k+1} term, let S be the smallest k-dimensional equator of E, with volume comparable to E1...Ek. Let S' be the largest (n−k−1)-dimensional equator; S and S' have linking number 1. A thick neighborhood V of S' is bilipschitz to S'×V', where V'=[0,E1]×...×[0,E_{k+1}]. Any chain C with ∂C=S must intersect V in a set whose projection covers V', otherwise it would miss the required linking. Thus Vol(C)≥c(n)E1...E_{k+1}, so FillVol(S)/Vol(S)≥c(n)E_{k+1}. For the E_{n−k} term, reverse the roles: let S be the smallest (n−k−1)-dimensional equator and S' the largest k-dimensional equator. The cycle S' has volume comparable to E_{n−k+1}...En, and any filling must pass through a thick neighborhood of S bilipschitz to S×[0,E_{n−k}]×...×[0,En], forcing filling volume at least c(n)E_{n−k}...En. Dividing by Vol(S') gives c(n)E_{n−k}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.1 (localized Hopf bound for a thick-thin 3-sphere)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (S^3,g) be formed by taking the connected sum of the unit round 3-sphere with a long thin tube of width w≪1 and length A≫1, modeled as S^2(w)×[0,A] with a cap, and assume w^2A is large. Then every map F:(S^3,g)→S^2 to the unit sphere with Lipschitz constant L has Hopf invariant at most C(1+Aw^3)L^4.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof improves on the global estimate Iso_1(g)Vol(g)L^4 by treating thick and thin parts separately. Use coarea to choose a fiber F^{-1}(y) whose length in the thick part is O(L^2) and whose length in the thin part is O(Aw^2L^2). In the first unit length of the tube, choose a cross-section S^2(w)×{h} meeting the fiber in O(L^2) points. Perform surgery along this cross-section, adding only O(wL^2)≤O(L^2) length, to split the fiber into cycles z1 in the thick part and z2 in the thin part. Fill z1 with area O(L^2). Fill z2 more efficiently using the rectangle-style argument from Section 3, obtaining area O(Aw^3L^2) rather than O(Aw^2L^2). The whole fiber is filled by a 2-chain of area O((1+Aw^3)L^2). The Hopf invariant is the degree of F on this filling, and the 2-dilation from the Lipschitz bound contributes another factor L^2, giving O((1+Aw^3)L^4).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 7.1 (k-dilation lower bound for degree maps between ellipses)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let E and E′ be n-dimensional ellipses with principal axes E0≤...≤En and E′0≤...≤E′n. Let F:E→E′ be a map of degree D with k-dilation Λ, and assume k≤(n+1)/2. For i=1,...,n set Q_i=E′_i/E_i. Then Λ^{(n+1)/k} ≥ c(n)|D| Q_{n+1−k} ∏_{i=1}^n Q_i.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use Proposition 5.2 on the target ellipse E′ with k1=k and k2=n+1−k. It gives, for any sufficiently large L, a map Φ:E′→S^k∨S^{n+1−k} with Lipschitz constant L and linking invariant at least c(n)E′_{n+1−k}Vol(E′)L^{n+1}. The composition Φ∘F has linking invariant multiplied by the degree D, hence at least c(n)|D|E′_{n+1−k}Vol(E′)L^{n+1}. Its k-dilation is at most ΛL^k, since F has k-dilation Λ and Φ has Lipschitz constant L. Applying Proposition 4.2 to Φ∘F on the source ellipse E, with effective parameter L_eff=Λ^{1/k}L, gives an upper bound C(n)E_{n+1−k}Vol(E)L^{n+1}Λ^{(n+1)/k}. Comparing lower and upper bounds and canceling L^{n+1} gives Λ^{(n+1)/k} ≥ c(n)|D|(E′_{n+1−k}/E_{n+1−k})(Vol(E′)/Vol(E)). Since Vol(E′)/Vol(E) is comparable to ∏_{i=1}^n Q_i, the stated inequality follows.

proof_techniques.name: Coarea converts k-dilation into a small fiber
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly uses the coarea formula in the form that a map with q-dilation at most Λ has some fiber of codimension q whose volume is at most the average Λ Vol(domain)/Vol(target). This is important because it uses q-dimensional Jacobian control rather than full Lipschitz control; for example, 2-dilation bounds produce short 1-dimensional fibers for maps S^3→S^2 and small (k2−1)-dimensional fibers for maps to S^{k1}.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a 4-dimensional rectangle map with Dil_2≤1, one can try to compose with lower-dimensional test maps on the target so that coarea produces controlled fibers or slices in the source. This is a natural way to turn the analytic 2-dilation hypothesis into geometric cycles that can be filled or linked.

proof_techniques.name: Fill a small fiber and evaluate Hopf/linking as a degree
proof_techniques.description: Hopf and linking invariants are computed by taking a regular fiber, filling it by a chain in the domain, and measuring the degree of the map on that filling after collapsing the appropriate target summand. The filling volume is controlled by an isoperimetric constant, and the degree on the filling is bounded by the relevant dilation times the filling volume divided by the target volume.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A degree-one map between rectangles can be tested by composing it with a map from the target rectangle or its double to a sphere or wedge with known linking invariant. The invariant of the composition is forced by degree, while the source-side filling argument and Dil_2≤1 can give an upper bound involving R_i.

proof_techniques.name: Directional volume bookkeeping in rectangles
proof_techniques.description: Instead of controlling only total mass, Section 3 decomposes the mass of a chain into coordinate-projected volumes Vol_J. This allows the proof to push a relative cycle to the boundary of a rectangle, creating a boundary chain B that may have large total volume, while retaining exactly the directional estimates needed to fill the remaining absolute cycle efficiently. The key distinction is between directions with e(J)=1 and e(J)>1, producing the special factor R_{k+1}/R1 in Lemma 2.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem is anisotropic and rectangular, so losing track of directions can destroy the sharp side-length exponents. These directional filling lemmas are directly portable candidates for controlling chains in R or S while preserving products of selected side lengths.

proof_techniques.name: Doubling a rectangle and splitting into two hemispheres
proof_techniques.description: The ellipse is replaced, up to C(n)-bilipschitz equivalence, by the double of a rectangle. A cycle in the double is split into northern and southern relative pieces; one piece is pushed to the equator using a relative filling lemma, and then the resulting absolute cycle in the other copy is filled using an absolute filling lemma. This converts a closed-manifold filling problem into two Euclidean rectangle problems.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Because the original map is a map of pairs (R,∂R)→(S,∂S), doubling the rectangles is a natural way to create maps between closed spaces where degree, linking, and Hopf-type invariants are available. The paper's double-rectangle decomposition suggests how to keep rectangular side-length information after doubling.

proof_techniques.name: Linked thick tubes as test maps with large invariants
proof_techniques.description: The lower-bound constructions place two thick tubular neighborhoods of linked cores inside an ellipse. On the normal disks to one core the map has high degree to S^{k1}, and on the normal disks to the other core it has high degree to S^{k2}; outside the tubes the map is the wedge basepoint. Generic fibers are many parallel copies of linked cores, so the linking invariant is the product of the two degrees.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the target rectangle S, one can try to build analogous test maps supported in linked product regions whose normal cross-section volumes are products of S_i. Composing such a map with f should give lower bounds involving target side lengths, to be compared with source-side upper bounds.

proof_techniques.name: Higher dilation controlled by lower dilation
proof_techniques.description: The proof of Proposition 2.1 uses the singular-value fact that if a map has k1-dilation at most L^{k1}, then for every k2≥k1 it has k2-dilation at most L^{k2}. Indeed, the product of the largest k1 singular values bounds the k1-th singular value by L, and all later singular values are no larger.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: From Dil_2(f)≤1 in dimension 4, one also gets control of 3- and 4-dimensional Jacobians. This can supply volume or degree constraints in addition to the two-dimensional area constraint.

proof_techniques.name: Filling-volume lower bounds from linked equators
proof_techniques.description: To prove sharpness of the ellipse isoperimetric constant, the paper chooses a cycle linked with a complementary equator. Any filling of the cycle must pass through a thick neighborhood of the linked equator, and projection to the normal rectangle must cover that rectangle; this forces a lower bound on filling volume equal to a product of side lengths.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This provides a template for proving that certain cycles or sweepout slices in a 4-rectangle cannot have fillings below a specified product of side lengths. Such lower bounds may be useful when deriving alternatives involving products like S3S4 or mixed powers of S2 and S3.

proof_techniques.name: Localized thick-thin decomposition instead of a single global isoperimetric constant
proof_techniques.description: Section 6 shows that the global bound Iso_1(g)Vol(g)L^4 may be far from sharp. The proof cuts a chosen fiber along a favorable cross-section, performs a small surgery, and fills the resulting pieces in regions with different geometry and different filling costs.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a single global filling inequality for a rectangle is too crude to yield the desired exponents, a similar localization by coordinate slabs or thick-thin regions may produce sharper mixed product estimates.

other_useful_info: All constants c(n), C(n), and the implicit constants in ≲ depend only on dimension. The paper repeatedly uses that an n-dimensional ellipse is C(n)-bilipschitz to the double of the rectangle with side lengths E1,...,En; the shortest ambient axis E0 does not appear in the resulting intrinsic rectangular side lengths. In the supplied text, the displayed statements of Propositions 5.1 and 5.2 omit the factors L^4 and L^{n+1}, respectively, but the introduction, proofs, and Proposition 7.1 use the versions with those powers. The formula for Proposition 7.1 is typographically garbled in the supplied text; the proof gives Q_{n+1−k} times ∏_{i=1}^n Q_i. The linear-algebra dilation monotonicity used in Proposition 2.1 is attributed to Appendix 1 of Guth, Area-expanding embeddings of rectangles, arXiv:0710.0403.
why_relevant: This companion paper estimates isoperimetric constants for ellipsoids and uses them to bound k-dilation of degree-nonzero maps. Its methods for converting filling inequalities and linking/Hopf-type invariants into dilation lower bounds are plausible tools for the 4-dimensional rectangle problem.

title: On Gromov’s Waist of the Sphere Theorem
overall_summary: The paper gives a detailed proof of Gromov's waist theorem for the unit sphere: for every continuous map f:S^n→R^k, a fiber has ε-neighborhood at least as large as the ε-neighborhood of an equatorial S^{n-k}. The proof combines a generalized Borsuk-Ulam theorem for convex partitions of the sphere, a refinement into low-dimensional pancakes, compactness of convexely derived measures, one-dimensional sin-concavity estimates, and disintegration over limiting infinite convex partitions. The most transferable material for the rectangle dilation problem is the machinery for forcing a common fiber to meet many convex pieces, then converting local convex-measure lower bounds into global volume lower bounds. The pancake construction is especially relevant because the paper explicitly identifies Euclidean rectangles as model pancakes: an n-rectangle is close to k-dimensional precisely when n-k side lengths are small.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1 (Gromov waist of the sphere)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S^n be the unit n-sphere with its canonical Riemannian volume, let q≤n, and let f:S^n→R^q be continuous. Let S^{n-q} denote an equatorial subsphere of S^n. The theorem states that there exists z∈R^q such that, for every ε>0, vol_n(f^{-1}(z)+ε) ≥ vol_n(S^{n-q}+ε), where A+ε denotes the ε-tubular neighborhood of A in S^n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is built in several layers. First, a Gromov-Borsuk-Ulam partition theorem produces finite equal-volume partitions of S^n into convex sets whose chosen centers have the same f-image. Second, by restricting allowed cutting hyperplanes and passing to large numbers of cuts, these partitions can be made into q-pancakes, so in the limit one obtains convex partitions supported on convexely derived measures of dimension at most q. Third, convexely derived measures supported on d-dimensional convex sets have continuous densities that are sin^{n-d}-concave along geodesics; a one-dimensional comparison with cos^{n-d}(t) gives the sharp lower bound μ(B(M_0(μ),ε)) ≥ vol(S^{n-d}+ε)/vol(S^n), where M_0(μ) is the unique density maximum. Fourth, the paper defines r-centers M_r(μ) as maximizers of x↦μ(B(x,r)) and proves that M_r(μ) converges to M_0(μ) as r→0, uniformly on compact families of fixed-dimensional measures. Fifth, for a smooth generic f and partitions Π_r adapted to f, low-dimensional pieces are shown to have asymptotically zero weight by a weak concavity estimate for ball masses and the fact that max_z vol(f^{-1}(z)+r)=O(r^q). Hence almost all limiting weight lies on q-dimensional pieces, and integrating the local lower bound over the convex partition gives max_z vol(f^{-1}(z)+ε) ≥ vol(S^{n-q}+ε). Finally, continuous f are obtained by uniform approximation by smooth generic maps and a Hausdorff/Fatou semicontinuity argument for tubular neighborhoods.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3 (Gromov-Borsuk-Ulam theorem for convex partitions)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a continuous center map c from the space of open geodesically convex subsets of S^n to S^n. Let f:S^n→R^q be continuous with q≤n. For every integer i≥1, there exists a partition of S^n into 2^i open convex sets S_1,...,S_{2^i}, all of equal n-dimensional spherical volume, such that f(c(S_1))=...=f(c(S_{2^i})).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof parameterizes special convex partitions by a product P_i=(S^n)^{2^i-1}, whose factors are indexed by internal vertices of the rooted binary tree T_i of depth i. A point of P_i assigns an oriented great hypersphere to each internal vertex; following a path from the root to a leaf and choosing the positive or negative side at each stage gives one convex piece. The automorphism group Aut(T_i), generated by switching descendants at vertices, acts freely on P_i and identifies oriented descriptions of the same unoriented partition. For a partition point p∈P_i and for each terminal cutting hyperplane h, let S_h be the convex piece on one chosen side, v(h)=vol(S_h), and φ(h)=v(h) f(c(S_h)). Extend v and φ upward by summing over descendant leaves. Define an equivariant map F:P_i→(R^{q+1})^{2^i-1} by assigning to each internal node h the pair (v(h)-v(-h), φ(h)-φ(-h)). Equivariance lets F descend to a section of the vector bundle L_i=(P_i×(R^{q+1})^{2^i-1})/Aut(T_i) over Q_i=P_i/Aut(T_i). A Stiefel-Whitney computation shows that the top Stiefel-Whitney class of L_i is nonzero, so every section has a zero. At a zero of F, each binary split has equal volume and equal weighted f-center sums. Inducting down the binary tree gives equal volume for all leaves and equality of all f(c(S_j)).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 2.2-2.3 (topology of the binary partition bundle)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let T_i be the rooted binary tree used to encode i-step partitions of S^n. Lemma 2.2 identifies the automorphism group recursively as Aut(T_i)=Aut(T_{i-1}) wr Z_2, an iterated wreath product, with the Z_2 factors switching the two children of vertices at the previous level. Lemma 2.3 states that for the vector bundle L_i=(P_i×(R^{q+1})^{2^i-1})/Aut(T_i) over Q_i=P_i/Aut(T_i), where P_i=(S^n)^{2^i-1} and the action is the natural one on tree-indexed factors, the top Stiefel-Whitney class w_top(L_i) is nonzero.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Lemma 2.2, Aut(T_{i-1}) acts by permuting vertices above the last level, while an independent Z_2 switch is available at each vertex just above the leaves. These switches form a normal subgroup (Z_2)^I, where I is the set of level i-1 vertices, and conjugation by Aut(T_{i-1}) permutes the factors, giving the wreath-product semidirect product. For Lemma 2.3, use the splitting P_i=P_{i-1}×(S^n)^{2^{i-1}} and the induced fibration Q_i→Q_{i-1} with fiber (RP^n)^{2^{i-1}}. On each fiber, the new part of L_i restricts to a sum of copies of the tautological line bundle γ_n over RP^n, repeated q+1 times for each new factor. Thus L_i decomposes as p_i^*L_{i-1} plus these tautological summands. Since w(γ_n)=1+a and powers a^j are nonzero in H^*(RP^n;Z_2) up to degree n, the top Stiefel-Whitney class multiplies inductively by a nonzero fiber class. Starting from the i=1 case gives w_top(L_i)≠0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4 (pancake partitions)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f:S^n→R^q be continuous, with q≤n, and keep a fixed continuous center map on open convex subsets. A convex set C⊂S^n is called a (q,ε)-pancake if every point of C is within distance ε of some q-dimensional convex subset of S^n. For every ε>0 there exists i_0 such that for all i≥i_0 there is a partition of S^n into 2^i open convex subsets such that each piece is a (q,ε)-pancake, all pieces have equal volume, and the centers of all pieces have the same image under f.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof modifies the partition construction of Theorem 3 by restricting the available cutting hyperplanes. If L is an (n-q-1)-dimensional linear subspace through the origin in R^{n+1}, then oriented hyperplanes containing L are parametrized by the unit sphere in L^⊥, identified with S^{q+1}. Since the target has dimension q, the same Borsuk-Ulam/Stiefel-Whitney argument applies when the cutting normal at a stage is required to lie in such an S^{q+1}. A compactness lemma on the Grassmannian supplies finitely many planes L_1,...,L_N whose associated restricted direction spheres form an ε-net in the sense needed to hit balls of cutting directions. Repeating enough stages with these restrictions forces every final convex piece to contain no ball of definite radius in any (q+1)-sphere section. A Blaschke-selection compactness argument then implies that any sequence of convex sets with this no-(q+1)-ball property converges to a convex set of dimension at most q; hence, for sufficiently many cuts, every piece lies within ε of a q-dimensional convex set. The equal-volume and common-image properties are inherited from Theorem 3.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.1 (density and sin-concavity of convexely derived measures)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S⊂S^n be a geodesically convex set of dimension d≤n. Let μ be a convexely derived probability measure supported on S, meaning that μ is a vague limit of normalized restrictions vol|S_j/vol(S_j) for open convex sets S_j⊂S^n. Then μ has a continuous density f with respect to the canonical d-dimensional Riemannian volume on S. Moreover, along every geodesic arc contained in S, the density f is sin^{n-d}-concave: equivalently, f^{1/(n-d)}, transported to an arc of the unit circle, extends to a 1-homogeneous concave function on the corresponding planar cone.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Take the Euclidean cones co(S_j)⊂R^{n+1}; they converge to co(S). The normalized Euclidean measures on co(S_j) converge to a convexely derived measure μ' on co(S). A Euclidean convex-geometry result cited from Gromov-Milman gives μ'=F dm_{d+1}, where F is (n-d)-concave. Because the cone construction comes from spherical volume and radial scaling, μ' is (n+1)-homogeneous while (d+1)-dimensional Lebesgue measure is (d+1)-homogeneous, so F is (n-d)-homogeneous. Restricting F to the cone over any geodesic arc σ⊂S gives an (n-d)-concave and (n-d)-homogeneous function on a two-dimensional cone. Therefore F^{1/(n-d)} is concave and 1-homogeneous there, which is exactly sin-concavity after restriction to the unit circle. The restriction to S gives the asserted spherical density f.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 4.3-4.7 (one-dimensional sin-concavity comparison)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let m>0. A nonnegative sin^m-concave function on an interval has at most one maximum point and no local minimum. If f is a nonzero nonnegative sin^m-concave function on [0,τ] whose maximum is attained at 0, then τ≤π/2. More precisely, if 0<ε<π/2 and h(t)=c cos^m(t) is chosen so that h(ε)=f(ε), then f(t)≥h(t) for 0≤t≤ε and f(t)≤h(t) for ε≤t≤τ. Consequently, for every α≥0 and ε≤π/2, one has the integral comparison ∫_0^{min(ε,τ)} f(t) sin^α(t) dt / ∫_0^τ f(t) sin^α(t) dt ≥ ∫_0^ε cos^m(t) sin^α(t) dt / ∫_0^{π/2} cos^m(t) sin^α(t) dt.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The uniqueness of the maximum and absence of local minima follow by writing g=f^{1/m} as the restriction of a 1-homogeneous concave function G on a planar cone. If two circle points had equal maxima, concavity and homogeneity would force a larger value at the normalized midpoint; a local minimum is excluded similarly by representing it as a normalized midpoint. For the comparison with cos^m, reduce to m=1 by taking m-th roots and symmetrize f by g(t)=f(|t|). A gluing lemma shows that this symmetrized function remains sin-concave because the one-sided derivatives at the maximum have the correct order. Let G be the homogeneous concave extension of g and let H(x,y)=cx, the homogeneous extension of c cos(t). Since G-H is concave and vanishes at the two symmetric points with angular distance ε, its sign is nonnegative in the sector between them and nonpositive outside. This yields the pointwise comparison. Multiplying by sin^α(t) and integrating on [0,ε] and [ε,τ] gives the displayed ratio inequality; the same comparison also rules out τ>π/2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 4.8-4.9 (sharp lower bound for balls in convexely derived measures and equatorial tube formula)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let μ be a convexely derived probability measure supported on a d-dimensional convex set S⊂S^n with d<n. Let M_0(μ) be the unique point where the density of μ with respect to d-dimensional volume attains its maximum. Then for every ε>0, μ(B(M_0(μ),ε)) ≥ [∫_0^ε cos^{n-d}(t) sin^{d-1}(t) dt] / [∫_0^{π/2} cos^{n-d}(t) sin^{d-1}(t) dt]. In addition, if S^{n-d} is an equatorial subsphere of S^n, then vol_n(S^{n-d}+ε)/vol_n(S^n) equals the same quotient of integrals.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use polar coordinates (t,u) centered at M_0(μ) inside the d-sphere containing S. Convexity of S gives a radial boundary function τ(u) so that points of S in direction u have 0≤t≤τ(u). Write dμ=f(t,u) sin^{d-1}(t) dt du. For each fixed direction u, the function t↦f(t,u) is sin^{n-d}-concave and has its maximum at t=0, so Lemma 4.7 with α=d-1 gives a lower bound for the fraction of the ray-mass lying in 0≤t≤ε by the model cos^{n-d} fraction. Integrating over u gives the stated lower bound for μ(B(M_0(μ),ε)). For the equatorial tube formula, push forward spherical volume under the distance-to-S^{n-d} map; the density is a constant times cos^{n-d}(t) sin^{d-1}(t) dt on [0,π/2], and normalization cancels the constant.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.1 and Corollary 5.3 (Bishop-Gromov lower bound for convex pieces and their limits)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every open convex set S⊂S^n, every x∈S, and every r, vol(S∩B(x,r))/vol(S) ≥ vol(B(x,r))/vol(S^n). If μ is any convexely derived probability measure on S^n and x belongs to support(μ), then μ(B(x,r)) ≥ vol(B(x,r))/vol(S^n).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For an open convex S, apply Bishop-Gromov comparison on the unit sphere: the ratio vol(S∩B(x,r))/vol(B(x,r)) is nonincreasing in r. Comparing its value at radius r to its limiting value when the ball fills S^n gives the displayed inequality. For a convexely derived limit μ=lim vol|S_j/vol(S_j), first extract a subsequence whose supports Hausdorff-converge to a compact convex set containing support(μ). If x∈support(μ), choose x_j∈S_j with x_j→x. Since metric ball boundaries have zero μ-mass in the relevant cases, vague convergence of measures on sets with null boundary passes the finite inequality to the limit.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 5.4-5.6 (compactness and continuity for convexely derived measures)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let MC be the vague closure of normalized volume measures of open convex subsets of S^n. Let MC_0 be the measures with zero-dimensional support and MC_+=MC\MC_0. The support map μ↦support(μ), from MC to compact subsets of S^n with the Hausdorff metric, is continuous. If MC_ρ denotes measures in MC whose support has diameter at least ρ>0, then sup_{μ∈MC_ρ, x∈S^n} μ(B(x,r)) tends to 0 as r→0. Finally, the function (μ,x,r)↦μ(B(x,r)) is continuous on MC_+×S^n×[0,π/2).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For support continuity, if μ_j→μ and supports S_j Hausdorff-converge to S, test functions supported away from S show support(μ)⊂S. Conversely, if x∈S, take x_j∈S_j→x and use the Bishop-Gromov lower bound from Corollary 5.3 on a bump function supported near x to show that μ gives positive mass to every neighborhood of x. For uniform small-ball mass on MC_ρ, reduce locally to Euclidean space. If B=B(x,r) and support(μ) has a point y at distance at least ρ/2 from x, translate so y is the origin in the affine span. Concavity and homogeneity of the density imply μ(λB)≥λ^n μ(B) for λ∈(0,1). Choosing N≈ρ/(4r) disjoint dilates λB with λ between 1/2 and 1 gives μ(B)≤C(n) r/ρ. For continuity of μ(B(x,r)), move x_j to x by isometries tending to the identity, use vague convergence and the fact that non-Dirac convexely derived measures give zero mass to ball boundaries, and then use Dini's lemma to make the dependence on r continuous.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.8 (disintegration formula for infinite convex partitions)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let CP be the vague closure, in the space of probability measures on MC, of finite convex partitions Π of S^n encoded by m(Π)=Σ_S [vol(S)/vol(S^n)] δ_{μ_S}, where μ_S=vol|S/vol(S) and the sum runs over the pieces S. Let A⊂S^n be a set such that ∂A has zero ℓ-dimensional volume in every ℓ-dimensional subsphere, for every 0<ℓ<n. If Π∈CP and Π(MC_0)=0, then vol(A)/vol(S^n)=∫_{MC} μ(A) dΠ(μ).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a finite convex partition, the identity is simply additivity of volume over the pieces after normalizing each piece. The boundary hypothesis ensures, via the vague convergence lemma for Radon measures on sets whose boundary has zero measure, that μ↦μ(A) is continuous on MC_+. Therefore the finite-partition identity passes to vague limits m(Π_j)→Π, provided Π gives no mass to MC_0. This yields the stated disintegration over the limiting convex partition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.9 and Corollary 5.10 (semicontinuity of r-centers and closedness of adapted partitions)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For μ∈MC and r>0, define v_{r,μ}(x)=μ(B(x,r)) and let M_r(μ) be the set of points in support(μ) where v_{r,μ} is maximal. If μ_i→μ∈MC_+, then every subsequential limit of points x_i∈M_r(μ_i) lies in M_r(μ), and every subsequential limit of points in the spherical convex hulls conv(M_r(μ_i)) lies in conv(M_r(μ)). For a continuous map f:S^n→R^q, define F_r to be the set of convex partitions Π∈CP for which ⋂_{μ∈support(Π)} f(conv(M_r(μ))) is nonempty. Then F_r is closed in CP.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the first assertion, let x_i∈M_r(μ_i) converge to x. For any y∈support(μ), support continuity gives y_i∈support(μ_i) with y_i→y. Continuity of ball masses gives v_{r,μ}(x)=lim v_{r,μ_i}(x_i) and v_{r,μ}(y)=lim v_{r,μ_i}(y_i). Since each x_i maximizes v_{r,μ_i}, x maximizes v_{r,μ}. For convex hulls, pass to cones and use Carathéodory: any limit of convex combinations of points from M_r(μ_i) is a convex combination of limit points from M_r(μ). For closedness of F_r, take Π_i→Π with Π_i∈F_r and choose z_i in the common intersection. After passing to a convergent subsequence z_i→z, every μ∈support(Π) is a limit of μ_i∈support(Π_i); the convex-hull semicontinuity and continuity of f imply z∈f(conv(M_r(μ))) for all μ in support(Π).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 5.11 (existence of adapted low-dimensional convex partitions)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every r>0, the set F_r of r-adapted convex partitions contains uniform atomic measures coming from finite convex partitions with arbitrarily many pieces. Moreover, using the pancake partitions of Theorem 4 and closedness of F_r, one obtains elements of F_r∩CP_{≤q}, where CP_{≤q} denotes convex partitions supported on convexely derived measures whose supports have dimension at most q.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The finite adapted partitions come from applying Theorem 3 with a center construction tied to M_r. Theorem 4 allows these finite partitions to be chosen with pieces lying in arbitrarily thin neighborhoods of q-dimensional convex sets. Passing to the compact space CP and using the closedness of F_r from Corollary 5.10 gives a limiting convex partition that is still r-adapted to f and whose support lies in MC_{≤q}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 5.12-5.16 (r-centers converge to density maxima)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let μ be a convexely derived measure supported on a d-dimensional convex set with d<n, and let M_0(μ) be the unique point where its density is maximal. Then d_H(M_r(μ),M_0(μ))→0 as r→0. More strongly, for each fixed d<n, if μ'→μ within MC_d and r→0, then d_H(M_r(μ'),M_0(μ))→0; consequently, on every compact subset of MC_d, the functions μ↦d_H(M_r(μ),M_0(μ)) converge uniformly to 0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a fixed μ, if x_r∈M_r(μ) stayed a definite distance from M_0(μ), a subsequence x_r→x would satisfy d(x,M_0(μ))≥δ. Normalized small-ball masses μ(B(y,r))/(α_d r^d) converge to the density φ(y) at continuity points. Since x_r maximizes the ball mass, the limit would force φ(y)≤φ(x) for all y, making x another density maximum, contradiction. For varying μ', first rotate supports so they lie in a common d-sphere and converge in the Hausdorff sense. A density estimate gives max φ≤2^{n+1}/vol_d(S), preventing blow-up when support volume stays nonzero. A compactness lemma for bounded concave functions on converging convex bodies gives subsequential uniform convergence of densities on compact subsets of the relative interior and an upper-semicontinuity bound at the boundary. The same maximizing-ball contradiction then proves the stronger convergence. Uniform convergence on compact families follows by compactness.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5 (waist lower bound from q-dimensional adapted mass)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume f:S^n→R^q is a generic smooth map. Let r_i→0 and let Π_i∈CP_{≤q}∩F_{r_i} be convex partitions of dimension at most q that are r_i-adapted to f. Then for every ε>0, max_{z∈R^q} vol(f^{-1}(z)+ε)/vol(S^n) ≥ [vol(S^{n-q}+ε)/vol(S^n)] · limsup_{i→∞} Π_i(MC_q).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each i, r_i-adaptedness gives z_i∈R^q such that for every μ∈support(Π_i) there is x_{i,μ}∈conv(M_{r_i}(μ)) with f(x_{i,μ})=z_i. On any compact K⊂MC_q, Lemmas 5.12-5.16 imply that x_{i,μ} is uniformly close to M_0(μ), and Lemma 5.6 gives μ(B(x_{i,μ},ε)) close to μ(B(M_0(μ),ε)). Since x_{i,μ} lies in the fiber f^{-1}(z_i), B(x_{i,μ},ε)⊂f^{-1}(z_i)+ε. The disintegration formula applies to f^{-1}(z_i)+ε for generic smooth f, because its boundary intersects q-dimensional convex sets in zero q-volume. Hence vol(f^{-1}(z_i)+ε)/vol(S^n) ≥ ∫_K μ(B(x_{i,μ},ε)) dΠ_i(μ). Lemma 4.8 supplies μ(B(M_0(μ),ε))≥vol(S^{n-q}+ε)/vol(S^n) for μ∈MC_q. Letting i→∞ and then taking the supremum over compact K⊂MC_q yields the displayed inequality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.17 (Prékopa-Leindler concavity of ball masses)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S⊂R^n be open and convex. Let φ:S→[0,∞) be m-concave, meaning φ^{1/m} is concave, and set μ=φ dvol_n. Then for each fixed r, the function x↦μ(B(x,r)∩S) is (m+n)-concave on S.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the generalized Prékopa-Leindler inequality quoted from Bobkov-Ledoux. For α=1/m and β=α/(1+α n)=1/(m+n), if w(θx+(1-θ)y)≥M_α^{(θ)}(u(x),v(y)) pointwise, then ∫w≥M_β^{(θ)}(∫u,∫v). Apply this with u=1_{B(x,r)}φ, v=1_{B(y,r)}φ, and w=1_{B(θx+(1-θ)y,r)}φ. Convexity of balls under Minkowski combination and m-concavity of φ give the pointwise hypothesis. The conclusion is exactly (m+n)-concavity of x↦μ(B(x,r)∩S).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.18 (weak concavity of spherical ball-mass functions)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: There exists c=c(n)>0 such that for every convexely derived measure μ on S^n, every sufficiently small r>0, and every K⊂support(μ), if v_{μ,r}(x)=μ(B(x,r)), then min_{x∈conv(K)} v_{μ,r/c}(x) ≥ c · min_{x∈K} v_{μ,r}(x), after adjusting c only as a function of n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: It suffices to prove the estimate along geodesic segments. In a small spherical chart, radially project to the tangent Euclidean space. If μ has k-dimensional support and density φ on S^n, extend φ homogeneously to the cone and restrict to the tangent space; the resulting Euclidean density is (n-k)-concave. Lemma 5.17 then gives concavity, with exponent depending only on n, of Euclidean ball masses. Hence at any point in the middle third of a segment, the ball mass at comparable radius is bounded below by a dimensional constant times the larger endpoint mass, and in particular by the smaller endpoint mass. The radial projection distorts distances and densities only by dimensional constants on the chosen chart. Covering a long spherical segment by boundedly many such charts gives the global weak concavity estimate with a constant depending only on n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6 (low-dimensional pieces force large small-radius fiber tubes)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: There exists c=c(n)>0 such that, for sufficiently small r>0, if f:S^n→R^q and Π∈F_r∩CP_{≤q}, then max_{z∈R^q} vol(f^{-1}(z)+r/c) ≥ c · Σ_{ℓ=0}^q vol(S^{n-ℓ}+c r) Π(MC_ℓ), with constants adjusted only as functions of n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose z∈R^q witnessing r-adaptedness of Π. For each μ in support(Π), with ℓ=dim support(μ), there is x∈conv(M_r(μ)) such that f(x)=z. Therefore B(x,r/c) lies inside f^{-1}(z)+r/c. Weak concavity gives μ(B(x,r/c))≥c min_{y∈M_r(μ)} μ(B(y,r)). Since every y∈M_r(μ) maximizes the r-ball mass, this is at least c μ(B(M_0(μ),r)), where M_0(μ) is the density maximum. Lemma 4.8 compares this last quantity to the normalized volume of an r-neighborhood of an equatorial S^{n-ℓ}; after absorbing radius changes into c, this gives a lower bound proportional to vol(S^{n-ℓ}+c r)/vol(S^n). Integrating over Π with the disintegration formula yields the stated inequality.

proof_techniques.name: Binary-tree equivariant partition spaces
proof_techniques.description: The paper encodes iterative hyperplane partitions of S^n by a product of spheres indexed by internal nodes of a rooted binary tree. The automorphism group of the tree acts freely on this parameter space, and volume/image-balance conditions become the zero set of an equivariant map. Passing to the quotient gives a vector bundle section; nonvanishing of the top Stiefel-Whitney class forces a zero, which is a partition with equal volumes and common f-image of centers.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A rectangle proof might try to build sweepouts or decompositions of a doubled rectangle using a similar equivariant balancing argument, forcing many pieces or slices to interact with a common fiber of a map derived from f. The key transferable feature is converting simultaneous volume-balance and image-coincidence requirements into a topological obstruction.

proof_techniques.name: Pancake refinement by restricted cutting directions
proof_techniques.description: After obtaining balanced convex partitions, the paper restricts the cutting hyperplanes at each stage to direction spheres associated with a finite set of lower-dimensional planes. Repeating enough cuts prevents any final piece from containing a sizable ball in a (q+1)-dimensional section. By compactness of convex sets, this forces each piece to lie near a q-dimensional convex set, i.e. to be a q-pancake.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem concerns rectangles with side-length inequalities; the paper explicitly notes that Euclidean rectangles are model pancakes when some side lengths are very small. A proof may use analogous decompositions of a 4-rectangle into pieces that are effectively 2-dimensional or 3-dimensional, then combine this with 2-dilation control.

proof_techniques.name: Convexely derived measure localization
proof_techniques.description: Finite convex partitions are compactified by replacing each piece with its normalized volume measure and passing to vague limits. Limits are convexely derived measures supported on lower-dimensional convex sets. This turns a limiting partition into a probability measure on the space of such localized convex measures, allowing global volume to be recovered by disintegration.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a rectangle argument produces increasingly fine partitions or sweepouts, this compactness framework suggests how to pass to a limiting family of lower-dimensional pieces without losing volume accounting. It may be useful after doubling boxes to avoid boundary effects.

proof_techniques.name: Conical lift from spherical convexity to Euclidean convexity
proof_techniques.description: To analyze densities of convexely derived measures on S^n, the paper cones convex sets to R^{n+1}. Euclidean convex-geometric facts give concavity of the limiting density on the cone, and homogeneity of the cone measure translates this into sin-concavity on spherical geodesics.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For rectangles, the Euclidean side of this argument is closer to the native geometry: one can try to use Brunn-Minkowski or log/α-concavity of slice densities directly on convex subsets of boxes.

proof_techniques.name: One-dimensional comparison with the cos^m model
proof_techniques.description: The paper reduces sharp ball-mass estimates to a one-dimensional statement: a nonnegative sin^m-concave density with maximum at an endpoint is bounded below near the maximum and above away from it by a multiple of cos^m(t). Integrating this comparison with the spherical polar Jacobian sin^{d-1}(t) yields the equatorial tube-volume ratio.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A rectangular analogue might replace sin/cos by affine or log-concave one-dimensional models. This is a promising template for proving lower bounds on the amount of a convex piece lying near a preferred center or slice.

proof_techniques.name: Disintegration over infinite convex partitions
proof_techniques.description: The paper represents a limiting convex partition Π as a probability measure on convexely derived measures and proves vol(A)/vol(S^n)=∫μ(A)dΠ(μ) for sets A with sufficiently regular boundary. This allows local estimates on each convex piece to be integrated into a global lower bound for the volume of a fiber neighborhood.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a degree-one rectangle map, one often needs to integrate local filling or slicing estimates over a sweepout. This disintegration identity is a clean model for justifying such integration after passing to limiting partitions.

proof_techniques.name: Maximal ball-mass centers and semicontinuity
proof_techniques.description: Instead of relying only on arbitrary centers of convex pieces, the paper defines M_r(μ) as the set where μ(B(x,r)) is maximal. These centers behave upper-semicontinuously under vague convergence, and as r→0 they converge to the unique density maximum M_0(μ) for fixed-dimensional convexely derived measures.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When a common fiber intersects only a controlled region of each piece rather than a precise center, maximal ball-mass centers give a robust substitute. This may help in rectangular sweepout arguments where exact centers are unstable under limiting or smoothing operations.

proof_techniques.name: Weak concavity of ball-mass functions via generalized Prékopa-Leindler
proof_techniques.description: The paper proves that for a measure with concave density on a convex Euclidean set, the function assigning to a point the measure of a fixed-radius ball is itself concave in a weaker exponent. Transferred to the sphere by charts, this gives a weak concavity estimate: ball mass at points in the convex hull of good centers is bounded below by a dimensional constant times the endpoint mass at a comparable radius.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This can bypass an obstacle where topology only forces a fiber to meet the convex hull of favorable points, not the points themselves. In a rectangle proof, an analogous estimate could convert intersection with a convex hull into a quantitative lower bound on nearby volume or area.

proof_techniques.name: Low-dimensional mass elimination by tube-volume growth
proof_techniques.description: For smooth generic f:S^n→R^q, small tubes around fibers have volume O(r^q). Proposition 6 says any positive mass of ℓ-dimensional partition pieces with ℓ<q would force a lower bound of order r^ℓ, too large as r→0. Therefore the limiting adapted partition must put asymptotically all its mass on q-dimensional pieces.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem has a 2-dilation hypothesis and degree-one topology; a similar contradiction between expected tube or slice growth and forced lower bounds from lower-dimensional pieces might yield side-length inequalities.

proof_techniques.name: Approximation by smooth generic maps and tubular semicontinuity
proof_techniques.description: After proving the waist estimate for smooth generic maps, the paper approximates a continuous map uniformly by such maps. If g_j→f and z_j is a good value for g_j, then g_j^{-1}(z_j) lies in a small preimage neighborhood for f. Passing to a subsequential limit z and using Fatou's lemma gives the desired lower bound for f^{-1}(z)+ε.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original map is only piecewise smooth. This approximation-and-semicontinuity pattern is useful for reducing to generic transversality assumptions while preserving dilation or degree estimates up to controlled error.

proof_techniques.name: Bishop-Gromov comparison inside convex subsets
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses the monotonicity of vol(S∩B(x,r))/vol(B(x,r)) for convex S⊂S^n to show that every normalized convex piece contains at least the ambient fraction of any ball centered in it. This estimate survives passage to convexely derived limits.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For rectangular or doubled rectangular domains, analogous relative volume lower bounds for convex pieces could supply crude but robust estimates when sharp localization is unnecessary.

other_useful_info: The paper explicitly remarks that in Euclidean space the typical examples of pancakes are rectangles: an n-dimensional rectangle is a k-pancake when the sizes of n-k side directions are very small. It cites Gromov 2003 for the original waist theorem, Gromov-Milman 1987 and Alesker for localization and convexity ideas, and Bobkov-Ledoux for the generalized Prékopa-Leindler inequality. The introduction also mentions Gromov's related metric measurements such as k-widths and k-volumes of maps, which are conceptually close to dilation and sweepout estimates.
why_relevant: This paper provides a detailed proof of Gromov's waist theorem. Its rigorous treatment of waist/sweepout estimates can be useful if the rectangle problem is approached by embedding or doubling rectangles and applying quantitative waist arguments.

title: Contraction of Areas vs. Topology of Mappings
overall_summary: The paper studies how small k-dilation interacts with homotopy classes of maps between spheres, especially area-contraction and torsion homotopy invariants. Its main lower-bound mechanism constructs homology cycles associated to a map, estimates their directed volumes from k-dilation bounds, and uses quantitative geometric measure theory, including Federer-Fleming deformation and a perpendicular-pair inequality, to force these cycles to be null-homologous when k-dilation is too small. Its main upper-bound mechanism is an h-principle construction showing flexibility of k-dilation above the threshold k > (m+1)/2. For the rectangle problem, the most transferable ingredients are the singular-value calculus for k-dilation, topological lower bounds via degree/homology, directed-volume estimates for product maps, deformation/null-homology criteria for small chains, and the appendix lemma on bilipschitz embeddings of rectangles under partial-product inequalities.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 2.1-2.2, singular-value formula for k-dilation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F : (M,g) → (N,h) be a C1 map of Riemannian manifolds. At x in M, let s1(x) ≥ s2(x) ≥ ... be the singular values of dF_x, with zeros appended if necessary. Then the operator norm of Λ^k dF_x is s1(x)⋯sk(x), and the k-dilation of F is Dil_k(F) = sup_x ||Λ^k dF_x|| = sup_x s1(x)⋯sk(x).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose orthonormal bases v1,...,vm for T_xM and w1,...,wn for T_{F(x)}N such that dF_x(vj)=sj wj. The wedge products v_{i1}∧...∧v_{ik} form an orthonormal basis of Λ^kT_xM, and Λ^k dF_x sends such a basis vector to s_{i1}⋯s_{ik} w_{i1}∧...∧w_{ik}. The largest singular value of Λ^k dF_x is therefore s1⋯sk. For the global formula, a small k-disk tangent to span(v1,...,vk) gives the lower bound for Dil_k, while for any oriented k-submanifold Σ one integrates |Λ^k dF_x(V_Σ(x))| over Σ to get Vol_k F(Σ) ≤ sup_x ||Λ^k dF_x|| Vol_k(Σ).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 2.4-2.5, monotonicity of dilations and pullback of forms
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If F is C1 and k < l, then Dil_k(F)^{1/k} ≥ Dil_l(F)^{1/l}. If α is a k-form on the target of F, then ||F^*α||_∞ ≤ Dil_k(F) ||α||_∞.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For singular values s1 ≥ s2 ≥ ..., the inequality (s1⋯sk)^{1/k} ≥ (s1⋯sl)^{1/l} holds pointwise, and taking suprema gives the monotonicity of dilations. For forms, (F^*α)_x = (Λ^k dF_x)^*(α_{F(x)}), and the adjoint has the same operator norm as Λ^k dF_x; hence |(F^*α)_x| ≤ ||Λ^k dF_x|| |α_{F(x)}| ≤ Dil_k(F)||α||_∞.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.6, degree controlled by top-dimensional dilation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a C1 map F : S^m → S^m between unit m-spheres, |Deg F| ≤ Dil_m(F).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let ω be the volume form of the unit sphere normalized only by its usual volume. Then Deg F = (Vol S^m)^{-1} ∫_{S^m} F^*ω. By the pullback estimate for m-forms, ||F^*ω||_∞ ≤ Dil_m(F)||ω||_∞, and integrating gives |Deg F| ≤ Dil_m(F).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Main Theorem
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix an integer m ≥ 3. If k > (m+1)/2, then there is a sequence of homotopically nontrivial maps F_j : S^m → S^{m-1} with Dil_k(F_j) → 0. If k ≤ (m+1)/2, then every homotopically nontrivial map F : S^m → S^{m-1} has Dil_k(F) ≥ c(m) > 0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The lower-bound half is a special case of the Steenrod-square lower bound: the nontrivial class in π_m(S^{m-1}) gives a cell complex B^{m+1}∪_F S^{m-1} with nontrivial Steenrod square, and the paper proves that such maps cannot have arbitrarily small k-dilation when k≤(m+1)/2. The upper-bound half is obtained from the h-principle for k-dilation. Given any F0 : S^m → S^n with m>n and k>(m+1)/2, the map is homotoped by precomposition with carefully constructed degree-one self-maps so that outside a controlled tubular neighborhood its image lies in a (k−1)-skeleton, where k-dilation vanishes, and inside the neighborhood all directions relevant to F0 are strongly contracted.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 4.1-4.2, Hopf invariant via a product cycle
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a C1 map F : S^3 → S^2 between unit spheres, |Hopf(F)| ≤ C Dil_2(F)^2. More precisely, choose a basepoint x0 in S^3 and let y0=F(x0). In S^3×S^3 choose an integral 4-chain Z0 with ∂Z0 = Diag(S^3) − Bouquet(S^3), where Bouquet(S^3)=S^3×{x0} ∪ {x0}×S^3. Since F×F maps the boundary into Diag(S^2)∪Bouquet(S^2), which is 2-dimensional, one can alter F×F(Z0) by a zero-volume 4-chain ν to obtain a 4-cycle Z(F) in S^2×S^2. Then [Z(F)] = Hopf(F)[S^2]×[S^2] in H_4(S^2×S^2;Z).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The homology computation uses the cell complex X=B^4∪_F S^2. Let a generate H^2(X;Z) and b generate H^4(X;Z); the Hopf invariant is the integer H such that a∪a=Hb. The cup square is interpreted by pulling back a×a under the diagonal X→X×X. The chain Z0 in ∂B^4×∂B^4, together with Diag(B^4) and Bouquet(B^4), bounds a 5-chain in B^4×B^4. Applying the characteristic map B^4→X shows that Z(F) is homologous in X×X to Diag(X)−Bouquet(X), up to flat-equivalent zero-volume corrections. Since a×a vanishes on the bouquet and restricts to the fundamental class on S^2×S^2, evaluation gives [Z(F)] = Hopf(F)[S^2]×[S^2]. The dilation estimate is then immediate: Vol_4 Z(F) ≤ Vol_4(F×F(Z0)) ≤ Dil_4(F×F) Vol_4(Z0), and Dil_4(F×F) ≤ Dil_2(F)^2 because the singular values of d(F×F) are the singular values of dF at the two factors combined.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.4, Steenrod-Hopf invariant via a cycle in Γ_i S^n
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F : S^m → S^n be C1 with m>n, and assume i=2n−m−1 is a nonnegative integer. Form X=B^{m+1}∪_F S^n and define SH(F) in Z2 by Sq^{m+1−n}(a)=SH(F)b, where a generates H^n(X;Z2) and b generates H^{m+1}(X;Z2). For a space Y define Γ_iY=(S^i×Y×Y)/((θ,y1,y2)∼(−θ,y2,y1)). Choose a mod 2 2n-chain Z0 in Γ_iS^m with ∂Z0=Diag(S^m)+Bouquet(S^m). The induced map Γ_iF sends Γ_iS^m to Γ_iS^n, and Γ_iF(Z0) can be corrected by a zero-volume 2n-chain ν to a 2n-cycle Z(F) in Γ_iS^n. Then [Z(F)] = SH(F)[S^n×S^n] in H_{2n}(Γ_iS^n;Z2), where S^n×S^n denotes a fiber of Γ_iS^n → RP^i.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The construction first notes that Diag(S^m) and Bouquet(S^m) are homologous in Γ_iS^m, using the cell structure of Γ_iS^m and the vanishing of H_d(Γ_iS^m) for m+i<d<2m. The target homology H_{2n}(Γ_iS^n;Z2) is Z2, represented by a fiber S^n×S^n. To identify the class of Z(F), take a classifying map Φ:X→K(Z2,n) for a. In Γ_iK(Z2,n) there is a cohomology class β of degree 2n whose restriction to each fiber is α×α and which vanishes on the bouquet. The standard construction of Steenrod squares says that pulling β back along RPi×X→Γ_iX→Γ_iK gives Σ_j ω^j⊗Sq^j a, so SH(F)=Γ_iΦ^*β[Diag(X)]. A flat-chain comparison using the characteristic map B^{m+1}→X shows Z(F) is homologous to Diag(X)−Bouquet(X) inside Γ_iX. Since β vanishes on the bouquet, evaluating Γ_iΦ^*β on Z(F) gives SH(F). Finally, on Γ_iS^n this cohomology class evaluates nontrivially on the fiber S^n×S^n, so the homology class of Z(F) is exactly SH(F) times the fiber class.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Steenrod squares and k-dilation theorem
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F : S^m → S^n be a C1 map. Let X=B^{m+1}∪_F S^n. If X has a nontrivial Steenrod square, equivalently in the notation of the paper SH(F)≠0 for the relevant square, and if k≤(m+1)/2, then Dil_k(F) ≥ c(m)>0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof uses the cycle Z(F) in Γ_iS^n whose homology class detects SH(F). Directed volumes of Z(F) are estimated by the k-dilation of F. If Dil_k(F) is very small and k≤(m+1)/2, then all directed volume of Z(F) in bad directions, those with the two S^n factors nearly balanced, is very small. If there were no bad-direction volume, the double cover of Z(F) in S^i×S^n×S^n would split into two cycles according to whether more tangent directions lie in the first or second S^n factor; one of these cycles projects degree one onto Z(F) but is null-homologous in the cover, forcing Z(F) to be null-homologous. With small nonzero bad volume, the double cover instead consists of two large pieces connected by a small bridge. The perpendicular-pair inequality cuts the bridge and caps one side by a chain of very small Hausdorff content. Projecting back to Γ_iS^n expresses Z(F) as the sum of a null-homologous cycle and a cycle of arbitrarily small Hausdorff content, hence null-homologous by the Federer-Fleming Hausdorff-content deformation argument. This contradicts SH(F)≠0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 6.1-6.3, directed volume calculus for products and Γ_i
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a Riemannian product M=A×B×C and a d-chain T, define Vol_{(a,b,c)}(T), for a+b+c=d, by testing tangent d-vectors against forms α∧β∧γ with α an a-form on A, β a b-form on B, γ a c-form on C, all of pointwise norm at most 1, and summing over simplices of the chain. Then Vol_d(T) is comparable, with constants depending only on dimension, to Σ_{a+b+c=d} Vol_{(a,b,c)}(T). If Φ=Φ_A×Φ_B×Φ_C is a product map, then Vol_{(a,b,c)}(Φ(T)) ≤ Dil_a(Φ_A) Dil_b(Φ_B) Dil_c(Φ_C) Vol_{(a,b,c)}(T). For Γ_iS^n, directed volumes are defined through the double cover S^i×S^n×S^n, with Vol_{(a,b,c)} identified with Vol_{(a,c,b)}; for F:S^m→S^n, Vol_{(a,b,c)}(Γ_iF(T)) ≤ Dil_b(F) Dil_c(F) Vol_{(a,b,c)}(T).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The comparison of total volume with the sum of directed volumes is linear algebra: any unit d-form on A×B×C decomposes into boundedly many sums of wedge products of forms from the three factors, while each such wedge product has norm at most 1. The product-map estimate follows by pulling back α∧β∧γ under Φ_A×Φ_B×Φ_C and using the definitions of the factor dilations. The Γ_i estimate is the same argument on the double cover, where Γ_iF lifts to id×F×F; the identity contributes no dilation in the S^i factor and the two copies of F contribute Dil_b(F) and Dil_c(F).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.4 and Corollaries 6.5-6.6, directed volume estimates for Z(F)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F:S^m→S^n be C1, let i=2n−m−1, and let Z(F) be the mod 2 2n-cycle in Γ_iS^n constructed from a fixed 2n-chain Z0 in Γ_iS^m. Then for every direction (a,b,c) with a+b+c=2n, Vol_{(a,b,c)}(Z(F)) ≤ C(m) Dil_b(F) Dil_c(F). Consequently, if Dil_{m+1−n}(F)≤1, then Vol_{2n}(Z(F)) ≤ C(m) Dil_{m+1−n}(F)^{(m+1)/(m+1−n)}. In particular, if SH(F)≠0, then Dil_{m+1−n}(F) ≥ c(m)>0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Since Z(F)=Γ_iF(Z0)+ν and ν has zero 2n-volume, directed volume comes only from Γ_iF(Z0). Lemma 6.3 gives Vol_{(a,b,c)}Γ_iF(Z0) ≤ Dil_b(F)Dil_c(F)Vol_{(a,b,c)}Z0, and Z0 is fixed, so its directed volumes are bounded by constants depending only on m. For the total-volume corollary, possible directions satisfy a≤i=2n−m−1, hence b+c≥m+1; because b,c≤n, each of b and c is at least m+1−n. Writing D=Dil_{m+1−n}(F)^{1/(m+1−n)} and using monotonicity gives Dil_b(F)≤D^b and Dil_c(F)≤D^c, so each directed volume is at most C D^{m+1}. Small total volume implies null-homology by Federer-Fleming deformation, but Proposition 5.4 says nonzero SH(F) makes Z(F) homologically nontrivial.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 6.7-6.8 and Proposition 6.9, bad directions and null-homology of good-direction cycles
