key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In Γ_iS^n, a direction (a,b,c) is called bad if |b−c|≤1 and good if |b−c|≥2. If Dil_k(F)≤1 and b,c≥k, then Vol_{(a,b,c)} Z(F) ≤ C Dil_k(F)^2. If k≤(m+1)/2, then every bad direction for the 2n-cycle Z(F) has b,c≥k, so all bad-direction volume is bounded by C Dil_k(F)^2. Moreover, if X is a polyhedral 2n-cycle in Γ_iS^n containing no 2n-faces in bad directions, then X is homologically trivial.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The volume estimate follows from Proposition 6.4 and monotonicity: Dil_b(F), Dil_c(F) ≤ Dil_k(F) when b,c≥k and Dil_k(F)≤1. To see b,c≥k for bad directions, use a+b+c=2n and a≤i=2n−m−1, so b+c≥m+1; if |b−c|≤1, elementary parity cases give b,c≥(m/2) for m even or ≥(m+1)/2 for m odd, hence ≥k. For the polyhedral null-homology statement, lift X to its double cover in S^i×S^n×S^n and split the lift into X1 with b<c and X2 with b>c. Because good directions satisfy |b−c|≥2, taking a boundary cannot make faces from X1 and X2 coincide, so X1 and X2 are separately cycles. The projection of X1 to Γ_iS^n maps degree one onto X, but X1 is null-homologous in the cover: a 2n-cycle in S^i×S^n×S^n with i<n can be nontrivial only through its projection to S^n×S^n, and X1 has zero (0,n,n)-directed volume. Hence X is null-homologous.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 7.1 and Lemma 7.2, small Hausdorff-content cycles are null-homologous
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (M^N,g) be a compact Riemannian manifold and let n≤N. There exists ε>0, depending on M,g,n, such that every n-cycle z in M with n-dimensional Hausdorff content HC_n(z)<ε is homologically trivial, with any coefficient group. The key local estimate is that in a unit Euclidean simplex Δ^N, radial projection from a randomly chosen point p in the concentric half-size simplex sends a set E⊂Δ to ∂Δ with average HC_d bounded by C(N)HC_d(E); if HC_d(E) is sufficiently small, p can be chosen outside E with the same bound.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the local estimate, cover E by balls B(x_i,R_i). Radial projection from p maps B(x_i,R_i) into a ball on ∂Δ of radius at most C Dist(p,x_i)^{-1}R_i, except in the trivial case Dist(p,x_i)≲R_i. Averaging Dist(p,x_i)^{-d} over p is finite for d≤N−1, giving the Hausdorff-content bound. For the global proposition, triangulate M with bounded geometry. Repeatedly apply the pushout estimate on N-simplices, then (N−1)-simplices, and so on, pushing the cycle to the n-skeleton while increasing HC_n by only a controlled factor. If the original HC_n is small enough, the final cycle in the n-skeleton cannot cover any n-simplex, hence is null-homologous.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Perpendicular Pair Inequality, Sections 7-8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let z and w be mod 2 (n−1)-cycles in R^N, and let y be a mod 2 n-chain with ∂y=z+w. Suppose z and w are perpendicular in the sense that for every coordinate (n−1)-tuple J, either Vol_J(z)=0 or Vol_J(w)=0, where Vol_J is the directed volume obtained by projecting to the coordinate J-plane. Then there exists an n-chain y' with ∂y'=z, with HC_n(y') ≤ C(n,N) Vol_n(y), and lying in the R-neighborhood of z for R≤C(n,N)Vol_n(y)^{1/n}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First approximate z,w,y by cubical chains at a very fine scale, preserving perpendicularity and changing z by an arbitrarily small homology. Define the α-thick region T_α(y) as the union of balls B(x,r) with Vol(y∩B(x,r))≥α r^n; Vitali covering gives HC_n(T_α(y))≤Cα^{-1}Vol_n(y). The goal is to build a filling of z entirely inside T_α(y). At dyadic scales s_i, deform z to cubical cycles z_i using Federer-Fleming deformation operators, but with bent dual skeleta rather than mere translations. A probabilistic local-grid construction ensures that if a dual face used in the deformation has nonzero intersection with z, then a nearby ball is α-thick for y; this uses the intersection-number lemma and a Lovasz-local-lemma-type probability argument. Thus every z_i lies in T_α(y), and z_i=0 once s_i exceeds C Vol(y)^{1/n}. Homologies A_i between z_{i−1} and z_i are built using compatible deformations at adjacent scales and homotopies between bent dual skeleta; the same local-grid property keeps A_i inside T_α(y). Then y'=Σ_i A_i satisfies ∂y'=z and lies in T_α(y), so HC_n(y') is bounded by HC_n(T_α(y))≤CVol(y). The stopping scale and the fact that deformations move points only O(s_i) give the stated neighborhood bound.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Intersection number lemma, Section 8.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let z,w be perpendicular mod 2 (n−1)-cycles in R^N and let ∂y=z+w. Fix α>0 and s>0. Let R0 be an axis-parallel rectangle of dimension N−n+1 with side lengths at most s, and for v in B^N(s) let R_v=R0+v. If the ball centered at the center of R0 with radius Ns is α-thin for y, meaning Vol_n(y∩B)<α(Ns)^n up to dimensional constants, then the probability over v∈B^N(s) that the mod 2 intersection number [z∩R_v] is nonzero is at most C(n,N)α.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The identity [(z+w)∩R_v]=[y∩∂R_v] holds because ∂y=z+w. Integral geometry shows that, under the α-thinness assumption, the probability that y intersects any face of ∂R_v is O(α), so with high probability [(z+w)∩R_v]=0. Let J be the coordinate (n−1)-tuple perpendicular to R0. By perpendicularity, either Vol_J(z)=0 or Vol_J(w)=0, so for almost every v one of [z∩R_v] or [w∩R_v] is automatically zero. Hence the equality [z∩R_v]+[w∩R_v]=0 cannot usually be due to cancellation; [z∩R_v] itself is nonzero only with probability O(α).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Federer-Fleming deformation operator estimates, Section 8.4 and Appendix 14.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Σ(s) be the cubical lattice of side length s in R^N and let ̄Σ(s) be the dual lattice. For a mod 2 d-chain T transverse to the translated dual lattice ̄Σ_v(s), define D_v(T)=Σ_{F^d⊂Σ^d(s)} [̄F_v^{N−d}∩T] F, a cubical d-chain in Σ^d(s). If |v|<s/2 and T is already a cubical d-chain in Σ(s), then D_v(T)=T. The operator commutes with boundary: D_v(∂T)=∂D_v(T). Averaging over |v|<s/2, Vol_d(D_vT)≤C(N)Vol_d(T). If z is a d-cycle, there is a (d+1)-chain A_v(z) in the C(N)s-neighborhood of z with ∂A_v(z)=z−D_v(z), and average Vol_{d+1}(A_v(z))≤C(N)sVol_d(z).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The identity on cubical chains follows because each dual face intersects its primal face once mod 2 and other faces zero times, with intersection number unchanged under small translation. Boundary-commutation is an intersection-number calculation: the coefficient of a (d−1)-face G in ∂D_v(T) is the intersection of T with the boundary of the dual face ̄G_v, which equals the intersection of ∂T with ̄G_v. The average volume bound follows from integral geometry: the chance that a translated dual face intersects T is controlled by s^{-N}Vol(T) in a ball of radius O(s), and summing over faces has bounded overlap. For the homology A_v, Federer-Fleming radial pushout maps T from each cube to lower skeleta until it lies in Σ^d_v(s); the trace of the homotopy has average volume O(sVol_d z), and translating back from the shifted lattice gives the chain connecting z to D_vz.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 14.2, bilipschitz embeddings of rectangles
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R=Π_{j=1}^n[0,R_j] and S=Π_{j=1}^n[0,S_j] be n-dimensional rectangles with side lengths sorted R1≤...≤Rn and S1≤...≤Sn. If Π_{j=1}^p R_j ≥ Π_{j=1}^p S_j for every p=1,...,n, then there is a locally C(n)-bilipschitz embedding from S into R.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on n. In dimension 2, if both sides of R are at least the corresponding sides of S, the identity works; otherwise S is longer and thinner than R but has no larger area, and one folds S back and forth inside R with bounded local bilipschitz constant. For n>2, if all R_j≥S_j the identity works. Otherwise let a be the first index with R_a<S_a, so R_{a−1}≥S_{a−1}. Modify only the two adjacent side lengths S_{a−1},S_a to get an intermediate rectangle S' with S'_{a}=R_a or S'_{a−1}=R_{a−1}, preserving their product and keeping the side lengths ordered; the 2-dimensional folding embeds S into S' with bounded distortion. The partial-product inequalities for S' relative to R are checked directly, and the common coordinate is then removed so the induction hypothesis embeds S' into R. Composing gives the desired locally C(n)-bilipschitz embedding.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary to Lemma 14.2, embedding convex sets into balls
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If A is an n-dimensional convex set in R^n with volume 1, then A admits a locally C(n)-bilipschitz embedding into the unit n-ball, and also into the upper hemisphere of the unit n-sphere.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: After rotating coordinates, enclose A in a rectangle R of volume at most C(n). With side lengths ordered, every partial product of side lengths of R is bounded by C(n). Lemma 14.2 embeds R, after a uniform rescaling in constants, into the unit cube with locally C(n)-bilipschitz distortion. The unit cube has a standard C(n)-bilipschitz embedding into the unit ball or upper hemisphere.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 12.2 and Lemma 12.3, C0 limits preserve k-dilation at differentiability points
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F_i:B^m→R^n be C1 maps with Dil_k(F_i)≤1, and suppose F_i converges uniformly to F. If F is differentiable at x, then Dil_k(dF_x)≤1. In particular, if F is C1, then Dil_k(F)≤1. The key special case is: if F_i:̄B^k→R^k converge uniformly to a linear map L and Dil_k(F_i)≤1, then Dil_k(L)≤1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: In the special case, if F_i is uniformly ε-close to L, then F_i(B^k) covers L(B^k) except possibly the ε-neighborhood of L(S^{k−1}); this uses Brouwer degree or winding number for maps of the boundary into R^k minus a point. Letting ε→0 gives Vol_k L(B^k)≤limsup Vol_k F_i(B^k)≤Vol_k(B^k), hence Dil_k(L)≤1. For the general case, assume dF_x has k-dilation greater than 1 on some k-plane P and project its image to the k-plane Q=dF_x(P). Rescale around x by factors 2^i and project F_i to Q, obtaining maps G_i:P→Q with k-dilation≤1 that converge on the unit ball to dF_x|_P. The special case gives a contradiction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 13.4, mixed dilation lower bound for S^4→S^3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If F:S^4→S^3 has nontrivial Steenrod-Hopf invariant, then Dil_2(F) Dil_3(F) ≥ c > 0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For such an F, the associated Steenrod cycle Z(F) is a 6-cycle in Γ_1S^3 and is homologically nontrivial, so its 6-volume is bounded below. Proposition 6.4 bounds each directed volume Vol_{(a,b,c)}Z(F) by C Dil_b(F)Dil_c(F). Since a≤1 and b,c≤3 with a+b+c=6, the only possible pairs (b,c) are (2,3), (3,2), and (3,3), giving Vol Z(F)≤C Dil_2(F)Dil_3(F)+C Dil_3(F)^2. Combining this with the nontrivial homology lower bound, and the already available positive lower bound for Dil_2 on nontrivial maps, yields the stated product lower bound.

proof_techniques.name: Singular-value bookkeeping for k-dilation
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly reduces k-dilation estimates to inequalities among singular values of the derivative. The basic identity is ||Λ^k dF||=s1⋯sk, and monotonicity Dil_k^{1/k}≥Dil_l^{1/l} follows from the ordering of singular values. Product maps are treated by observing that the singular values of a product derivative are the singular values from each factor combined.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For maps between 4-rectangles with Dil_2≤1, this is the first tool to extract pointwise control on 3- and 4-dimensional Jacobians and to translate area contraction into inequalities for volumes of images of chains or slices.

proof_techniques.name: Topological invariant as a geometric cycle
proof_techniques.description: Instead of only expressing invariants such as the Hopf invariant through differential forms, the paper builds an explicit cycle Z(F) from a fixed chain in a product or twisted product and the induced product map. The homology class of this cycle is exactly the topological invariant. Small dilation then forces the cycle to have small volume or small directed bad volume, contradicting nontrivial homology.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A degree-one map of pairs between rectangles can often be tested by pushing forward fundamental chains or sweepout cycles. The same philosophy suggests encoding degree as a nontrivial homology class and using Dil_2 plus side-length hypotheses to force a forbidden small representative.

proof_techniques.name: Directed volumes in product spaces
proof_techniques.description: The paper refines volume estimates by decomposing a d-chain in A×B×C into directional contributions Vol_{(a,b,c)} according to how many tangent directions lie in each factor. Product maps then satisfy Vol_{(a,b,c)}(Φ_A×Φ_B×Φ_C)(T) ≤ Dil_a(Φ_A)Dil_b(Φ_B)Dil_c(Φ_C)Vol_{(a,b,c)}(T). This anisotropic accounting is essential when total volume is not controlled but certain directional components are small.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Rectangles are highly anisotropic, and the desired conclusion involves mixed products of side lengths rather than only total volume. Directed-volume bookkeeping is a natural way to track which coordinate directions or slices are forced to expand under a degree-one map.

proof_techniques.name: Good/bad direction splitting
proof_techniques.description: For the Steenrod cycle, directions with nearly equal contributions from two target factors are called bad; small k-dilation controls exactly these bad components. If bad components vanish, the double cover of the cycle splits into two separate cycles, each living in unbalanced directions, and one proves null-homology. If bad components are merely small, a surgery argument cuts and caps the small bridge.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A rectangle proof may similarly distinguish coordinate directions whose expansion would violate Dil_2 from directions that can be handled topologically or by projection. This is especially relevant to proving alternatives involving R3R4 and total volume-type expressions.

proof_techniques.name: Perpendicular-pair surgery
proof_techniques.description: The perpendicular-pair inequality solves the following geometric problem: if ∂y=z+w and z,w have disjoint directed supports, then z can be filled with Hausdorff content controlled by Vol(y). The proof combines intersection-number estimates, dyadic Federer-Fleming deformations, thick-region localization, and probabilistic choices of local grids. In the paper this inequality supplies the small caps needed to separate the two sheets of the double cover of Z(F).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a rectangle argument produces two boundary cycles connected by a small-volume chain, and their coordinate-direction supports are incompatible, this inequality gives a way to fill one side cheaply and derive a homological contradiction.

proof_techniques.name: Small Hausdorff content implies null-homology
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses a Federer-Fleming deformation theorem in Hausdorff-content form: on a compact Riemannian manifold, an n-cycle with sufficiently small n-dimensional Hausdorff content is null-homologous. The proof pushes the cycle skeleton-by-skeleton in a triangulation using radial projections that control Hausdorff content.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This can turn quantitative smallness of a pushed-forward chain or sweepout in the target rectangle into a topological contradiction with degree one, even when volume estimates are too weak or chains are crumpled.

proof_techniques.name: Intersection-number estimates from thinness
proof_techniques.description: The intersection-number lemma says that if an n-chain y has little volume in a ball, then random translates of an axis-parallel complementary rectangle usually have zero mod 2 intersection with a boundary component z, provided the two boundary components are perpendicular in directed volume. This converts local volume thinness into topological vanishing of cubical deformation coefficients.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For rectangles, random coordinate slices and intersection numbers with fibers are a plausible way to detect degree. Thinness estimates may help prove that certain slices cannot carry the required topological intersections under Dil_2 constraints.

proof_techniques.name: Federer-Fleming deformation by dual cubical intersections
proof_techniques.description: The deformation operator D_v(T)=Σ_F[̄F_v∩T]F replaces a chain by a cubical chain whose coefficients are intersection numbers with dual faces. It commutes with boundary, preserves already cubical chains under small translations, controls average volume, and provides controlled homologies between a cycle and its cubical approximation.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a standard way to discretize chains in a rectangle while preserving homological information. It is particularly useful when side lengths suggest choosing a cubical grid adapted to the target or domain dimensions.

proof_techniques.name: Partial-product rectangle embedding and folding
proof_techniques.description: Appendix 14.2 proves that if every initial partial product of the target rectangle R dominates the corresponding partial product of S, then S embeds locally bilipschitzly into R. The proof is constructive: repeated two-dimensional folding adjusts adjacent side lengths while preserving product inequalities, then induction handles the remaining coordinates.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original statement is entirely about side-length product inequalities for 4-rectangles. This lemma is directly relevant for understanding sharpness, constructing comparison maps, or recognizing when a collection of product inequalities is sufficient for bounded-distortion embeddings.

proof_techniques.name: Blow-up compactness for bounded k-dilation
proof_techniques.description: The paper records that uniform C0 limits of maps with Dil_k≤1 retain Dil_k≤1 at every differentiability point. The proof rescales around a point and uses Brouwer degree to show a limiting linear map cannot expand k-volume.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If one proves the rectangle theorem by contradiction using a sequence of counterexamples and rescaling or limiting, this result justifies passing the Dil_2≤1 condition to a differentiable limit.

proof_techniques.name: Differential forms as k-dilation tests
proof_techniques.description: For any k-form α, the pullback satisfies ||F^*α||∞≤Dil_k(F)||α||∞. The paper uses this for degree and recalls its use in Gromov's Hopf invariant inequality, where a primitive of F^*ω is estimated analytically.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For degree-one maps of rectangles, integrating pulled-back coordinate volume forms or forms adapted to slices can yield immediate necessary inequalities, and may serve as a baseline before more refined width estimates.

other_useful_info: The paper explicitly cites Guth's PhD thesis [GUT], Area-contracting maps between rectangles, as related prior work; that citation is likely the most direct source for the original rectangle theorem. Section 12 recalls the Tsui-Wang theorem: maps S^m→S^n with Dil_2<1 are contractible for m,n≥2, illustrating the strength of area-contraction in topological problems. Section 13 discusses Uryson width questions and conjectures for metrics satisfying Λ^k g≤Λ^k g0; this is philosophically close to rectangle width-volume obstructions, although the conjectural statements are not proved in this paper. The paper emphasizes that small k-dilation is highly nonlinear: maps with tiny 2-dilation can be surjective in some dimension-lowering settings, so lower-bound proofs must use topology, directed volume, or sweepout-type information rather than linear intuition alone.
why_relevant: This paper studies when small k-dilation is obstructed by topology, especially for area-contraction. It supplies a modern toolkit connecting Dil_2 bounds, Steenrod-square-type obstructions, and quantitative geometric measure theory.

title: Area-Contracting Maps Between Rectangles
overall_summary: Guth's thesis develops sharp-up-to-constants lower and upper bounds for k-dilation phenomena between rectangles, with a complete solution for (n-1)-dilation diffeomorphisms and for k-expanding embeddings of rectangles. The core lower-bound methods are k-width, width-volume inequalities, packing many wide subsets, slicing/isoperimetric arguments, and filling-volume obstructions for cycles; the core upper-bound methods are nonlinear snake/folding/pinching maps. The material most directly relevant to the original 4-dimensional 2-dilation problem is Chapter 8, especially the lower bounds obtained by restricting a 2-contracting map to the boundary and identifying rectangular boundaries with ellipsoids, plus the solid-torus/filling-area argument that yields the alternative R_3 R_4 versus a mixed volume-type lower bound. Chapters 2, 3, and 6 provide the reusable width-volume and rectangle-packing machinery that underlies those lower bounds, while Chapter 8 gives the specific 4-dimensional estimates and constructions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.1.1, snake map in dimension 3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R and S be 3-dimensional rectangles with ordered side lengths R_1 ≤ R_2 ≤ R_3 and S_1 ≤ S_2 ≤ S_3. If R_1 = S_1, R_2 > S_2, and R_2 R_3 > S_2 S_3, then there is a diffeomorphism from R to S with 2-dilation bounded above by a dimensional constant C.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose a smooth quasi-isometric embedding I of the rectangle [0,3S_2]×[0,3S_3] into [0,R_2]×[0,R_3]; this is possible because R_2 ≥ S_2 and R_2 R_3 ≥ S_2 S_3. Let H on [0,3S_2]×[0,3S_3] equal R_1 on the central rectangle [S_2,2S_2]×[S_3,2S_3] and a tiny epsilon near the boundary, with Lipschitz constant <1; pull it forward by I and extend by epsilon outside image(I). First compress R below the graph x=H(y,z) via Phi_1(x,y,z)=(x H(y,z)/R_1,y,z), which is Lipschitz bounded. Next choose a diffeomorphism phi_2 of [0,R_2]×[0,R_3] onto image(I) that is the identity on the central image, nearly a retraction outside it, has large Lipschitz constant only in directions where its smaller singular value is tiny, and maps the complement of image(I) near the boundary of image(I). Define Phi_2(x,y,z)=(x,phi_2(y,z)); although Phi_2 alone may have large 2-dilation, the preceding compression makes the x-direction tiny in exactly the region where phi_2 stretches, so Phi_2∘Phi_1 has 2-dilation bounded by C. Then use I^{-1} on the last two coordinates to map [0,R_1]×image(I) quasi-isometrically into [0,S_1]×[0,3S_2]×[0,3S_3], and finally retract smoothly, with Lipschitz constant arbitrarily close to 1, onto the central copy of S. The composition is a diffeomorphism R to S with uniformly bounded 2-dilation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.1, complete (n-1)-dilation criterion for diffeomorphisms between rectangles
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R and S be n-dimensional rectangles with ordered side lengths R_1 ≤ ... ≤ R_n and S_1 ≤ ... ≤ S_n. If there is an (n-1)-contracting diffeomorphism from R to S, then for every integer l with 1 ≤ l ≤ n-1 one has R_1...R_l (R_{l+1}...R_n)^((n-l-1)/(n-l)) ≥ c S_1...S_l (S_{l+1}...S_n)^((n-l-1)/(n-l)), and also R_2...R_n ≥ c S_2...S_n, where c>0 depends only on n. Conversely, if these inequalities hold with a sufficiently large dimensional constant C in place of c, Guth constructs an (n-1)-contracting diffeomorphism from R to S. Equivalently, if Q_i=S_i/R_i, the minimum possible (n-1)-dilation is comparable to the maximum of Q_1...Q_l(Q_{l+1}...Q_n)^((n-l-1)/(n-l)) for 1≤l≤n-1 and Q_2...Q_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The upper-bound construction is by composing linear maps with generalized snake maps. The basic 3-dimensional snake map is extended by taking products with identity maps, and algebraic induction reduces the stated inequalities to successive situations where one can trade length in one long direction for length in another direction while keeping (n-1)-dilation bounded. The lower bounds use the notion of width, i.e. the least maximal (n-1)-volume of fibers of a generic PL map to R. A rectangle R has width comparable to R_1...R_{n-1}; this gives the l=n-1 inequality by monotonicity of width under (n-1)-contracting diffeomorphisms. For l<n-1, pack S with N approximately equal to S_{l+1}...S_n/S_l^{n-l} disjoint subrectangles of dimensions S_1×...×S_l×S_l×...×S_l, each of width comparable to S_1...S_l S_l^{n-l-1}. Their inverse images in R are disjoint subsets of R with at least that width. The rectangle-relative width-volume inequality bounds the volume needed for any subset of R with that width, so summing these lower volume bounds over the N inverse images and comparing with Vol(R) gives the displayed inequalities. The final inequality R_2...R_n ≥ c S_2...S_n follows by applying (n-1)-contraction to the boundary volume.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.2.4, width-volume inequality inside a rectangle for codimension-one width
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let U be an open subset of an n-dimensional rectangle R with ordered side lengths R_1≤...≤R_n. For each integer l with 0≤l≤n-1, the codimension-one width of U satisfies Width(U) ≤ C (R_1...R_l)^(1/(n-l)) Vol(U)^((n-l-1)/(n-l)), where C depends only on n. For l=0 this is Width(U)≤C Vol(U)^((n-1)/n); for l=n-1 it reduces to Width(U)≤C R_1...R_{n-1}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Approximate the characteristic function of U by a continuous compactly supported function f with 0≤f≤1. Average f over the first l coordinate directions to obtain a function g on the (n-l)-dimensional rectangle R_{l+1}×...×R_n, normalized by g(y)=(R_1...R_l)^{-1}∫ f(x,y) dx. Apply the Euclidean width-volume inequality to g in dimension n-l to find a PL function whose level sets have g-integral bounded by C(∫g)^((n-l-1)/(n-l)). Pull this function back to R by ignoring the first l coordinates. A fiber in R is the product of the first l-coordinate rectangle with a fiber downstairs, so the U-volume on each fiber is multiplied by R_1...R_l, giving exactly the stated exponent.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.1.1 and corollaries, lower bounds for k-width
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For 1≤k≤n, define the k-width of an n-dimensional Riemannian domain U as the infimum of W such that there is a generic PL map π:U→R^{n-k} whose fibers all have k-volume at most W. Gromov's argument gives that the k-width of the unit n-cube is bounded below by a positive dimensional constant c(n). Consequently, the k-width of an n-dimensional rectangle R with ordered side lengths R_1≤...≤R_n is comparable to R_1...R_k: it is at least c R_1...R_k and at most R_1...R_k.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the codimension-one case, a PL function on the unit ball has some level set cutting the ball into two equal-volume regions, and the Euclidean isoperimetric inequality forces that level set to have positive (n-1)-volume. For general k, Gromov's proof uses a fine triangulation of the parameter space R^{n-k}. If all k-dimensional fibers had very small k-volume, fill the inverse images of vertices by small (k+1)-chains using the isoperimetric inequality, then inductively fill cycles associated to edges, faces, and higher-dimensional simplices. At the end, the sum over top-dimensional simplices gives an n-cycle homologous to the fundamental cycle of the cube, so one of the constructed cycles must have volume at least 1, contradicting smallness unless W≥c(n). For rectangles, apply a linear map from R to a cube with k-dilation (R_1...R_k)^{-1} for the lower bound, and use projection onto the last n-k coordinates for the upper bound.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.1, Euclidean width-volume inequality for k-width
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f be a continuous compactly supported function on R^n with 0≤f≤1. Define the k-width of f as the least W for which there is a generic PL map π:R^n→R^{n-k} such that ∫_{π^{-1}(y)} f ≤ W for all y. Then k-width(f) ≤ C (∫_{R^n} f)^{k/n}, where C depends only on n and k. In particular, if U is a bounded open subset of R^n, then k-width(U) ≤ C Vol(U)^{k/n}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: After rescaling, reduce to ∫f=1. Choose a translate S of the k-skeleton of the unit cubical lattice such that ∫_S f is bounded; this follows by averaging over translations. Construct a periodic PL homeomorphism Ψ that fixes S and pushes almost all of space into a tiny neighborhood of S, except for a tiny neighborhood of the dual (n-k-1)-skeleton. On each good simplex, Ψ maps almost every k-plane to a k-plane almost parallel to a k-face of S; on the bad region the total k-volume contribution is bounded because a generic k-plane intersects only boundedly many bad simplices on the support of f. Choose a generic linear projection π_0:R^n→R^{n-k} whose fibers have the good-angle property for all finitely many simplex types, and set π=π_0∘Ψ^{-1}. Each fiber Ψ(P) is mostly close and parallel to S, so its f-integral is controlled by ∫_S f, plus a bounded contribution from the bad region where f≤1. This proves the normalized estimate, and scaling gives the general formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.2.1, rectangle-relative k-width upper bound
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let U be an open subset of an n-dimensional rectangle R with ordered side lengths R_1≤...≤R_n. For each integer l with 0≤l≤k, the k-width of U satisfies W_k(U) ≤ C (R_1...R_l)^((n-k)/(n-l)) Vol(U)^((k-l)/(n-l)), where C depends only on n and k.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The cases l=0 and l=k are respectively the Euclidean width-volume inequality and the trivial bound by the k-width of R. For intermediate l, approximate the characteristic function of U by f and average over the first l coordinate directions to get g on the (n-l)-dimensional rectangle R_{l+1}×...×R_n. Apply the Euclidean width-volume theorem to g with k replaced by k-l, obtaining a map to R^{n-k} whose fibers have controlled g-integral. Pull back this map to R by ignoring the first l coordinates. Multiplying the fiber estimate by R_1...R_l gives the stated powers of R_1...R_l and Vol(U).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.1, isoperimetric inequality using packings by wide boundary subsets
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If U is a bounded open set in R^n with smooth boundary, then there exist disjoint subsets S_i of ∂U such that Vol(U) ≤ C Σ_i W_{n-2}(S_i)^{n/(n-2)}, where W_{n-2} denotes (n-2)-width and C depends only on n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Call a ball B(p,R) good if W_{n-2}(∂U∩B(p,R)) ≥ c R^{n-2}. For each p∈U, construct maps Φ_R from R^n to the unit n-sphere that send B(p,R) to the complement of the south pole by an exponential-type map and send the outside to the south pole. Put a metric g on ∂U×(0,∞) that, on the slice R, is essentially (π/R) times the given metric on ∂U∩B(p,R), tiny outside that set, and very large in the R-direction. The resulting map to S^n has degree 1 after truncating R to [A^{-1},A] and pinching the boundary slices to points. Since a nonzero-degree map to the sphere forces positive (n-2)-width, the slicing lemma implies some slice ∂U×{R} has (n-2)-width at least c in the rescaled metric, hence ∂U∩B(p,R) has width at least cR^{n-2}. Thus every p∈U lies in some good ball. Apply the Vitali covering lemma to select disjoint good balls B(p_i,R_i) with Σ R_i^n ≥ c Vol(U); set S_i=∂U∩B(p_i,R_i), and use W_{n-2}(S_i)≥cR_i^{n-2}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.1, slicing inequality for codimension-one width of a cylinder
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be an m-dimensional manifold and let g be a Riemannian metric on M×[0,1]. Let g_t be the restriction of g to the slice M×{t}. Then W_{m-1}(M×[0,1],g) ≤ 2 sup_{t∈[0,1]} W_{m-1}(M,g_t), where W_{m-1} denotes codimension-one width.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each t choose a generic PL function π_t on M whose level sets have (m-1)-volume at most W, where W is slightly above sup_t W_{m-1}(M,g_t). If π_t varied continuously in t, the map (x,t)↦(π_t(x),t) would give the desired slicing. To handle discontinuity, discretize t into many intervals and choose finitely many maps π_i valid for slightly enlarged metric ranges. Between two generic PL maps π_0 and π_1, after reparameterizing their images into (0,1), use the explicit homotopy F_t(x)=min(π_0(x)+t, π_1(x)+1-t). Each fiber of F_t is contained in the union of one fiber of π_0 and one fiber of π_1, hence has volume at most 2W. Patching these homotopies over the discretization gives the result.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.1, width-volume inequality for a one-parameter family inside a rectangle
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R be an n-dimensional rectangle with ordered side lengths R_1≤...≤R_n, let f be a continuous compactly supported function on R×R with 0≤f≤1, and write points as (x,y), where x∈R and y∈R. For every δ>0 there is a generic PL map π:R×R→R^{n-k}×R such that, for every fiber π^{-1}(z,y) and every integer l with 0≤l≤k, one has ∫_{π^{-1}(z,y)} f ≤ C (R_1...R_l)^((n-k)/(n-l)) (δ+∫_{R×{y}} f)^((k-l)/(n-l)); moreover π^{-1}(z,y) lies within δ of the slice R×{y}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is a parameterized version of the rectangle-relative width-volume inequality. The proof modifies the skeleton-squeezing argument by using a cubical scaffold whose local scale L(y) depends on the mass δ+∫_{R×{y}}f. A very large metric in the parameter y-direction makes L almost constant on the relevant local balls. A barycentric wiggling argument chooses the scaffold so that the integral of f over each k-face is controlled by the local mass near that face. Then a PL squeezing map pushes most fibers near this scaffold, and a carefully chosen PL projection, close to projection in the y-coordinate, ensures that fibers stay δ-close to R×{y}. Averaging over the first l rectangle coordinates gives the R_1...R_l factor, exactly as in Proposition 3.2.1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6.1, k-expanding embeddings of rectangles
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R and S be n-dimensional rectangles with ordered side lengths R_1≤...≤R_n and S_1≤...≤S_n. If there is a k-expanding embedding of S into R, meaning an embedding that does not decrease the k-volume of any k-dimensional submanifold, then for every integer l with 0≤l≤k and every integer p with k+1≤p≤n one has R_1...R_l (R_{l+1}...R_p)^((k-l)/(p-l)) ≥ c S_1...S_l (S_{l+1}...S_p)^((k-l)/(p-l)). Conversely, if all these inequalities hold with a sufficiently large dimensional constant C in place of c, Guth constructs a k-expanding embedding of S into R.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For necessity, Guth proves a stronger statement for thick neighborhoods of lower-dimensional rectangular lattice skeleta. He defines the k-width of a system of bump functions around an embedded complex X: roughly, every degree-one nice family of k-cycles must have thick shadows overlapping the faces of X in the pattern dictated by X. A lattice-neighborhood theorem shows that an L-neighborhood of such a complex has k-width around X at least cL^k. Push the associated bump functions into R under the assumed k-expanding embedding. Average over the first l coordinates of R and apply a multiple-family width-volume inequality to build a degree-one family of p-cycles in R such that each p-cycle meets only a controlled number of faces of the embedded complex. A combinatorial width lemma for the (n-p)-skeleton of a rectangular lattice says that any degree-one family of p-cycles must meet at least c times the relevant product of lattice dimensions. Comparing the upper and lower face counts yields the displayed inequality. For sufficiency, an algebra lemma produces a rectangle T and a k-contracting linear map R→T such that the partial products of T dominate those of S; then standard quasi-isometric folding of S into T gives the desired k-expanding embedding into R.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6.2, k-width around a rectangular lattice complex
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be the q-skeleton of an m-dimensional rectangular cubical lattice embedded in R^n as part of a cubical lattice of side length L, and let U be the L/3-neighborhood of X. Then the k-width of U around X, in Guth's sense of systems of bump functions attached to faces of X and tested against nice degree-one families of k-cycles, is at least c L^k, with c depending only on n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: After scaling L=1, define for each face F of X a small neighborhood U(F) around the center of F and a smaller U'(F), arranged so that U(F) and U(G) overlap only when one face is contained in the other and so that the union of U'(G) over subfaces G of F covers F. Choose bump functions b(F) supported in U(F), and set B(F) to be the sum over subfaces of F, with constants so that Σ_F B(F)≤1. Given any nice degree-one family C of k-cycles, triangulate the parameter space finely and define thick shadows t_w(F). Guth inductively modifies C over the faces of the parameter triangulation, face dimension by face dimension and complex dimension by complex dimension, by performing small surgeries inside U(F) whenever a parameter simplex misses the thick shadow of F. The surgery replaces portions crossing U'(F) by fillings in U(F) avoiding all subface neighborhoods that must be avoided; smallness follows because the relevant intersections have k-volume O(w). The final family C' agrees with C outside a compact set and avoids each face F whenever the parameter point is outside the thick shadow of B(F), which is exactly the definition that the k-width around X is at least w.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.2.1, width-volume estimate for multiple families of functions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let B'(F) be a finite collection of continuous compactly supported functions on R^{n-l}, written with coordinates (x,y)∈R^{p-l}×R^{n-p}, satisfying 0≤B'(F)≤1 and ∫_{R^{p-l}×{y}} Σ_F B'(F) ≤ M for every y. For a generic PL map π:R^{n-l}→R^{n-k}, write coordinates on the target as (z,y)∈R^{p-k}×R^{n-p}, and let N_W(y) be the number of faces F for which ∫_{π^{-1}(z,y)} B'(F) ≥ W for some z. Then for every W>0 there exists such a π with N_W(y) ≤ C W^{-(p-l)/(k-l)} M for every y.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By scaling reduce to W=1 and rescale y so the functions vary slowly in the y-direction. Use a cubical k-skeleton of fixed small scale s, choose a barycentric wiggle so that in every slab near a fiber, only O(M) functions have appreciable integral on the skeleton, and choose a projection whose fibers are transverse to the bad region and mapped nearly tangent to the skeleton in the good region. As in the proof of the width-volume inequality, each π-fiber is controlled by its skeleton contribution plus a uniformly small bad-region contribution; choosing s sufficiently small forces all functions except those in a controlled exceptional set to have fiber integral <1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.3.1, combinatorial width of a rectangular lattice skeleton
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be the (n-p)-skeleton of a rectangular cubical lattice in R^m with side counts N_1≤...≤N_m, embedded in R^n. Let C be a nice degree-nonzero family of p-cycles in R^n parameterized by R^{n-p}. Then at least one p-cycle in the family intersects X in at least c N_1...N_{m+p-n} faces. In the special case m=n, this lower bound is c N_1...N_p.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First reduce to the standard embedding of the skeleton in a rectangular region of the unit lattice by embedding in high codimension if necessary and intersecting the family with the embedded R^m. Suppose every cycle meets fewer than W faces. Using a fine triangulation of the parameter space, inductively surger the family so that each chain C'(A) over a parameter simplex A is covered by O(W) unit cubes of the rectangular lattice, while preserving degree. The surgery removes intersections with illegal faces of increasing dimension and replaces local products K×B by L×∂B, where L fills K inside the face. Next use a coarse isoperimetric inequality for rectilinear cycles: any k-cycle covered by M cubes in the rectangle fills by a (k+1)-chain covered by O(M N_{k+1}) cubes, obtained by pushing from a point and averaging. Applying Gromov's inductive filling argument over the parameter triangulation, the top-dimensional cycles D(A) are covered by O(W N_{p+1}...N_n) cubes. Since their sum represents a nonzero multiple of the fundamental class, at least one must cover all the rectangle, forcing W≥c N_1...N_p.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.5.1, lower bounds for k-dilation of rectangle diffeomorphisms from expanding-embedding inequalities
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R and S be n-dimensional rectangles and put Q_i=S_i/R_i. If f:R→S is a diffeomorphism, then its k-dilation is at least c D_{l,p} for every 0≤l≤k and k+1≤p≤n, where D_{l,p}=Q_1...Q_l (Q_{l+1}...Q_p)^((k-l)/(p-l)). Also, its k-dilation is at least c D'_{l,p} for every 1≤l≤k+1 and k+2≤p≤n, where D'_{l,p}=Q_2...Q_l (Q_{l+1}...Q_p)^((k-l+1)/(p-l)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If f has k-dilation D, then after scaling the domain by D^{1/k}, f becomes k-contracting from D^{1/k}R to S, and its inverse is a k-expanding embedding of S into D^{1/k}R. Applying Theorem 6.1 gives D≥cD_{l,p}. For the primed inequalities, restrict f to the boundary. The boundary of a rectangle is bilipschitz to the double of its largest face, so the same expanding-embedding argument applies in dimension n-1 with side lengths R_2,...,R_n and S_2,...,S_n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 8.1.1, 2-contracting diffeomorphisms between 3-dimensional ellipsoids
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let E and F be 3-dimensional ellipsoids in R^4 with principal axes R_1≤R_2≤R_3≤R_4 and S_1≤S_2≤S_3≤S_4. Up to dimensional multiplicative constants, there is a 2-contracting diffeomorphism from E to F only if the following hold: B1: R_2 R_3 ≥ c S_2 S_3; B2: R_2 R_3 R_4 ≥ c S_2^2 S_3 S_4; B3a: if R_2^2 ≥ S_2 S_3 up to constants, then R_2 R_3 R_4 ≥ c S_2^{1/2} S_3^{3/2} S_4; B3b: if R_2^2 ≤ S_2 S_3 up to constants, then R_3 R_4 ≥ c S_3 S_4. Conversely, if the analogous inequalities hold with large constants, Guth constructs a diffeomorphism E→F with 2-dilation bounded by a dimensional constant.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For B1, the 2-width of E is comparable to R_2R_3: an upper bound comes from slicing by the long coordinate, and a lower bound comes from a rectangular region R_2×R_3×R_4 inside E. Since 2-width cannot increase under a 2-contracting diffeomorphism, R_2R_3≥cS_2S_3. For B2, F contains N≈S_3S_4/S_2^2 disjoint regions quasi-isometric to S_2-cubes. Their inverse images in E each have 2-width at least cS_2^2. The width-volume inequality inside E gives volume at least c R_2^{-1}S_2^4 for each inverse image. Summing over N disjoint inverse images and comparing with Vol(E)≈R_2R_3R_4 yields R_2R_3R_4≥cS_2^2S_3S_4. For B3, F contains a solid torus quasi-isometric to a circle of length S_2 times a rectangle S_3×S_4. Circles lying over the central part of the S_3×S_4 rectangle have filling area at least cS_2S_3 in F. Their inverse images in E have the same lower filling-area obstruction. If R_2^2>S_2S_3, then any curve in E with length <cR_2 lies in a nearly Euclidean ball and fills with area O(L^2), so the inverse-image curves have length at least c(S_2S_3)^{1/2}; because transverse 2-area to the circle family is not decreased by the inverse of a 2-contracting diffeomorphism, the inverse image of the solid torus has volume at least c(S_2S_3)^{1/2}S_3S_4, giving B3a after comparing with Vol(E). If R_2^2<S_2S_3, the same argument gives length at least cR_2 and hence R_3R_4≥cS_3S_4. For the converse, Guth treats the two cases separately: in the R_2^2>S_2S_3 case he maps E, after removing a line and using an expanding embedding of a suitable rectangle, to an intermediate ellipsoid G which has a bounded 2-dilation map to F; in the other case, inequalities reduce to Theorem 2.1 for rectangles R_2×R_3×R_4 and S_2×S_3×S_4, and doubling gives the ellipsoid diffeomorphism.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 8.1.2, filling estimate for small relative 2-cycles in a 4-rectangle
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R be a 4-dimensional rectangle with ordered side lengths R_1≤R_2≤R_3≤R_4. If C is an oriented relative rectifiable 2-cycle in R with area less than (1/2)R_1R_2, then C has an oriented filling 3-chain with volume less than C R_1 R_2^2, where C is dimensional.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Approximate C by a rectilinear relative 2-cycle C' in a very fine cubical subdivision, with arbitrarily small filling error, and arrange that the projection of C' to the (x_1,x_2)-plane has area at most (2/3)R_1R_2. Let S be the complement of this projection in [0,R_1]×[0,R_2], so area(S)≥(1/3)R_1R_2. Guth uses the rectilinear coning construction from Chapter 7: for a point p, define F_p(A) for each coordinate-parallel face A by coning or pushing A toward the boundary in the first coordinate direction not tangent to A, extended linearly over C'. For a random p, the average filling volume is bounded by a directional expression. Choosing p with first two coordinates in S improves the estimate because p avoids the projection of C'; the average volume is then at most C area(C') R_2. Since area(C')≤R_1R_2, some p gives a filling of volume at most C R_1R_2^2. Adding back the negligible approximation filling gives the result for C.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 8.1.3, lower bound from filling areas of coordinate 2-planes
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R and S be 4-dimensional rectangles with ordered side lengths. If there is a 2-contracting diffeomorphism from R to S and if C R_1R_2 < S_1S_2S_3 for a sufficiently large dimensional constant C, then R_3R_4 ≥ c S_3S_4.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each point (y_3,y_4) in the central subrectangle [(1/3)S_3,(2/3)S_3]×[(1/3)S_4,(2/3)S_4], consider the relative 2-cycle P_{y_3,y_4}=[0,S_1]×[0,S_2]×{y_3}×{y_4} in S. This 2-plane has filling 3-volume at least cS_1S_2S_3, because any relative filling must cross a definite distance in the x_3 direction. Its inverse image under the diffeomorphism has filling volume at least the same amount in R. By the preceding small-cycle filling estimate, the inverse-image 2-cycle must have area at least cR_1R_2. The inverse images of the family of 2-planes fiber the set U=f^{-1}([0,S_1]×[0,S_2]×central S_3S_4 rectangle). Since the map is 2-contracting, the transverse 2-area to this family is at least the S_3S_4 area of the parameter rectangle. Hence Vol(U)≥c R_1R_2 S_3S_4. As U⊂R and Vol(R)=R_1R_2R_3R_4, cancellation gives R_3R_4≥cS_3S_4. The printed proof invokes Proposition 8.1.2 at precisely this step.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 8.1, unit 4-cube to 4-rectangle criterion up to constants
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S be a 4-dimensional rectangle with ordered side lengths S_1≤S_2≤S_3≤S_4. If there is a 2-contracting diffeomorphism from the unit 4-cube to S, then S_2 S_3 S_4^2 ≤ C. Conversely, if S_2 S_3 S_4^2 ≤ c, then Guth constructs a 2-contracting diffeomorphism from the unit 4-cube to S.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the lower bound, restrict the diffeomorphism to the boundary and use Proposition 8.1.1 for ellipsoids. With R_i=1, B1 gives S_2S_3≤C. Since R_2^2=1 is then in the B3a regime up to constants, B3a gives 1≥c S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}S_4. Combining this with S_2≤S_3 yields S_2S_3S_4^2≤C. For the construction, Guth uses the pinching map of Section 8.2: choose A=S_2^{1/2}S_3^{1/2} and B=S_2^{-1/2}S_3^{1/2}S_4. Under S_2S_3S_4^2 small, the unit cube admits a bounded 2-dilation degree-one pinching map to A×A×A×B, and there is a 2-contracting linear map from A×A×A×B to S.

proof_techniques.name: Snake map and quasi-isometric folding
proof_techniques.description: The snake map is Guth's basic nonlinear construction showing that k-dilation is not controlled by linear maps. In dimension 3, a long thin rectangle is quasi-isometrically embedded as a winding strip inside another rectangle; the map first compresses the domain under a graph, then retracts most of the cross-section into the strip, and finally unwraps the strip. The large derivative of the retraction occurs where another singular value has been made tiny, so the product of the largest two singular values remains bounded.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Although the original problem asks for a lower bound, the snake construction indicates which inequalities are plausibly sharp and why products such as R_3R_4, rather than individual side lengths, are natural. It also explains why one should not try to compare only linear maps between the rectangles.

proof_techniques.name: k-width as a monotone obstruction to k-contracting maps
proof_techniques.description: The k-width of a domain is defined using generic PL maps to R^{n-k}; it measures the least possible maximal k-volume of fibers in a sweepout. If a map is k-contracting and sufficiently well behaved, k-width of images cannot exceed k-width of preimages. Guth combines lower bounds for the k-width of rectangles and cubes with upper bounds for subsets of rectangles in terms of their volume.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a degree-one 2-contracting map R→S, one can attempt to sweep or pack parts of S by 2-wide objects and pull them back to R. This is the basic mechanism behind many product inequalities involving side lengths.

proof_techniques.name: Width-volume inequality via skeleton squeezing
proof_techniques.description: The width-volume theorem is proved by selecting a translate of the cubical k-skeleton on which the integral of a function f is controlled, then constructing a PL homeomorphism that squeezes almost all of space into a tiny neighborhood of that skeleton. A generic projection chosen at good angles gives fibers that are mostly near the skeleton, and the remaining bad part has uniformly controlled k-volume. This yields W_k(U)≤C Vol(U)^{k/n}, and the rectangle-relative version is obtained by averaging over short coordinate directions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is the key estimate for converting a lower bound on the 2-width of a preimage into a lower bound on its volume inside a 4-rectangle, which is often the missing step in degree/packing arguments.

proof_techniques.name: Packing many wide subsets
proof_techniques.description: Guth repeatedly packs the target rectangle with many disjoint subrectangles or cube-like regions whose k-width is known from below. Under a k-contracting diffeomorphism, their inverse images are disjoint subsets of the domain with the same lower width. The width-volume inequality then forces each inverse image to have a certain volume, and summing over all of them yields product inequalities among the side lengths.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A proof of the original dichotomy can try to pack S by 2-dimensional or 3-dimensional families adapted to the S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}S_4 expression, then show that if R_3R_4 is not large, the inverse images consume too much volume.

proof_techniques.name: Boundary reduction to ellipsoids
proof_techniques.description: The boundary of an n-dimensional rectangle is bilipschitz, with constants depending only on n, to an (n-1)-dimensional ellipsoid whose semiaxes are comparable to the side lengths of the rectangle. Guth uses this to transfer lower bounds for maps between ellipsoids to lower bounds for diffeomorphisms between rectangles by restricting to the boundary.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original map is relative degree 1, so its boundary behavior should carry degree information. The Chapter 8 ellipsoid inequalities, especially Proposition 8.1.1, are directly aligned with the products R_3R_4 and S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}S_4 appearing in the desired conclusion.

proof_techniques.name: Solid-torus family of circles and filling-area obstruction
proof_techniques.description: In Proposition 8.1.1, Guth embeds in the target ellipsoid a solid torus modeled on a circle of length S_2 over a rectangle S_3×S_4. Circles over the central part have filling area at least cS_2S_3. Their inverse images under a 2-contracting diffeomorphism must retain this filling-area lower bound. An isoperimetric estimate in the source ellipsoid then forces each inverse-image circle to have length at least either c(S_2S_3)^{1/2} or cR_2, depending on whether R_2^2 is larger or smaller than S_2S_3. Combining with transverse-area monotonicity gives the B3a/B3b inequalities.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This technique is the closest match to the dichotomy in the original problem: if the source lacks enough R_3R_4 transverse area, then inverse images of a torus family must be long enough to force a larger total volume.

proof_techniques.name: Rectilinear filling from a random point avoiding a projection
proof_techniques.description: For relative cycles in a rectangle, Guth approximates by rectilinear chains and fills by a coordinate coning construction F_p depending on a point p. Averaging p gives directional control of the filling volume. In Proposition 8.1.2, if a 2-cycle has small area, its projection to the first two coordinates leaves a large set; choosing p over this complement improves the filling estimate and forces any cycle with large filling volume to have area at least comparable to R_1R_2.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This can be used to show that preimages of coordinate 2-planes in S must have large area in R when their filling volume is large, which is exactly the mechanism in Proposition 8.1.3.

proof_techniques.name: Slicing a degree-one map to the sphere
proof_techniques.description: The proof of the boundary isoperimetric inequality builds a degree-one map from a cylinder of boundary slices into a sphere, with a metric that localizes each slice near ∂U∩B(p,R). A slicing lemma forces some slice to have large width. This converts degree information into the existence of a good radius R at each interior point, followed by Vitali covering.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a proof of the original statement needs to localize a degree-one map to balls or slices, this gives a template for turning global degree into a quantitative width or filling obstruction on some slice.

proof_techniques.name: Area-expanding embedding obstruction via width around complexes
proof_techniques.description: Guth generalizes k-width from open sets to neighborhoods of complexes by attaching bump functions to faces and testing all degree-one families of k-cycles. He then combines a geometric width lower bound for thick lattice skeleta with a combinatorial width lower bound saying that degree-one p-cycle families must meet many faces of an (n-p)-skeleton. This yields the full list of k-expanding embedding inequalities.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Even if the original map is not an embedding, these inequalities suggest which product quantities are forced by 2-dilation alone and can supply auxiliary bounds such as R_1R_2-type or volume-type estimates.

proof_techniques.name: Pinching high-dilation regions to lower-dimensional skeleta
proof_techniques.description: Several Chapter 8 constructions allow maps whose derivative is large on some region because that region is subsequently collapsed into a lower-dimensional set. Since k-dilation ignores directions after rank drops below k, composing a high-stretch retraction with a pinching map can produce bounded 2-dilation. This is used for the pinching and double-pinching maps from 4-rectangles.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: These constructions indicate that lower bounds must use degree, filling, or width in a way robust under collapse to low-dimensional sets; naive pointwise stretching estimates alone cannot prove the desired theorem.

other_useful_info: Throughout the thesis, c and C denote positive constants depending only on the dimension and may change from line to line; all rectangle side lengths are ordered increasingly. Chapter 8 explicitly states that the full problem of determining the least 2-dilation of diffeomorphisms between 4-dimensional rectangles is not solved there, but the lower bounds B1-B3 and Proposition 8.1.3 are proved and are the most relevant rectangle-specific tools. The Chapter 8 lower-bound statements are formulated for diffeomorphisms; however, many proof ingredients are phrased in terms of width, filling area of cycles, transverse area, and degree-type sweepouts, which are plausible to adapt to piecewise smooth degree-one relative maps. Guth repeatedly uses the fact that the boundary of a rectangle is bilipschitz to an ellipsoid with comparable axes, so boundary estimates can be transferred up to dimensional constants. There is a possible consistency issue in the printed Chapter 8 chain from Proposition 8.1.2 to Proposition 8.1.3 concerning the exact power of R_2 in the filling-volume threshold; if porting that argument, verify the exponent in the source estimate carefully rather than relying only on the displayed shorthand.
why_relevant: Guth's thesis is the original source for the sharp up-to-constants estimates for k-dilation of degree-one maps between rectangles. It is the closest match to the stated problem and likely contains the rectangle-specific slicing, tightening, and relative isoperimetric arguments needed here.

title: The Width-Volume Inequality
overall_summary: The paper defines k-width of a Riemannian manifold or Euclidean domain using sweepouts by flat relative k-cycles, and proves the Euclidean width-volume inequality W_k(U) ≤ C(n) Vol(U)^{k/n} for bounded open U ⊂ R^n. The core construction is to bend a family of parallel k-planes around a translated lattice k-skeleton, so that each sweeping cycle lies mostly in the skeleton and has controlled mass. The paper refines this to subsets of rectangles and packages the result as packing-width estimates, then uses these estimates to prove lower bounds for k-dilation of nonzero-degree maps between rectangles. It also proves that the optimal (n−1)-dilation between two n-rectangles is given up to dimensional constants by an explicit maximum of monomials in the side-length ratios, with snake maps giving matching upper bounds. For the original problem, the most directly useful ingredients are the monotonicity of k-width under degree maps with bounded k-dilation, the rectangle-adapted width-volume and packing-width estimates, the lower bounds for D_k(R,S), and the singular-value fact that a map with small 2-dilation also has controlled 3-dilation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 1 monotonicity of k-width under restriction and degree maps
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let W_k(M) denote the k-width of a compact oriented Riemannian n-manifold M, with relative flat k-cycles used when M has boundary; W_k(M) is the infimum over all sweepout families of k-cycles of the supremal k-mass in the family. If M is an open subset of a Riemannian manifold N with the induced metric, then W_k(M) ≤ W_k(N). If f: M → N is a piecewise smooth map of nonzero degree between compact oriented n-manifolds, with the corresponding relative boundary condition in the case of manifolds with boundary, and if the k-dilation of f is at most λ, then λ W_k(M) ≥ W_k(N).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the restriction monotonicity, take a family of flat k-cycles sweeping out N and restrict each integral Lipschitz chain to the open subset M. This restriction map commutes with boundary, is bounded for the flat norm, and sends the gluing homomorphism for N to the relative gluing homomorphism for M, so the restricted family sweeps out M and has no larger mass. For the map monotonicity, push forward a sweeping family F of k-cycles in M by f. Naturality of the gluing homomorphism implies that f(F) sweeps out N whenever f has nonzero degree. Since k-dilation at most λ means that f sends every integral Lipschitz k-chain of mass m to a k-chain of mass at most λm, the width of f(F) is at most λ times the width of F. Taking the infimum over F gives λ W_k(M) ≥ W_k(N).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.1, Gromov non-degeneracy of width
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the standard round metric on S^n, the k-width W_k(S^n) is bounded below by a positive constant c(n) depending only on n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume there is a sweepout family F of k-cycles in S^n with arbitrarily small width. Approximate F by a finite complex of cycles C: to each i-simplex Δ^i in a fine triangulation of the parameter space, associate an integral Lipschitz (k+i)-chain C(Δ^i), with the boundary relation matching the simplicial boundary and with all higher-dimensional chains very small. The gluing homomorphism is nontrivial because F sweeps out S^n. If the vertex cycles have very small mass, the isoperimetric inequality fills each vertex cycle by a (k+1)-chain of small mass. Inductively, after filling all lower-dimensional simplices, each modified chain over an i-simplex becomes a small (k+i)-cycle, hence is null-homologous by the isoperimetric inequality, and can be filled with controlled mass. At the top dimension, a nontrivial glued n-cycle is expressed as a sum of tiny n-cycles, each null-homologous in S^n, contradicting nontriviality. Therefore the width cannot be arbitrarily small.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.2, k-width of a rectangle
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R = [0,R_1] × ... × [0,R_n] be an n-dimensional rectangle with side lengths ordered R_1 ≤ ... ≤ R_n. Then c(n) R_1...R_k ≤ W_k(R) ≤ R_1...R_k.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the upper bound, project R onto its last n−k coordinates. The fibers are k-rectangles with side lengths R_1,...,R_k, volume R_1...R_k, and they form a relative k-cycle sweepout of R. For the lower bound, map R linearly and degree one onto the unit n-cube by x_i ↦ x_i/R_i. This map has k-dilation (R_1...R_k)^{-1}. By monotonicity of width under degree maps, (R_1...R_k)^{-1} W_k(R) ≥ W_k(unit cube). The unit cube contains a round ball or is controlled by the non-degeneracy result, so W_k(unit cube) ≥ c(n), giving W_k(R) ≥ c(n)R_1...R_k.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Construction 1 and Lemma 2.1, bending planes around a skeleton
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let B(R) be the radius-R ball in R^n and let S be the k-skeleton of a unit cubical lattice in R^n. There is a family F of k-cycles sweeping out B(R) such that each cycle in F is contained in S except for a subset of k-mass at most C(n), and the multiplicity with which any cycle uses S is bounded by C(n). The constant C(n) is independent of R. The construction uses the following PL mapping lemma: for each ε>0 there is a piecewise linear proper map Ψ: R^n → R^n, linear on a triangulation whose top simplices are labeled good or bad, such that good simplices are mapped into S, bad simplices lie in the ε-neighborhood of the dual (n−k−1)-skeleton T, every unit ball meets at most C(n) simplices, |Ψ(x)−x|≤C(n), and every simplex has diameter at most C(n).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose an (n−k)-plane P through the origin in general position with respect to the cubical skeleton, and begin with the family F_0 of all k-planes Q perpendicular to P. The desired family is Ψ(F_0). The map Ψ is constructed using the dual skeleton T. For every k-face A of S and every dual (n−k−1)-face B of T in its link, take the convex hull K(A,B); these tiles cover R^n with disjoint interiors. Split K(A,B) into a good part away from B and a bad part lying within O(ε) of B, barycentrically subdivide all pieces, and define Ψ on barycenters of faces so that good pieces collapse into A while bad pieces stretch across K(A,B). The link relation ensures consistency on common faces, and the tile geometry gives the uniform local-complexity, diameter, and displacement bounds independent of ε. Since Ψ is bounded distance from the identity, the straight-line homotopy Ψ_t=(1−t)Ψ+t id is proper, so Ψ has degree one. Good simplex contributions Ψ(Q∩Δ) lie in S; bounded displacement and bounded local simplex count give bounded multiplicity on S. Bad simplices are controlled by transversality: inside a fixed large ball, the dual skeleton lies in finitely many (n−k−1)-planes T_i; for generic P, the projections of T_i to P meet transversely. Taking ε sufficiently small, a fiber Q of the projection to P meets the ε-neighborhood of only O(n) such T_i, hence only O(n) small balls and only C(n) bad simplices. The image of each bad piece has k-volume O(C(n)) because of the diameter and displacement bounds. Thus every Ψ(Q) has only C(n) mass off S and bounded multiplicity on S, and because Ψ has degree one the family sweeps out B(R).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1, width-volume inequality
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let U be a bounded open subset of R^n with Euclidean volume V(U). Then its k-width satisfies W_k(U) < C(n) V(U)^{k/n}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By scaling, reduce to V(U)=1. Translate the unit cubical lattice and let S(x) be its k-skeleton. Averaging over x in a unit cube gives average k-volume |U∩S(x)| equal to a dimensional constant, so choose a translate S with |U∩S|≤C(n). Put U inside a sufficiently large ball B(R) and apply Construction 1 to obtain a sweepout of B(R) by k-cycles each lying in S except for mass C(n), and with multiplicity on S bounded by C(n). Restrict this sweepout to U; restriction preserves the sweepout property for relative cycles. For each cycle E, decompose E∩U into the part in S and the part outside S. The in-skeleton mass is at most C(n)|S∩U|≤C(n), and the off-skeleton mass is at most C(n). Hence every restricted cycle has k-mass at most C(n), proving W_k(U)≤C(n) when V(U)=1; scaling gives the stated exponent k/n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.1, width-volume inequality for functions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f: R^n → [0,1] be compactly supported. Define W_k(f) as the infimum, over k-cycle sweepouts of a large ball containing supp(f), of the supremum of the integrals of f over the cycles, with multiplicity. Then W_k(f) < C(n) (∫_{R^n} f)^{k/n}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: After scaling assume ∫f=1. Translate the unit k-skeleton S so that ∫_S f≤C(n) by the same averaging argument used for sets. Apply the bending construction to sweep a large ball by cycles E that lie in S except for mass C(n), with bounded multiplicity on S. Split E into E_1⊂S and the off-skeleton part E_2. The integral over E_1 is bounded by C(n)∫_S f≤C(n); the integral over E_2 is bounded by mass(E_2) because 0≤f≤1, hence also by C(n). Rescaling gives the general estimate.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.2, width-volume inequality for subsets of a rectangle
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R = [0,R_1] × ... × [0,R_n] be an n-rectangle with R_1≤...≤R_n, and let U⊂R be open with volume V(U). For every integer l with 0≤l≤k, one has W_k(U) < C(n) (R_1...R_l)^{(n−k)/(n−l)} V(U)^{(k−l)/(n−l)}, where the empty product for l=0 is 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The case l=0 is Theorem 1. The case l=k follows from monotonicity W_k(U)≤W_k(R)≤R_1...R_k. For 0<l<k, define a function f on the (n−l)-rectangle [0,R_{l+1}]×...×[0,R_n] by f(y)=(R_1...R_l)^{-1} Vol_l(U∩([0,R_1]×...×[0,R_l]×{y})). Then 0≤f≤1 and ∫f=(R_1...R_l)^{-1}V(U). By Proposition 2.1 in dimension n−l, f admits a sweepout by (k−l)-cycles F(p) for which ∫_{F(p)} f≤C(n)[(R_1...R_l)^{-1}V(U)]^{(k−l)/(n−l)}. Taking the product F(p)×[0,R_1]×...×[0,R_l] gives k-cycles; restricting them to U yields a sweepout of U. The k-volume in U is at most (R_1...R_l) times the f-integral, giving exactly C(n)(R_1...R_l)^{(n−k)/(n−l)}V(U)^{(k−l)/(n−l)}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.3, packing-width of a rectangle
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a bounded open set U, define P_{k,N}(U) to be the supremal W such that U contains N disjoint subsets U_i each satisfying W_k(U_i)≥W. If R is an n-rectangle with side lengths R_1≤...≤R_n, then P_{k,N}(R), up to multiplicative constants C(n), equals the infimum over integers 0≤l≤k of R_1...R_l (R_{l+1}...R_n)^{(k−l)/(n−l)} N^{−(k−l)/(n−l)}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the upper bound, take any N disjoint subsets U_i⊂R. One has volume at most Vol(R)/N. Applying Proposition 2.2 to that subset, for each l, gives W_k(U_i)≤C(n)(R_1...R_l)^{(n−k)/(n−l)}(Vol(R)/N)^{(k−l)/(n−l)}, which simplifies to C(n)R_1...R_l(R_{l+1}...R_n)^{(k−l)/(n−l)}N^{−(k−l)/(n−l)}. Therefore P_{k,N}(R) is bounded above by the infimum of these expressions. For the lower bound, use rectangular grid packings. If N is small enough, specifically N≤(R_{k+1}...R_n)/R_k^{n−k}, one can pack N subrectangles of dimensions R_1×...×R_k×R_k×...×R_k, each having k-width comparable to R_1...R_k. If N is larger, choose l<k and S with R_l≤S≤R_{l+1} so that N subrectangles of dimensions R_1×...×R_l×S×...×S fill a definite fraction of R; then N R_1...R_l S^{n−l}≥c(n)Vol(R), and each such subrectangle has k-width comparable to R_1...R_l S^{k−l}. Solving for S yields the matching lower expression. These grid packings match one of the upper bounds up to C(n).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.1, derivative formula for k-dilation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a piecewise smooth map f between n-dimensional Riemannian manifolds, the k-dilation of f equals sup_x ||Λ^k df_x||, the supremal operator norm of the induced linear map on k-vectors. If the singular values of df_x are 0≤s_1≤...≤s_n, then ||Λ^k df_x||=s_{n−k+1}...s_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The singular k-vectors for Λ^k df_x are wedge products of k singular vectors of df_x, and the corresponding singular values are all products of k distinct singular values of df_x. The largest product is s_{n−k+1}...s_n and is achieved on a simple k-vector, so a small k-disk tangent to the corresponding singular directions is stretched by this factor to first order. Conversely every infinitesimal k-disk has volume stretch at most this operator norm. A standard calculus/localization argument gives equality between the global k-dilation and the supremum of these pointwise norms.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.2, higher dilations controlled by k-dilation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f be a piecewise smooth map between n-dimensional Riemannian manifolds. If the k-dilation of f is at most λ, then for every i>0 with k+i≤n, the (k+i)-dilation of f is at most λ^{(k+i)/k}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: At a point, write the singular values of df as s_1≤...≤s_n. The hypothesis gives s_{n−k+1}...s_n≤λ, so in particular s_{n−k+1}≤λ^{1/k}. The (k+i)-dilation density is s_{n−k−i+1}...s_n. Since the i extra factors are no larger than s_{n−k+1}, this product is at most s_{n−k+1}^i(s_{n−k+1}...s_n)≤λ^{i/k}λ=λ^{(k+i)/k}. Taking the supremum over points proves the result.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.3, basic lower bound for D_k between rectangles
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R and S be n-rectangles with ordered side lengths R_1≤...≤R_n and S_1≤...≤S_n, and put Q_i=S_i/R_i. If U⊂R and f:(U,∂U)→(S,∂S) is a nonzero-degree piecewise smooth map with k-dilation λ, then λ≥c(n) Q_1...Q_k. Equivalently, the infimal k-dilation D_k(U,S) is at least c(n)Q_1...Q_k.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By monotonicity under subsets, W_k(U)≤W_k(R)≤R_1...R_k. Since f has nonzero degree and k-dilation λ, width monotonicity under degree maps gives λ W_k(U)≥W_k(S). Proposition 1.2 gives W_k(S)≥c(n)S_1...S_k. Combining these inequalities gives λ≥c(n)S_1...S_k/(R_1...R_k)=c(n)Q_1...Q_k.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.4, packing-width lower bounds for D_k between rectangles
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R and S be n-rectangles with ordered side lengths R_1≤...≤R_n and S_1≤...≤S_n, and let Q_i=S_i/R_i. If U⊂R, then for every integer l with 0≤l≤k, the infimal k-dilation D_k(U,S) of a nonzero-degree map from U to S satisfies D_k(U,S) ≥ c(n) Q_1...Q_l (Q_{l+1}...Q_n)^{(k−l)/(n−l)}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let f:U→S have nonzero degree and k-dilation λ. For any N, take N disjoint subsets S_i⊂S with k-width nearly P_{k,N}(S). Their preimages U_i=f^{-1}(S_i) are disjoint subsets of U, and the restricted maps have nonzero degree in the relative sense. Width monotonicity gives W_k(U_i)≥λ^{-1}W_k(S_i), so λ P_{k,N}(U)≥P_{k,N}(S). Since U⊂R, P_{k,N}(U)≤P_{k,N}(R), hence λ≥P_{k,N}(S)/P_{k,N}(R). Now choose N so that S contains a grid of roughly N subrectangles with dimensions S_1×...×S_l×S_l×...×S_l when l≥1, or cubes of side S_1 when l=0; each has k-width comparable to S_1...S_l S_l^{k−l} or S_1^k in the l=0 case. Apply the upper estimate for P_{k,N}(R) from Proposition 2.3 with the same l. After substituting N and simplifying, the quotient is bounded below by c(n)Q_1...Q_l(Q_{l+1}...Q_n)^{(k−l)/(n−l)}. The paper notes that optimizing over N gives no further lower bounds beyond the maximum of these monomials, up to constants.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Boundary-volume lower bound for (n−1)-dilation in the proof of Theorem 2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R and S be n-rectangles with ordered side lengths R_1≤...≤R_n and S_1≤...≤S_n, and put Q_i=S_i/R_i. If f:(R,∂R)→(S,∂S) is degree one with (n−1)-dilation λ, then λ≥(1/n) Q_2...Q_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The restriction f|_{∂R}:∂R→∂S must be surjective because f has degree one as a map of pairs. Therefore λ Vol_{n−1}(∂R)≥Vol_{n−1}(∂S). Since R_1≤...≤R_n, each boundary face of R has (n−1)-volume at most R_2...R_n, so Vol(∂R)≤2n R_2...R_n. The two faces of S perpendicular to the first coordinate have total volume 2S_2...S_n, so Vol(∂S)≥2S_2...S_n. Combining gives λ≥S_2...S_n/(nR_2...R_n)=(1/n)Q_2...Q_n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Construction 2, the snake map
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let n≥3 and 2≤k≤n−1. Let R and S be n-rectangles with side lengths ordered as usual. Suppose R_i=S_i for all i≤n−k, and suppose R_{n−k+1}...R_{n−k+b} ≥ S_{n−k+1}...S_{n−k+b} for every b=1,...,k. Then there is a degree one map f:(R,∂R)→(S,∂S) with k-dilation at most C(n).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write R=R'×R'' and S=S'×S'', where R' and S' have dimension n−k and are congruent, and R'',S'' have dimension k. The product inequalities imply that a dilated copy 3S'' admits a smooth C(n)-bilipschitz embedding I into R''; call its image A. Choose a smooth cutoff H on 3S'' equal to 1 on the central S'' and zero near the boundary, with Lipschitz constant approximately S_{n−k+1}^{−1}; extend H∘I^{-1} by zero to all of R'', calling the extension Hbar. Define Φ_1(x',x'')=(Hbar(x'')x',x''). Because R_{n−k}=S_{n−k}≤S_{n−k+1}, the Lipschitz constant of Φ_1 is bounded by C(n), and its image lies in Q=(R'×A)∪({0}×R''). Next choose a piecewise smooth retraction φ_2:R''→A mapping R''\A to ∂A, and define Φ_2(x',x'')=(x',φ_2(x'')). Although Φ_2 may have large k-dilation on all of R, its restriction to Q has k-dilation at most 1: it is the identity on R'×A, while on {0}×(R''\A) it maps a k-dimensional set into {0}×∂A, of dimension k−1. Then define Φ_3(x',x'')=(x',I^{-1}(x'')), a C(n)-bilipschitz map from R'×A to S'×3S''. Finally retract S'×3S'' onto S by a 1-Lipschitz convex retraction Φ_4. The composition Φ_4∘Φ_3∘Φ_2∘Φ_1 is degree one and has k-dilation bounded by C(n).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2, optimal (n−1)-dilation between rectangles up to constants
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R and S be n-dimensional rectangles with ordered side lengths R_1≤...≤R_n and S_1≤...≤S_n, and define Q_i=S_i/R_i. Let D_{n−1}(R,S) be the infimal (n−1)-dilation of a degree one map (R,∂R)→(S,∂S). Up to a multiplicative constant C(n), D_{n−1}(R,S) is the maximum of the following n monomials: for each l=1,...,n−1, Q_1...Q_l (Q_{l+1}...Q_n)^{(n−l−1)/(n−l)}, and the additional monomial Q_2...Q_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The lower bounds are assembled from earlier estimates. The monomials with l=1,...,n−1 are exactly the Proposition 3.4 packing-width lower bounds with k=n−1. The final monomial Q_2...Q_n is the boundary-volume lower bound: a degree one map sends ∂R onto ∂S, so λVol(∂R)≥Vol(∂S), and the ordered side lengths imply Vol(∂R)≤2nR_2...R_n and Vol(∂S)≥2S_2...S_n. For upper bounds, the proof rescales S so that the stated lower-bound maximum is 1. This gives inequalities (*) consisting of R_1...R_l(R_{l+1}...R_n)^{(n−l−1)/(n−l)} ≥ S_1...S_l(S_{l+1}...S_n)^{(n−l−1)/(n−l)} for l=1,...,n−1, and R_2...R_n≥S_2...S_n. In dimension n=3, the proof handles three cases: if R_1<S_1, first use a 2-contracting linear map to an intermediate T with T_1=S_1 and then a snake map T→S; if R_1≥S_1 but R_2<S_2, use a 2-contracting linear map to T with T_2=S_2 and then a 1-contracting linear map T→S; if R_1≥S_1 and R_2≥S_2, use the snake map directly. For higher n, the main lemma treats the case R_1=S_1. It introduces conditions C(p): R_1=S_1, product inequalities R_2...R_a≥S_2...S_a for 2≤a≤p, the mixed inequalities R_2...R_l(R_{l+1}...R_n)^{(n−l−1)/(n−l)}≥S_2...S_l(S_{l+1}...S_n)^{(n−l−1)/(n−l)} for p≤l≤n−1, and R_2...R_n≥S_2...S_n. The proof descends in p. Condition C(n−1) gives the hypotheses of the snake map. If C(p) holds but a first product R_2...R_b<S_2...S_b occurs, with p<b≤n−2, the proof applies a sequence of (n−1)-expanding linear transformations to S, decreasing a block of side lengths around b and increasing later side lengths equally, until an intermediate T satisfies R_2...R_b=T_2...T_b while preserving the relevant mixed quantities. Then R and T satisfy C(b), so the induction hypothesis gives a good map R→T, and the linear map T→S is (n−1)-contracting. The cases R_1<S_1 and R_1>S_1 are reduced to the equal-first-side lemma by further (n−1)-contracting or (n−1)-expanding linear transformations of S to an intermediate T with T_1=R_1, checking algebraically that the inequalities (*) are preserved. Composing the resulting maps gives degree one maps with (n−1)-dilation bounded by C(n), matching the lower bounds.

proof_techniques.name: Sweepouts plus gluing homomorphism
proof_techniques.description: The paper encodes a sweepout as a continuous family of flat k-cycles whose associated gluing homomorphism from the homology of the parameter space to H_n(M,∂M;Z) is nontrivial. A fine triangulation of the parameter space and small flat-norm approximations turn such a family into a chain-level complex C assigning (k+i)-chains to i-simplices, with boundaries matching simplicial boundaries. This model makes it possible to prove naturality under maps and restriction to subdomains.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a degree one map f:(R,∂R)→(S,∂S), pushforward of sweeping cycles or pullback of wide target subsets can convert the degree hypothesis and Dil_2(f)≤1 into lower bounds on the widths of subsets of R.

proof_techniques.name: Bending planes around a cubical skeleton
proof_techniques.description: To prove width-volume estimates, Guth begins with parallel k-planes and composes them with a proper degree-one PL map Ψ that is bounded distance from the identity. The map collapses most of space into a fixed translated cubical k-skeleton S, while only a small neighborhood of the dual (n−k−1)-skeleton can contribute off-skeleton mass. A generic choice of plane directions ensures each bent plane meets only boundedly many bad regions, while averaging over translations ensures U has small intersection with S.
