proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is the engine behind estimates such as W_2(U)≤C Vol(U)^{1/2} and the sharper rectangle-relative estimates for preimage sets U⊂R. It is especially relevant if one subdivides S and studies disjoint preimages in R.

proof_techniques.name: Rectangle-adapted width-volume via slicing off short directions
proof_techniques.description: For U⊂R, the paper improves the raw Euclidean estimate by slicing U along the first l short directions of R. The normalized fiber-volume function f on the remaining n−l coordinates satisfies 0≤f≤1 and ∫f=Vol(U)/(R_1...R_l). A width-volume estimate for f, then taking products with the l-dimensional short rectangle, gives W_k(U)≤C(R_1...R_l)^{(n−k)/(n−l)}Vol(U)^{(k−l)/(n−l)}.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In dimension 4 with k=2, the l=1 estimate bounds the 2-width of a preimage subset using R_1 and its volume; the hypothesis that R_1 is small relative to S_1 is exactly the kind of information this refinement exploits.

proof_techniques.name: Packing-width lower bounds by disjoint target blocks
proof_techniques.description: The paper defines P_{k,N}(U) as the largest width W for which U contains N disjoint subsets of k-width at least W. If a degree map f:U→S has k-dilation λ, then preimages of N disjoint wide subsets in S are N disjoint subsets of U with width reduced by at most λ, so λP_{k,N}(U)≥P_{k,N}(S). Rectangular grid packings give sharp estimates of P_{k,N}(R) up to C(n), leading to explicit monomial lower bounds for D_k(R,S).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A natural route is to choose many disjoint subrectangles of the target S, estimate their 2-width from below, and use the rectangle-adapted upper bounds for the 2-width of their disjoint preimages in R.

proof_techniques.name: Singular-value bootstrapping from k-dilation to higher dilation
proof_techniques.description: Using the pointwise singular values of df, Guth proves that if Dil_k(f)≤λ then Dil_{k+i}(f)≤λ^{(k+i)/k}. The argument is purely linear algebraic and depends on the fact that the largest (k+i)-fold product of singular values is controlled by the largest k-fold product times i additional factors, each at most λ^{1/k}.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: With Dil_2(f)≤1 in four dimensions, one immediately gets Dil_3(f)≤1 and Dil_4(f)≤1. This allows use of the paper's (n−1)-dilation estimates and boundary-volume obstruction in addition to the direct 2-width estimates.

proof_techniques.name: Boundary-volume obstruction for hypersurface dilation
proof_techniques.description: For maps between rectangles, a degree one map of pairs restricts to a surjective map of boundaries. If its (n−1)-dilation is λ, the boundary volume of the target is at most λ times the boundary volume of the source. Ordered side lengths turn this into λ≥c(n)S_2...S_n/(R_2...R_n).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: After applying singular-value bootstrapping, Dil_2(f)≤1 gives Dil_3(f)≤1 in dimension 4, hence a necessary inequality involving R_2R_3R_4 and S_2S_3S_4.

proof_techniques.name: Scaling normalization of homogeneous inequalities
proof_techniques.description: The estimates for D_k(R,S) are homogeneous in the target side lengths. In proving Theorem 2, Guth rescales S so that the maximum lower-bound monomial is normalized to 1, then proves that the resulting inequalities imply existence of a map with bounded dilation. This reduces algebraic comparisons to dimensionless side-length product inequalities.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The alternatives in the original problem are homogeneous side-length inequalities up to an absolute constant. A contradiction proof can similarly normalize one target or source scale and track which monomial lower bounds become larger than 1.

proof_techniques.name: Snake-map factorization
proof_techniques.description: The snake map constructs low k-dilation degree-one maps by first embedding a dilated copy of the target's last k-dimensional rectangle into the source's last k-dimensional rectangle as a winding set A, then collapsing the source to a union R'×A ∪ {0}×R'', retracting this union to R'×A, undoing the bilipschitz embedding, and finally retracting to the target. The key point is that the potentially high-Lipschitz retraction has zero k-dimensional Jacobian on the collapsed part and is the identity on the main part.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Although the original problem asks for a lower bound, the snake construction explains why linear stretching estimates alone are insufficient; lower-bound arguments need robust sweepout or packing methods that survive such nonlinear collapses.

other_useful_info: All constants c(n) and C(n) in the paper depend only on dimension and may change from line to line. The paper explicitly states as an open problem the estimation of D_k(R,S) up to a constant factor for 2≤k≤n−2; thus the case n=4,k=2 lies in the intermediate range for which this paper gives general lower bounds but not a complete sharp formula. The paper cites Guth's thesis [9] for additional upper and lower bounds for intermediate k-dilation between rectangles. Proposition 4.1 shows that for every n≥3 and 2≤k≤n−1 there are rectangles R,S for which the best linear k-dilation is larger than the optimal nonlinear k-dilation by an arbitrarily large factor; the example uses R with dimensions ε,...,ε,1,1 and S with dimensions ε,...,ε,ε^{−1}, together with the snake map. The appendix also records Falconer's theorem that for k>n/2, bounded open U⊂R^n has small linear k-width controlled by Vol(U)^{k/n}, and cites Bourgain for a weaker linear 2-width estimate in R^4; these are related to but separate from the nonlinear width machinery used in the main lower bounds.
why_relevant: This paper proves lower bounds for k-dilation of degree-one maps between Euclidean domains and estimates such bounds for rectangles. Its width/sweepout machinery is directly relevant to turning Dil_2(f) ≤ 1 and degree one into side-length inequalities.

title: Minimax Problems Related to Cup Powers and Steenrod Squares
overall_summary: The paper defines minimax volumes for cohomology classes on spaces of mod 2 flat k-cycles in the unit n-ball and proves quantitative lower and upper bounds for the classes a(k,n)^p and Sq_0^{Q_0}...Sq_{n-k-1}^{Q_{n-k-1}}a(k,n), where a(k,n) is the fundamental cohomology class of the cycle space. The lower bounds combine Gromov's isoperimetric contraction argument, Lusternik-Schnirelmann theory, and a vanishing lemma for Steenrod squares; the upper bounds use explicit cycle families and a bend-and-cancel construction based on skeleton-squeezing maps. The most directly relevant part for degree-one maps is Appendix 3: it explains that k-dilation of a degree-one map between Riemannian manifolds compares minimax volumes of corresponding cycle-space cohomology classes. The appendix also states Guth's rectangle k-dilation theorem and formulates a conjectural anisotropic minimax formula for rectangles that would imply such rectangle dilation inequalities.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 1 construction: cycle space, gluing homomorphism, and fundamental class a(k,n)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let B^n be the unit n-ball. The paper defines Z(k,n) to be the completion, in the area-distance, of the space of mod 2 relative Lipschitz k-cycles in (B^n,∂B^n). The area-distance between two relative k-cycles C_1 and C_2 is the infimum of the (k+1)-volume of a relative Lipschitz chain D with ∂D=C_1-C_2. For a continuous family F:X→Z(k,n), with X a simplicial complex, one defines a gluing homomorphism G:H_i(X;Z_2)→H_{k+i}(B^n,∂B^n;Z_2). In degree i=n-k, this gives a homomorphism H_{n-k}(Z(k,n);Z_2)→Z_2, hence a cohomology class a(k,n)∈H^{n-k}(Z(k,n);Z_2). A family F detects a cohomology class α if F^*α≠0. The minimax volume of α is V(α)=inf_F sup_{C in F} Vol_k(C), where F ranges over all families detecting α.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The main device is a complex of cycles, a discrete chain-level approximation to a continuous family of cycles. Given a fine triangulation of X and a small δ>0, choose for each vertex v a relative Lipschitz k-cycle C(v) close in area-distance to F(v). For each edge, choose a small (k+1)-chain filling the difference of the endpoint cycles. Inductively, for each i-simplex Δ, choose a (k+i)-chain C(Δ) whose boundary is the sum of the chains assigned to ∂Δ. The Federer-Fleming isoperimetric inequality supplies the fillings as long as k+i<n; for dimension n one uses the fact that an n-cycle of sufficiently small volume has degree zero and hence bounds; above dimension n the filling is automatic. This chain map shifted by k induces G:H_i(X)→H_{k+i}(B^n,∂B^n). A homotopy argument on X×[0,1], using the same inductive filling procedure, shows that G is independent of all small choices and of refinement. For an (n-k)-cycle z in Z(k,n), G([z])∈H_n(B^n,∂B^n)=Z_2 defines the pairing with a(k,n).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Federer-Fleming isoperimetric inequality as used in Section 1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: There is a constant C(n) such that if k<n and C is a mod 2 relative Lipschitz k-cycle in the unit n-ball, then C bounds a relative Lipschitz (k+1)-chain D with Vol_{k+1}(D)<C(n) Vol_k(C)^{(k+1)/k}. The paper also uses the facts that a relative n-cycle in the unit n-ball of volume less than Vol(B^n) has degree zero and bounds, and that relative cycles of dimension greater than n automatically bound.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The inequality is quoted rather than proved. In the paper it is used inductively: after assigning chains to the boundary of a simplex in the parameter complex, that boundary is a small relative cycle, and the isoperimetric inequality fills it with controlled volume. The small n-dimensional case is handled by degree: if an n-cycle in B^n has volume smaller than the unit ball, it cannot represent the nonzero relative fundamental class.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 2.1, 2.2, and 2.3: Gromov lower bound for the fundamental class
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every 0≤k<n there is c(n)>0 such that V(a(k,n))≥c(n). More strongly, if a family F:X→Z(k,n) detects a(k,n), then some cycle in F has filling volume at least c(n), where the filling volume of a relative k-cycle C is the least (k+1)-volume of a relative chain D with ∂D=C. The paper also defines V_+(α) by saying that V_+(α)≥V if for every family F:X→Z(k,n), F^*α vanishes on an open neighborhood of the subset of parameters whose cycles have volume at most V; then V_+(a(k,n))≥c(n).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume first that every cycle in a detecting family has very small volume ε. Approximate F by a complex of cycles C over a fine triangulation of X. Construct a homotopy from C to the zero complex over X×[0,1]. For each vertex v, fill C(v) by a (k+1)-chain of volume O_n(ε) using the isoperimetric inequality. Then proceed inductively over higher-dimensional cells Δ×(0,1): their already-defined boundary is a small cycle and is filled by the isoperimetric inequality; in the n-dimensional step, small n-volume implies degree zero; above n, fillings are automatic. This homotopy makes the gluing homomorphism zero, contradicting detection of a(k,n). The filling-volume version is the same argument, starting from small fillings of the vertex cycles rather than small volumes. For V_+, if S is the set of parameters with small cycle volume V, the isoperimetric inequality gives small filling volume on S; filling volume is continuous in the area-distance, so an open neighborhood of S has small filling volume. Applying the filling-volume version shows that F^*a(k,n) vanishes on that neighborhood.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Cup Product Theorem, Section 3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let α_1,...,α_P be cohomology classes in H^*(Z(k,n);Z_2), and let α=α_1∪...∪α_P. Then V_+(α)≥4^{-k} [Σ_{i=1}^P V_+(α_i)^{n/(n-k)}]^{(n-k)/n}. The paper remarks that the same argument works for arbitrary coefficient groups and also for integral cycle spaces.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose disjoint balls B_i inside the unit n-ball with radii r_i satisfying Σ r_i^n≤4^{-n}; the paper gives a greedy packing argument after ordering the radii. For a family F:X→Z(k,n), define S(i) to be the set of parameters x such that Vol_k(F(x)∩B_i)≤(1-ε) r_i^k V_+(α_i). After rescaling B_i to the unit ball, the definition of V_+ implies that F^*α_i vanishes on a neighborhood of S(i). Lusternik-Schnirelmann theory implies that F^*(α_1∪...∪α_P) vanishes on a neighborhood of ∪_i S(i). If a cycle is not in any S(i), then its total volume is larger than Σ_i (1-ε) r_i^k V_+(α_i). Thus all cycles below that volume lie in the union where the product class vanishes. Finally choose the radii to maximize Σ_i r_i^k V_+(α_i) under Σ_i r_i^n≤4^{-n}; the maximizing choice gives the displayed expression, and ε is sent to zero.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1: minimax volumes of cup powers
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For each n there are constants 0<c(n)<C(n) such that for every 0≤k<n and every positive integer p, c(n) p^{(n-k)/n} ≤ V(a(k,n)^p) ≤ C(n) p^{(n-k)/n}, where a(k,n)∈H^{n-k}(Z(k,n);Z_2) is the fundamental cohomology class of the mod 2 relative k-cycle space of the unit n-ball.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The lower bound is an immediate application of the Cup Product Theorem with α_i=a(k,n), together with V_+(a(k,n))≥c(n). Equivalently, one can take p disjoint balls of radius r≈p^{-1/n}; if a family detects a(k,n)^p, then by Lusternik-Schnirelmann theory some cycle must have at least c r^k volume in each of the p balls, giving total volume at least c p r^k=c p^{(n-k)/n}. For the upper bound, the paper first builds a family F(p) of 0-cycles in Z(0,n-k) detecting a(0,n-k)^p, with each cycle containing at most p points. If n-k=1, this uses real roots modulo 2 of degree p polynomials, parameterized by RP^p. If n-k≥2, it uses the p-fold symmetric product SP^p S^{n-k} and Nakaoka's calculation that the top cohomology is generated by the pth power of the degree n-k generator. The k-fold suspension Σ:Z(0,n-k)→Z(k,n) turns these into unions of at most p parallel k-planes detecting a(k,n)^p. These raw cycles have too much volume, so the proof applies a skeleton-squeezing PL map Ψ at scale s=p^{-1/n}, pushing most of space into a k-dimensional lattice skeleton. Each bent k-plane lies in the skeleton except for O_n(1) pieces of volume O(s^k); after mod 2 cancellation, the skeleton contribution has volume O(s^{k-n}) and the exceptional contribution has volume O(p s^k), both O(p^{(n-k)/n}).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Vanishing Lemma for Steenrod squares, Section 4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a simplicial complex, let α∈H^p(X;Z_2), and let π:S^i×X→X be projection to X. For an open set V⊂S^i×X, write V(θ)={x∈X:(θ,x)∈V} and P[V]=∩_{θ∈S^i} (V(θ)∪V(-θ)). If π^*α vanishes on V, then the lower Steenrod square Sq_i α vanishes on P[V]. Here Sq_i:H^p→H^{2p-i} denotes the lower-square notation Sq_i α=Sq^{p-i}α.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof uses a standard construction of Steenrod squares. Represent α by a map f:X→K(Z_2,p). Let Γ_iX=(S^i×X∧X)/Z_2, where Z_2 sends (θ,x_1,x_2) to (-θ,x_2,x_1), and let Λ_iX be obtained from Γ_iX by collapsing the RP^i coming from the basepoint of X∧X. The diagonal gives Δ:RP^i×X→Λ_iX. For a universal class λ∈H^{2p}(Λ_iK(Z_2,p)), one has Δ^*(Λ_i f)^*λ=Σ_{j=0}^i ω^j⊗Sq_j α, where ω generates H^1(RP^i;Z_2). If K⊂V is compact and π^*α vanishes on V, then f∘π can be homotoped to the basepoint on a neighborhood U of K. Use this homotopy F_t to define equivariant maps G_t(θ,x)=(θ,F_t(θ,x),F_t(-θ,x)) into S^i×K∧K. These descend to a homotopy of Λ_i f∘Δ which is basepoint-valued on RP^i×P[U]. Hence the class Σω^j⊗Sq_jα vanishes near RP^i×P[K], and the Kunneth theorem implies Sq_iα vanishes near P[K]. An exhaustion of V by compact sets gives the result for P[V]; the same argument shows P[V] is open.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Main Lemma 1, Section 4: pyramid thickness implies a Steenrod minimax lower bound
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix n, 1≤k≤n-1, and 1≤i≤n-k-1. An i-pyramid of open sets of height P consists of maps U_p:(S^i)^{P-p}→O, 0≤p≤P, where O is the space of open subsets of R^n, satisfying U_p(θ_1,...,θ_{P-p})⊂U_{p+1}(θ_1,...,θ_{P-p-1}) and U_p(θ_1,...,θ_{P-p-1},φ)∩U_p(θ_1,...,θ_{P-p-1},-φ)=∅. Assume U_P(*) is contained in the unit ball and every bottom set U_0(θ) is convex. Define T_0(θ)=1/2 Rad(U_0(θ))^k, where Rad(A) is the largest radius of a ball in A, and define recursively T_p(θ)=inf_{φ∈S^i} [T_{p-1}(θ,φ)+T_{p-1}(θ,-φ)]. Let T=T_P(*). Then for every α∈H^*(Z(k,n);Z_2), V_+(Sq_i^P α)≥T V_+(α).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a family F:X→Z(k,n), define small-cycle sets S_p⊂(S^i)^{P-p}×X by requiring Vol_k(F(x)∩U_p(θ))≤T_p(θ)V_+(α). At the bottom level, convexity of U_0(θ) allows a continuous choice, up to an arbitrarily small loss, of balls B(θ)⊂U_0(θ) with radius nearly Rad(U_0(θ)). Restrict F(x) to B(θ) and rescale to the unit ball; this family is homotopic to F pulled back by projection, and on S_0 its cycles have volume below V_+(α). Thus the pullback of F^*α vanishes near S_0. For the induction step, view (S^i)^{P-p+1}×X as S^i×Y with Y=(S^i)^{P-p}×X. If the pullback of F^*Sq_i^{p-1}α vanishes near S_{p-1}, apply the Vanishing Lemma to conclude that the pullback of F^*Sq_i^p α vanishes near P[S_{p-1}]. The pyramid nesting and antipodal disjointness, together with the recursive definition of T_p, imply S_p⊂P[S_{p-1}]: if F(x) is small in U_p(θ), then for every φ it is small in at least one of the two disjoint subsets U_{p-1}(θ,φ) or U_{p-1}(θ,-φ). Therefore the induction reaches p=P. Since every parameter whose total cycle volume is at most T V_+(α) lies in S_P, the definition of V_+ gives the desired lower bound.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Main Lemma 2, Section 4: construction of thick pyramids
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix n, 1≤k≤n-1, and 1≤i≤n-k-1. For every height P, the paper constructs an i-pyramid of open convex subsets of the unit n-ball such that for every ε>0 there is c(n,ε)>0, independent of P, and the k-thickness T of the pyramid satisfies T≥c(n,ε)(2-ε)^{((n-i-k)/(n-i))P}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The construction parameterizes possible cut directions by spaces O(p) of sequences v_1,...,v_{P-p} of unit vectors such that each string of n-i consecutive vectors is orthonormal, using fixed initial vectors v_{1-n},...,v_0. The forgetful maps O(p-1)→O(p) are trivial S^i-bundles; hence O(p) can be identified with (S^i)^{P-p}. Define convex sets CON_P(*)=B^n. Inductively, CON_p(v_1,...,v_{P-p}) is one of the two equal-volume halves of CON_{p+1}(v_1,...,v_{P-p-1}) cut by a hyperplane perpendicular to v_{P-p}; replacing v_{P-p} by -v_{P-p} selects the opposite half. This gives the pyramid rules and convexity. To estimate thickness, follow a minimizing branch in the recursive infima, producing a binary tree of convex bodies C_I, |I|≤P, where each C_I is bisected by volume and any n-i consecutive cutting normals are orthogonal. The goal is to show Σ_{|I|=P} Rad(C_I)^{-1}≤C(β,n)2^P(2+ε(β,n))^{P/(n-i)}, with ε(β,n)→0 as β→∞. Hölder then gives Σ Rad(C_I)^k ≥ c(β,n)(2-ε)^{((n-i-k)/(n-i))P}. The proof of the inverse-radius estimate approximates every convex body C by a rectangle with side lengths R_1(C)≤...≤R_n(C), up to dimensional constants. Let π_q(C) be the average q-volume of orthogonal projections of C, and define N_{a,β}(C)=β^a Vol(C)^{-1/a}π_{n-a}(C)^{1/a}, N_β(C)=max_a N_{a,β}(C). One has N_β(C) comparable to Rad(C)^{-1} up to C(β,n). If R_{a+1}(C_I)≥βR_a(C_I), then over n-i generations the orthogonality of cut normals gives Σ_{J∈D_{n-i}(I)}π_{n-a}(C_J)≤(2+ε)^aπ_{n-a}(C_I), and hence Σ N_{a,β}(C_J)≤(2+ε)2^{n-i}N_{a,β}(C_I). Conversely, if N_{a,β}(C)=N_β(C), comparison with N_{a-1,β} and N_{a+1,β} forces R_{a+1}(C)≥c(n)β^2R_a(C). Grouping G≈δ(n)logβ generations at a time ensures the needed gap persists for all relevant descendants. Iterating the grouped estimate yields the displayed inverse-radius bound.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Steenrod Tower Theorem, Section 4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every ε>0 there is c(n,ε)>0 such that the following holds. Let α∈H^*(Z(k,n);Z_2), let i be an integer with 0≤i≤n-k-1, and let P>0. Then V_+(Sq_i^P α)≥c(n,ε)(2-ε)^{((n-i-k)/(n-i))P} V_+(α). For i=0, Sq_0 is the lower Steenrod square equal to cup-square on a class, so Sq_0^P α is an iterated 2^P-th cup power.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For i=0, the statement follows from the Cup Product Theorem applied repeatedly to α^{2^P}; in fact the proof gives a lower bound with base 2 rather than 2-ε. For i≥1, Main Lemma 2 supplies an i-pyramid of height P with k-thickness T≥c(n,ε)(2-ε)^{((n-i-k)/(n-i))P}. Main Lemma 1 then gives V_+(Sq_i^P α)≥T V_+(α), which is the theorem.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2: minimax volumes for towers of Steenrod squares
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For each ε>0 there is c(n,ε)>0 and there is C(n), independent of ε, such that for every 0≤k<n and all nonnegative integers Q_0,...,Q_{n-k-1}, c(n,ε) Π_{i=0}^{n-k-1} (2-ε)^{((n-k-i)/(n-i))Q_i} ≤ V(Sq_0^{Q_0}...Sq_{n-k-1}^{Q_{n-k-1}} a(k,n)) ≤ C(n) Π_{i=0}^{n-k-1} 2^{((n-k-i)/(n-i))Q_i}. The notation is lower-square notation: Sq_i:H^d→H^{2d-i} is Sq^{d-i}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the lower bound, use V≥V_+. Handle the Sq_0^{Q_0} factor with the Cup Product Theorem, giving a factor comparable to 2^{((n-k)/n)Q_0}. Then apply the Steenrod Tower Theorem successively for i=1,...,n-k-1, and finally use V_+(a(k,n))≥c(n). This yields a slightly stronger base 2 for i=0 and base 2-ε for i≥1, hence the stated bound. For the upper bound, the paper constructs explicit families of 0-cycles F_m(Q_0,...,Q_{m-1}) detecting Sq_0^{Q_0}...Sq_{m-1}^{Q_{m-1}}a(0,m). The base m=1 uses roots modulo 2 of real polynomials of degree 2^{Q_0}. The induction uses the translation map T:SZ(0,m-1)→Z(0,m), the addition map A:SP^dZ(0,m)→Z(0,m), and symmetric products with d=2^{Q_0}; Nakaoka's theorem on H^*(SP^d S^N;Z_2) and a pseudomanifold degree lemma verify detection. Applying k-fold suspension gives families F_{k,n}(Q) of k-cycles detecting the desired class; geometrically, each cycle is a union of parallel k-planes with additional structure: for every l≥k, it lies in at most 2^{Q_0+...+Q_{n-l-1}} parallel l-planes. A multiscale bend-and-cancel construction then applies skeleton-squeezing maps Ψ[n-1,s_0],...,Ψ[k,s_{n-k-1}] with scales s_i=Π_{j=0}^i 2^{-Q_j/(n-j)}. An induction shows that after squeezing down to dimension l, each cycle lies in at most C(n)2^{Q_0+...+Q_{n-l-1}} pieces of l-plane of diameter O(s_{n-l-1}). At l=k the volume is bounded by C(n)2^{Q_0+...+Q_{n-k-1}}s_{n-k-1}^k, which equals C(n)Π_i 2^{((n-k-i)/(n-i))Q_i}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 5.1, 5.2, 5.3 and Nakaoka theorem: cohomological tools for constructing detecting families
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The k-fold suspension map Σ:Z(0,n-k)→Z(k,n), defined by taking a 0-cycle C in B^{n-k} to (C×R^k)∩B^n, satisfies Σ^*a(k,n)=a(0,n-k). The translation map T:SZ(0,m-1)→Z(0,m), defined by T(t,z)=({t}×z)∩B^m for t∈[-1,1], satisfies σ T^*a(0,m)=a(0,m-1), where σ:H^{*+1}(SZ(0,m-1))→H^*(Z(0,m-1)) is the suspension isomorphism. If X is an N-dimensional pseudomanifold with N≥2, then SP^dX is a dN-dimensional pseudomanifold; if F:X→Y is a map between N-dimensional pseudomanifolds, then deg(SP^dF)=deg(F) mod 2. The paper also uses Nakaoka's theorem: for N≥2, H^N(SP^dS^N;Z_2)=Z_2 with generator β restricting to the generator on S^N, and the top group H^{dN}(SP^dS^N;Z_2)=Z_2 is generated by β^d.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Σ, evaluate both sides on a family F:P→Z(0,n-k) and a class h∈H_{n-k}(P). Approximate F by a complex of cycles C; then ΣC is a complex approximating ΣF, and the top glued chain is C(h)×R^k restricted to B^n, which has the same degree as C(h). For T, triangulate the suspension of an (m-1)-cycle in the parameter space and use the product complex C(Δ)×[a,b]; the resulting glued m-chain is C(z)×[-1,1] restricted to B^m, preserving degree. For symmetric products, X^d is a pseudomanifold; the diagonal has codimension at least 2 when N≥2, so quotienting by the symmetric group leaves SP^dX a pseudomanifold. Counting preimages of a generic point shows that deg(SP^dF)=deg(F) over Z_2. Nakaoka's theorem is quoted from Nakaoka, Cohomology mod p of symmetric products of spheres.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Skeleton-squeezing lemma from Section 5 and [8]
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let 0≤l<n and s>0. Let S be the l-skeleton of the cubical lattice in R^n with side length s, and let T be the dual (n-l-1)-skeleton. For every ε>0 there is a piecewise-linear map Ψ:R^n→R^n, linear on each simplex of a triangulation, whose top-dimensional simplices are labeled good or bad, such that each good simplex Δ satisfies Ψ(Δ)⊂S, each bad simplex lies in the ε-neighborhood of T, the number of simplices meeting any ball of radius s is bounded by C(n), the displacement |Ψ(x)-x| is at most C(n)s, and every simplex has diameter at most C(n)s.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The lemma is quoted from Guth's width-volume paper [8]. In this paper it is used as follows: if a k-plane is in general position with respect to the dual skeleton T, then it meets the ε-neighborhood of T in only controlled small pieces. Away from those bad pieces, Ψ sends the plane into the l-skeleton S. When many mod 2 cycles are pushed into the same skeleton, overlapping portions cancel, so the total volume is controlled by the volume of the skeleton plus a controlled exceptional contribution.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.4: multiscale bend-and-cancel volume control
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the proof of Theorem 2, let F_{k,n}(Q) be the suspended family of k-cycles associated to Q=(Q_0,...,Q_{n-k-1}), and let s_i=Π_{j=0}^i 2^{-Q_j/(n-j)}. Choose skeleton-squeezing maps Ψ[l,s] at generic lattice positions, with small parameters ε_k≪ε_{k+1}≪...≪ε_{n-1}. Then for each l with k≤l≤n, every cycle in Ψ[l,s_{n-l-1}]∘...∘Ψ[n-1,s_0] F_{k,n}(Q) lies in a union of at most C(n)Π_{m=0}^{n-l-1}2^{Q_m} pieces of l-planes, each of diameter at most C(n)s_{n-l-1}. For l=n the assertion is interpreted as a vacuous initial bound.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is a backward induction on l. Assume the assertion at level l and apply Ψ[l-1,s_{n-l}]. Split a cycle C into its intersection with the bad simplices and with the good simplices of this squeezing map. For the bad part, the original unsqueezed cycle lies in a controlled number of parallel (l-1)-planes; by choosing the lattice centers generically and ε_{l-1} sufficiently small after the larger ε's, the preimage of the bad set meets each such plane in only O_n(1) tiny pieces. Their images lie in O_n(1) pieces of (l-1)-planes of diameter O(s_{n-l}). For the good part, the induction hypothesis says C is covered by pieces of l-planes of diameter O(s_{n-l-1}); Ψ sends the good part into the (l-1)-skeleton of a lattice of side s_{n-l} within O(s_{n-l}) of those l-plane pieces. The number of (l-1)-faces needed to cover this is O_n((s_{n-l-1}/s_{n-l})^l)=O_n(2^{Q_{n-l}}), giving exactly the next multiplicative factor.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 9.1: minimax-volume obstruction to k-dilation of degree-one maps
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (M,g) and (N,h) be compact n-dimensional Riemannian manifolds in the setting of the paper's absolute or relative mod 2 cycle spaces, and let Φ:(M,g)→(N,h) be a piecewise C^1 map of degree 1 mod 2 with k-dilation Λ. Let O be any natural mod 2 cohomology operation, for example a cup power or a tower of Steenrod squares. Then Λ V_{(M,g)}(Oa(k,M)) ≥ V_{(N,h)}(Oa(k,N)), where a(k,M) and a(k,N) are the fundamental cohomology classes of the respective k-cycle spaces.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The map Φ sends k-cycles in M to k-cycles in N, giving Z_k(Φ):Z(k,M)→Z(k,N). By construction of the fundamental class, Z_k(Φ)^*a(k,N)=(deg Φ)a(k,M), so for degree 1 mod 2 this is a(k,M). Naturality of O gives Z_k(Φ)^*Oa(k,N)=Oa(k,M). If F is a family in M detecting Oa(k,M) with all cycles of volume at most V_{(M,g)}(Oa(k,M))+δ, then Z_k(Φ)∘F detects Oa(k,N). The k-dilation bound makes every image cycle have volume at most Λ times the original volume. Letting δ→0 gives the inequality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 9.2: relative version for maps from a subdomain
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (M^n,g) be a compact n-manifold, let (N,h) be a compact n-manifold with boundary, let U⊂M be an open set with piecewise smooth boundary, and let Φ:(U,∂U)→(N,∂N) be a piecewise C^1 map of degree 1 mod 2 with k-dilation Λ. For any natural mod 2 cohomology operation O, Λ V_{(M,g)}(Oa(k,M)) ≥ V_{(N,h)}(Oa(k,N)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose, up to δ, an optimal family of k-cycles in M detecting Oa(k,M). Restrict each cycle to U, obtaining a family of relative k-cycles in (U,∂U) that detects Oa(k,U). Composing with Φ gives a family of relative k-cycles in N detecting Oa(k,N), by the same degree-one naturality used in Proposition 9.1. Volumes increase by at most Λ, so the minimax volume in N is at most Λ times the minimax volume in M, up to δ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Appendix 3 theorem quoted from [9], [10]: k-dilation between rectangles
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R and S be n-dimensional rectangles with side lengths R_1≤...≤R_n and S_1≤...≤S_n. Let U⊂R be an open set with piecewise C^1 boundary, and let Φ:U→S be a degree 1 piecewise C^1 map with k-dilation Λ. Define Q_i=S_i/R_i. Then for every integer j with 0≤j≤k and every integer l with k+1≤l≤n, Λ ≥ c(n)(Q_1...Q_j)(Q_{j+1}...Q_l)^{(k-j)/(l-j)}. Conversely, for every pair of rectangles R,S there is a set U⊂R and a degree 1 map U→S with k-dilation at most C(n) max_{0≤j≤k, k+1≤l≤n}(Q_1...Q_j)(Q_{j+1}...Q_l)^{(k-j)/(l-j)}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper states this as the main theorem of Guth's thesis and subsequent work [9], [10], and does not prove it here. The surrounding discussion explains one possible route to such inequalities: Proposition 9.2 gives Λ≥V_S(Oa(k,S))/V_R(Oa(k,R)) for every natural cohomology operation O, so sharp anisotropic estimates of minimax volumes for rectangles would imply the displayed lower bounds.

proof_techniques.name: Cycle-space cohomology as a dilation obstruction
proof_techniques.description: The paper associates to every Riemannian n-manifold M a space of k-cycles and a fundamental cohomology class a(k,M) measuring whether a family of k-cycles sweeps out M. A degree-one map Φ:M→N induces a map of cycle spaces pulling a(k,N) back to a(k,M), and natural cohomology operations commute with this pullback. If Φ has k-dilation Λ, then all k-cycle volumes in an image family are multiplied by at most Λ, forcing Λ times the minimax volume in M to dominate the minimax volume in N.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is the paper's most direct bridge to degree-one maps with bounded 2-dilation. For rectangles, one can try to choose a cohomology operation O for which V_R(Oa(2,R)) is small under the negation of the desired alternatives while V_S(Oa(2,S)) is large.

proof_techniques.name: Gromov isoperimetric contraction of small-cycle families
proof_techniques.description: If every cycle in a family has sufficiently small volume, then one can fill the vertex cycles by the isoperimetric inequality and inductively fill all higher cells of a discrete approximation to the family. This constructs a homotopy of the family to the zero family in the cycle space, so the family cannot detect the fundamental class a(k,n). A filling-volume variant and the V_+ formalism make the vanishing occur on open neighborhoods of small-volume parameter sets.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In an anisotropic rectangle proof, the same philosophy can show that a family cannot sweep out a region if its slices are too small in suitable subrectangles. The V_+ open-neighborhood formulation is useful when applying Lusternik-Schnirelmann or Steenrod vanishing arguments.

proof_techniques.name: Lusternik-Schnirelmann lower bounds from disjoint regions
proof_techniques.description: For cup powers, the paper covers the parameter space by sets S(i) where the cycle is small inside a chosen ball B_i. Since the fundamental class restricts trivially to each S(i), Lusternik-Schnirelmann theory makes the cup product vanish on the union. A family detecting the cup product therefore contains a cycle that is large in every B_i, and disjointness of the B_i converts local lower bounds into a global volume lower bound.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This suggests proving rectangular lower bounds by selecting many disjoint subrectangles or slabs in S, showing small intersections kill a class, and forcing a cycle in a detecting family to carry area in many pieces. It is especially relevant to estimates involving cup powers Sq_0^Q.

proof_techniques.name: Steenrod-square vanishing via antipodal parameterized covers
proof_techniques.description: The Vanishing Lemma replaces the two-set vanishing rule for cup squares by an S^i-parameterized rule. If π^*α vanishes on V⊂S^i×X, then Sq_iα vanishes on the set of x for which the fiber V(x) maps onto RP^i, equivalently for every θ either (θ,x) or (-θ,x) lies in V. The proof uses the equivariant definition of Steenrod squares through S^i×X∧X and a homotopy that sends one of each antipodal pair to the basepoint.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original rectangle statement has exponents suggestive of a non-cup Steenrod operation in dimension four, especially Sq_1 on 2-cycles. This lemma is the tool for converting parameterized geometric decompositions into vanishing of Sq_1 classes.

proof_techniques.name: Pyramids of convex sets for iterated Steenrod lower bounds
proof_techniques.description: To iterate Steenrod-square arguments, the paper constructs an i-pyramid: a binary hierarchy of nested open convex sets indexed by antipodal S^i choices. The recursive thickness T measures how much k-volume a cycle must contribute at the bottom if it is not small at any stage. Main Lemma 1 turns thickness into a minimax lower bound, while Main Lemma 2 constructs thick pyramids by repeated equal-volume hyperplane bisections with any n-i consecutive cut directions orthogonal.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A rectangular analogue could use anisotropic bisections by coordinate or near-coordinate hyperplanes to produce lower bounds tuned to side lengths. In dimension four with k=2, an Sq_1 pyramid corresponds to one-parameter families of cuts and may be relevant to the S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2} type exponent.

proof_techniques.name: Anisotropic convex-body bookkeeping through widths and projections
proof_techniques.description: The geometric proof of thick pyramids controls the inverse inradius of many descendant convex sets. It approximates each convex body by a rectangle with ordered side lengths, introduces average projection volumes π_q(C), and uses quantities N_{a,β}(C)=β^a Vol(C)^{-1/a}π_{n-a}(C)^{1/a}. Orthogonality of cut directions prevents too many cuts from being nearly parallel to the same short directions, giving exponential control of Σ Rad(C_I)^{-1}.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This bookkeeping is a model for handling rectangles with very unequal side lengths. A solver could adapt the projection-volume quantities to actual rectangular boxes and track how bisections or sweepouts interact with R_1≤R_2≤R_3≤R_4.

proof_techniques.name: Bend-and-cancel upper-bound construction
proof_techniques.description: The upper bounds start with families of unions of many parallel k-planes, then apply a PL skeleton-squeezing map that pushes most of each plane into a low-dimensional lattice skeleton. Because cycles are mod 2, overlapping portions in the skeleton cancel, so the final volume is controlled by the skeleton's k-volume plus small exceptional pieces near a dual skeleton. The Steenrod tower upper bounds use several squeezing maps at successively finer scales and lower-dimensional skeleta.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Although the original problem asks for an obstruction, bend-and-cancel describes near-optimal examples and thus helps identify the correct scale of minimax estimates. It may also guide the construction of comparison families in R whose volumes give upper bounds for V_R(Oa).

proof_techniques.name: Suspension, translation, and symmetric-product cycle families
proof_techniques.description: The paper constructs detecting families of 0-cycles using roots of polynomials, symmetric products of spheres, translation in one coordinate, and addition of cycles. Suspension then converts these 0-cycle families into k-cycle families. Cohomological identities for suspension and translation, plus Nakaoka's cohomology of symmetric products, verify that these families detect the required cup powers or Steenrod towers.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For rectangles, product or suspension-like families of flat slices can give explicit upper bounds for minimax volumes in the domain rectangle. Such upper bounds are needed in the comparison Λ V_R≥V_S to derive contradictions.

proof_techniques.name: Rectangle minimax strategy from Appendix 3
proof_techniques.description: Appendix 3 explains that if one could estimate minimax volumes of rectangles for classes such as Sq_{n-l}^Q a(k,R), then Proposition 9.2 would give lower bounds for k-dilation between rectangles. The conjectured formula involves an infimum over j≤k of R_1...R_j(R_{j+1}...R_l)^{(k-j)/(l-j)} times an exponential factor depending on Q.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem is a four-dimensional rectangle statement for k=2, exactly the kind of situation Appendix 3 points to. The relevant operations to test are likely cup powers Sq_0^Q and the one nontrivial higher lower square Sq_1^Q for 2-cycles in dimension four.

other_useful_info: All homology and cohomology in the main body are with Z_2 coefficients unless otherwise stated; an integer degree-one map is degree one mod 2, so the mod 2 obstruction applies. Appendix 1 proves that the paper's area-distance completion of relative Lipschitz cycles agrees with the standard flat-cycle space, which can justify using flat cycles in minimax arguments. Appendix 2 states that the lower-bound arguments also apply to integral cycle spaces, with fundamental classes a(k,n,Z), a(k,n,Q), and a(k,n,Z_2), though many matching upper bounds are unknown. Appendix 3 records the conjectural rectangle minimax estimate: for a rectangle R with side lengths R_1≤...≤R_n and k+1≤l≤n, V_R(Sq_{n-l}^Q a(k,R)) should be comparable up to C(n) to inf_{0≤j≤k} R_1...R_j (R_{j+1}...R_l)^{(k-j)/(l-j)} 2^{((l-k)/(l-j))Q}; the paper says such estimates would imply the rectangle dilation lower bound quoted from [9], [10]. The key external references explicitly cited for rectangle maps are Guth's thesis [9] and Guth [10], while the skeleton-squeezing construction is from Guth [8].
why_relevant: This paper develops minimax lower bounds for families of cycles using cohomology operations and explicitly explains how such bounds give lower bounds for k-dilation of degree-one maps. Its appendix discusses degree-one maps between rectangles and the relevant cycle-space framework.

title: A Short Proof of Gromov's Filling Inequality
overall_summary: Wenger gives a short proof of Gromov's Euclidean-type filling volume inequality for singular Lipschitz cycles with integer or Z2 coefficients in L∞-spaces, and notes that the same proof works in CAT(0)-spaces. The proof uses three basic estimates for Lipschitz chains: coarea for slices, a cone inequality, and a lower density estimate. Its core construction is a Vitali-type decomposition of a cycle into disjoint balls capturing a definite fraction of the volume, with boundary slices small enough to fill inductively; the resulting localized cycles have diameter controlled by their volume and can then be coned. For the rectangle k-dilation problem, the most relevant outputs are the filling inequality itself, the cut-and-fill decomposition, the good-slice/coarea selection, and the idea of localizing fillings by 1-Lipschitz projections.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Main theorem: Gromov filling volume inequality in L∞
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X = L∞(Ω) be the Banach space of bounded functions on a set Ω with the supremum norm, and let n ≥ 1. Let z be an n-dimensional singular Lipschitz cycle in X with coefficients in Z or Z2. The filling volume Fillvol(z), defined as the infimum of Vol(c) over singular Lipschitz (n + 1)-chains c in X with ∂c = z, satisfies Fillvol(z) ≤ C_n Vol(z)^(1 + 1/n), where C_n depends only on n, after fixing one of the standard comparable notions of parametrized volume. The proof in the paper is also stated to work for CAT(0)-spaces.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The argument is by induction on n. For n = 1, the diameter of a closed curve is bounded by its length, and the cone inequality immediately gives a quadratic filling bound. For n ≥ 2, assume the filling inequality in dimension n - 1 with constant C_{n-1} ≥ 1. Choose ε = min{1/(4^(n-1) C_{n-1}^{n-1} A_n E_n^n n^n), 1/2}, where A_n and E_n are the density and coarea constants from Section 2. Apply the disjoint-ball proposition to the n-cycle z. For each selected ball B_i, the boundary ∂(z ∩ B_i) has controlled (n - 1)-volume, so by the inductive filling inequality it bounds an n-chain c_i of volume at most one quarter of Vol(z ∩ B_i). A 1-Lipschitz projection onto B_i is used to assume c_i lies inside B_i. Define the localized n-cycles hat z_i = (z ∩ B_i) - c_i and the residual n-cycle z' = z - Σ_i hat z_i. The estimates from the proposition and the smallness of c_i imply that diam(hat z_i) is bounded by a constant times Vol(hat z_i)^(1/n), that the total volume of the hat z_i is controlled by Vol(z), and that Vol(z') is smaller than Vol(z) by a fixed multiplicative amount. Iterating the same decomposition on z' makes the residual volume arbitrarily small. Each localized cycle hat z_i is filled by the cone inequality, giving filling volume bounded by a constant times Vol(hat z_i)^(1 + 1/n). Summing these fillings and using the inequality Σ a_i^α ≤ (Σ a_i)^α for α ≥ 1 gives a filling of z of volume at most C_n Vol(z)^(1 + 1/n); the negligible final residual is coned with arbitrarily small volume because it has bounded diameter and arbitrarily small volume.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 2 basic estimates: coarea, cone, and lower density
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a definition of parametrized volume for singular Lipschitz chains. For each n there are constants A_n ≥ 2^(-1) n^(-n/2) ω_n and D_n, E_n, depending only on n and on the chosen volume, with the following properties, where ω_n is the Euclidean volume of the unit ball in R^n. Let X be a metric space and let z be an n-dimensional singular Lipschitz cycle in X. Coarea: if ρ: X → R is 1-Lipschitz, then for almost every r in R, Vol_{n-1}(z ∩ {ρ = r}) ≤ E_n d/dr Vol_n(z ∩ {ρ ≤ r}); moreover z ∩ {ρ = r} is an (n - 1)-cycle for almost every r after a minor smoothing of the level set. Cone inequality: if X is CAT(0) or a Banach space and x_0 ∈ X, then the (n + 1)-chain obtained by joining every point of z to x_0 by geodesic segments, respectively straight line segments, has boundary z and satisfies Vol_{n+1}(c) ≤ D_n R Vol_n(z), where R is the smallest radius such that the support of z is contained in B(x_0, R). Lower density: for almost every x in the support of z, liminf_{r→0+} Vol_n(z ∩ B(x,r))/r^n > A_n. If X is a Banach space and the chosen volume is Gromov's mass* volume, then D_n ≤ 1 and E_n = 1. If X is CAT(0), then all the volume definitions agree and D_n = 1/(n + 1), E_n = 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper records these as standard facts rather than proving them in detail. The coarea estimate is the usual slicing inequality for a Lipschitz chain by a 1-Lipschitz function, comparing the level-slice volume with the derivative of the sublevel volume. The cone inequality comes from parameterizing the geodesic or straight-line homotopy from the cycle to x_0 and bounding the contribution of the radial direction by R. The lower density estimate is noted to be clear for piecewise C1 submanifolds and, for Lipschitz cycles, is cited to Kirchheim; the paper also notes that this density estimate is false for chains with real coefficients.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition: disjoint balls capturing volume with small boundary
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a metric space, let n ≥ 2, let z be an n-dimensional singular Lipschitz cycle in X, and let 0 < ε < 1. With A_n and E_n as in the Section 2 estimates, there exist finitely many pairwise disjoint closed balls B_i ⊂ X, i = 1,...,k, such that: (i) Vol_n(z ∩ B_i) ≥ 4^(-n) A_n ε diam(B_i)^n for each i; (ii) Vol_{n-1}(∂(z ∩ B_i)) ≤ E_n (A_n ε)^(1/n) n Vol_n(z ∩ B_i)^((n-1)/n) for each i; and (iii) Vol_n(z ∩ ⋃_i B_i) ≥ 5^(-n) Vol_n(z).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For x in the support of z and r ≥ 0, define V(x,r) = Vol_n(z ∩ B(x,r)) and r_0(x) = max{r ≥ 0 : V(x,r) ≥ A_n ε r^n}. The lower density estimate implies 0 < r_0(x) < ∞ for almost every x in the support of z. The maximality of r_0 gives the comparison used in the proof: V(x,5r_0(x)) < 5^n V(x,r_0(x)). Apply Vitali's 5r-covering lemma to choose finitely many points x_i such that the balls B(x_i, 2r_0(x_i)) are pairwise disjoint, the balls B(x_i, 5r_0(x_i)) cover the support of z, and Vol_n(z ∩ ⋃_i B(x_i, r_0(x_i))) ≥ 5^(-n) Vol_n(z). For a fixed i, consider V_i(r) = V(x_i,r) on the interval (r_0(x_i), 2r_0(x_i)). If the derivative inequality V_i'(r) < (A_n ε)^(1/n) n V_i(r)^((n-1)/n) did not hold on a set of positive measure, integrating the opposite inequality for V_i(r)^(1/n) would force V_i(2r_0) ≥ A_n ε (2r_0)^n, contradicting the definition of r_0. Hence there is a non-negligible set of radii r in (r_0, 2r_0) for which this derivative inequality holds. For such r, the coarea inequality gives Vol_{n-1}(z ∩ {d(·,x_i)=r}) ≤ E_n (A_n ε)^(1/n) n Vol_n(z ∩ B(x_i,r))^((n-1)/n). Choosing one such r and setting B_i = B(x_i,r) gives (ii). Since r < 2r_0, diam(B_i) ≤ 2r < 4r_0, while Vol_n(z ∩ B_i) ≥ Vol_n(z ∩ B(x_i,r_0)) ≥ A_n ε r_0^n, giving (i). Finally, the chosen B_i contain the balls B(x_i,r_0(x_i)), so the captured-volume estimate gives (iii).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Inequality (6): inductive filling of the selected ball boundaries
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be either an L∞-space or a CAT(0)-space, let n ≥ 2, and assume the filling inequality in dimension n - 1 with constant C_{n-1} ≥ 1. Choose ε = min{1/(4^(n-1) C_{n-1}^{n-1} A_n E_n^n n^n), 1/2}. If B_i are the balls supplied by the disjoint-ball proposition for an n-cycle z, then for each i there is an n-chain c_i with ∂c_i = ∂(z ∩ B_i), support contained in B_i, and Vol_n(c_i) ≤ C_{n-1} Vol_{n-1}(∂(z ∩ B_i))^(n/(n-1)) ≤ (1/4) Vol_n(z ∩ B_i).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply the inductive filling inequality to the (n - 1)-cycle ∂(z ∩ B_i). The proposition bounds its volume by E_n (A_n ε)^(1/n) n Vol_n(z ∩ B_i)^((n-1)/n). Raising this to the power n/(n-1) and multiplying by C_{n-1} gives C_{n-1} E_n^(n/(n-1)) (A_n ε)^(1/(n-1)) n^(n/(n-1)) Vol_n(z ∩ B_i). The chosen upper bound on ε is exactly arranged so that this coefficient is at most 1/4. If the resulting filling chain is not contained in B_i, the paper projects it to B_i using a 1-Lipschitz projection, which does not increase volume and fixes the boundary. In L∞, the projection onto the unit ball centered at 0 is the coordinatewise truncation P(f)(y) = sgn(f(y)) min{|f(y)|,1}, with translation and scaling for general balls; in CAT(0)-spaces, the paper uses the orthogonal projection onto the ball.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Inequalities (7) to (9): localized cycles, diameter control, and residual decay
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the setup of the inductive proof, define hat z_i = (z ∩ B_i) - c_i and z' = z - Σ_i hat z_i = (z ∩ (⋃_i B_i)^c) - Σ_i c_i. Then hat z_i and z' are n-cycles. They satisfy 3/4 Vol_n(z ∩ B_i) ≤ Vol_n(hat z_i) ≤ 5/4 Vol_n(z ∩ B_i), diam(hat z_i) ≤ diam(B_i) ≤ (4^(n+1)/(3 A_n ε))^(1/n) Vol_n(hat z_i)^(1/n), and (3/5) Σ_i Vol_n(hat z_i) + Vol_n(z') ≤ Vol_n(z). In addition, Vol_n(z') ≤ (1 - (3/4)5^(-n)) Vol_n(z). Iterating the same construction on z' makes the residual volume as small as desired.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The chains hat z_i are cycles because ∂c_i = ∂(z ∩ B_i), and z' is a cycle because it is obtained from the cycle z by subtracting cycles. The volume comparison for hat z_i follows from the triangle inequality and Vol(c_i) ≤ (1/4) Vol(z ∩ B_i): subtracting c_i can reduce or increase volume by at most that amount. Since c_i has been projected into B_i, the support of hat z_i lies in B_i, giving diam(hat z_i) ≤ diam(B_i). Property (i) of the proposition gives diam(B_i) ≤ 4 (A_n ε)^(-1/n) Vol(z ∩ B_i)^(1/n), and the lower bound Vol(hat z_i) ≥ (3/4) Vol(z ∩ B_i) converts this into the displayed diameter-volume estimate. For the residual, Vol(z') ≤ Vol(z ∩ (⋃B_i)^c) + Σ_i Vol(c_i) ≤ Vol(outside) + (1/4)Vol(inside). Combining this with Vol(hat z_i) ≤ (5/4)Vol(z ∩ B_i) yields (3/5)Σ_i Vol(hat z_i) + Vol(z') ≤ Vol(z). Since the selected balls capture at least 5^(-n) of the volume of z, the residual bound Vol(z') ≤ (1 - (3/4)5^(-n))Vol(z) follows. Repeating the construction reduces the leftover volume by this fixed factor.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Quantitative bound on filling constants
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Evaluating the constants in the inductive proof gives a filling constant satisfying C_n ≤ 27 n D_n E_n C_{n-1}^{(n-1)/n}, where D_n is the cone constant and E_n is the coarea constant from Section 2. In particular, when the volume is Gromov's mass* volume in a Banach space, D_n ≤ 1 and E_n = 1, and the paper states that the isoperimetric constant is bounded above by 27^n n!.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: After the decomposition, fill each localized cycle hat z_i by the cone inequality: Vol(hat c_i) ≤ D_n diam(hat z_i) Vol(hat z_i). The diameter-volume estimate gives Vol(hat c_i) ≤ D_n (4^(n+1)/(3A_n ε))^(1/n) Vol(hat z_i)^(1+1/n). Summing over i and using Σ_i a_i^(1+1/n) ≤ (Σ_i a_i)^(1+1/n), together with the control of Σ_i Vol(hat z_i) by Vol(z), gives a filling bound with a constant depending on D_n and ε^(-1/n). Substituting the chosen ε, which was set by the inductive filling step and contains C_{n-1}^{-(n-1)}, yields the displayed recurrence up to the numerical simplification made in the paper. For Gromov's mass* volume, D_n and E_n are at most 1, and the paper gives the resulting explicit upper bound 27^n n!.

proof_techniques.name: Good-radius selection from coarea and maximal density scale
proof_techniques.description: For each point x in the support of a cycle, the proof defines a maximal radius r_0(x) for which the volume inside B(x,r) is at least A_n ε r^n. Maximality prevents the volume function V(x,r) from growing too fast on the interval from r_0 to 2r_0. Therefore there is a radius r in that interval where V'(x,r) is controlled by n(A_n ε)^(1/n)V(x,r)^((n-1)/n). The coarea formula then turns this derivative control into a bound on the volume of the boundary slice ∂(z ∩ B(x,r)).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a robust way to find slices of controlled boundary area from volume information. In a rectangle k-dilation argument, analogous slicing by distance or coordinate functions could produce hypersurfaces or chains whose images have controlled volume and are suitable for filling or tightening.

proof_techniques.name: Vitali decomposition into disjoint controlled balls
proof_techniques.description: After assigning a scale r_0(x) to almost every point of the cycle, the proof applies Vitali's 5r-covering lemma to select disjoint enlarged balls B(x_i,2r_0(x_i)) whose 5r-enlargements cover the support. Because maximality gives V(x_i,5r_0) < 5^n V(x_i,r_0), the selected disjoint inner balls capture a definite fraction, 5^(-n), of the total volume. This creates finitely many spatially separated regions where the cycle has enough volume relative to the ball diameter.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This suggests decomposing complicated cycles or sweepout slices into localized pieces with controlled geometry. Such localization can help convert area or k-dilation bounds into filling or volume bounds piece by piece.

proof_techniques.name: Inductive cut-fill-replace scheme
proof_techniques.description: Inside each selected ball B_i, the restricted chain z ∩ B_i is not generally a cycle; its boundary is the slice z ∩ ∂B_i. The proof fills this boundary using the inductive isoperimetric inequality in dimension n - 1, obtaining an n-chain c_i whose volume is at most one quarter of Vol(z ∩ B_i). Replacing z ∩ B_i by the cycle hat z_i = (z ∩ B_i) - c_i produces a genuine localized n-cycle while losing only a controlled amount of volume.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Many rectangle distortion arguments require replacing a chain by homologous or relatively homologous chains with better geometry. This technique gives a template: slice, fill the slice boundary cheaply, and isolate cycles whose diameter and volume can be controlled.

proof_techniques.name: Localization of fillings by 1-Lipschitz projection
proof_techniques.description: The inductive filling c_i of ∂(z ∩ B_i) may initially lie outside B_i. The paper projects c_i back to B_i using a 1-Lipschitz projection, which cannot increase parametrized volume and preserves the boundary because the boundary already lies in B_i. In L∞ this projection is coordinatewise truncation; in CAT(0)-spaces it is the orthogonal projection onto a closed ball.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Euclidean rectangles and balls have natural 1-Lipschitz nearest-point projections onto convex subsets. This localization principle may help keep fillings inside prescribed boxes or neighborhoods while preserving volume inequalities needed for degree or relative-boundary arguments.

proof_techniques.name: Coning after diameter-volume control
proof_techniques.description: Once each localized cycle hat z_i is supported in a ball whose diameter is bounded by a constant times Vol(hat z_i)^(1/n), the cone inequality gives a filling volume bounded by a constant times Vol(hat z_i)^(1+1/n). This reduces the global filling estimate to a summation problem, handled by the convexity inequality Σ a_i^α ≤ (Σ a_i)^α for α ≥ 1.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In Euclidean rectangle problems, straight-line coning is available. If one can first produce cycles or fibers whose diameter is controlled by their volume or by rectangle side lengths, coning gives explicit filling-volume bounds that can force contradictions with degree.

proof_techniques.name: Iterative residual reduction
proof_techniques.description: The ball decomposition captures only a fixed fraction of the cycle volume, not all of it. The proof handles this by applying the same decomposition repeatedly to the residual cycle. Each round reduces the residual volume by a uniform factor 1 - (3/4)5^(-n), so after enough rounds the leftover volume is arbitrarily small. Since the leftover remains of bounded diameter, its cone filling has arbitrarily small volume.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is useful when a single slicing or covering step cannot control an entire chain. A rectangle proof might iteratively tighten or decompose a sweepout, discarding a geometrically decreasing error while preserving enough quantitative control to obtain a final contradiction.

other_useful_info: The paper emphasizes that its proof avoids the Federer-Fleming Euclidean isoperimetric inequality and in fact yields a new proof of the Euclidean isoperimetric inequality. It states that the same proof works in any metric space with a cone inequality and 1-Lipschitz projections onto closed balls. The introduction notes that Gromov's original proof applies more broadly to arbitrary Banach spaces and to Riemannian manifolds admitting cone type inequalities, and also works for real coefficients; Wenger 2005 is cited for an extension to complete metric spaces admitting cone type inequalities using Ambrosio-Kirchheim currents. The lower density estimate used in this note is false for real coefficients, clear for piecewise C1 submanifolds, and for Lipschitz cycles is attributed to Kirchheim 1994. For Banach spaces, the parametrized Hausdorff, Holmes-Thompson, and Gromov mass* volumes agree up to a factor at most n^(n/2), so the choice of volume affects only constants.
why_relevant: This paper gives an efficient proof of Gromov's filling inequality for Lipschitz cycles. Filling inequalities are a core ingredient in the tightening and isoperimetric-profile arguments used in rectangle k-dilation problems.

title: Isoperimetry of Waists and Concentration of Maps
overall_summary: Gromov proves sharp waist inequalities for maps from the round sphere to Euclidean space and Gaussian/log-concave analogues, showing that some fiber has neighborhoods at least as large as the model equatorial fiber. The core method is a topological convex-partition or needle-decomposition argument: one partitions the source into convex pieces, uses Borsuk-Ulam/Stiefel-Whitney obstructions to force the chosen centers of all pieces into one fiber, and then integrates one-dimensional isoperimetric comparisons over the partition. The paper develops stronger Z2-parametric versions, stable under products and fibrations, and applies them to cycle families rather than only literal fibers. For the original rectangle/dilation problem, the most useful content is the general philosophy and machinery that degree or homological essentialness plus volume-contraction hypotheses force large slices or sweepout cycles, together with product/fibration and localization techniques that may be adapted to anisotropic rectangular geometries.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Waist of the Sphere Theorem (§1, proved in §5.9)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S^n be the unit round n-sphere with its spherical n-volume. For every continuous map f:S^n→R^k there exists a point z∈R^k such that, writing Y_z=f^{-1}(z) and Y_z+ε for the closed ε-neighborhood of Y_z in S^n, one has Vol_n(Y_z+ε) ≥ Vol_n(S^{n-k}+ε) for every ε>0, where S^{n-k}⊂S^n denotes an equatorial subsphere. In particular, letting ε→0 gives that some level has codimension-k Minkowski volume at least Vol_{n-k}(S^{n-k}).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is based on the convex partition theorem. One first constructs, for a suitable center rule c assigning a point to each convex slice, a convex partition of S^n into low-dimensional convex pieces, together with a value z of f, such that the level f^{-1}(z) meets the chosen center of almost every piece. For finite partitions this is obtained by parameterizing iterated binary partitions by oriented hyperplanes, forming an equivariant imbalance map whose coordinates record volume imbalance and weighted f-center imbalance, and applying a nonvanishing Stiefel-Whitney class to force a zero. Passing to a limiting sequence of partitions gives pieces of the needed dimension. For the round measure, the conditional measures on the convex pieces are convexly derived from spherical measure and have the corresponding sin-concavity. Choosing the center as the maximum point of the conditional density, the one-dimensional comparison lemma implies that, inside each piece, the measure of the ε-ball about the center is at least the model contribution coming from the equatorial S^{n-k}. Since f^{-1}(z) meets these centers, Y_z+ε contains these piecewise ε-balls. Integrating the conditional lower bounds over the partition gives Vol(Y_z+ε) ≥ Vol(S^{n-k}+ε). The spherical case requires a limiting/approximation argument because the center map on lower-dimensional convex sets can have ambiguity; Gromov handles this by approximating f by generic smooth maps and using weak concavity of intersections of balls with convex sets, with the alternative ambiguity case producing infinite Minkowski measure.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Log-Waist Theorem (§3.1, proof in §5.8)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a convex subset of R^n equipped with a probability measure μ whose density satisfies the normalized log-concavity condition used in the paper; in the proof this is the Gaussian-model condition, for smooth densities Hess(log φ)≤-Id. Then for every continuous map f:X→R^k there exists z∈R^k such that μ(f^{-1}(z)+ε) ≥ γ_k(B(0,ε)) for all ε>0, where γ_k is standard Gaussian measure on R^k and γ_k(B(0,ε)) = (∫_0^ε x^{k-1} exp(-x^2/2) dx)/(∫_0^∞ x^{k-1} exp(-x^2/2) dx). If n≥k, the inequality is sharp; equality is achieved for linear maps Ga^n→R^k from standard Gaussian n-space.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The argument parallels the spherical waist proof. A convex partition theorem gives a partition into convex needles and a level f^{-1}(z) meeting the selected center of almost every conditional measure. The relevant center is the point where the conditional density is maximal. Convex derivation preserves the required log-concavity: conditional measures obtained as limits of normalized restrictions to convex domains remain 1-log-concave on their lower-dimensional supports. The main technical issue is that the center rule is naturally defined on lower-dimensional limiting convex sets rather than on all full-dimensional approximating sets. In the Gaussian/log-concave setting, Gromov uses the concavity property that for a 1-log-concave measure μ and convex domains U_1,U_2, the function x↦μ((U_1+x)∩U_2) is 1-log-concave on its positivity set. This allows approximate continuous extensions of the lower-dimensional center rule without losing much mass in ε-balls. On each needle, a one-dimensional comparison for log-concave densities with maximum at the center gives the Gaussian k-ball lower bound. Integrating these bounds over the partition yields the stated waist inequality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Waists of Cartesian powers (§3.2)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a compact connected Riemannian manifold equipped with a probability measure μ=φ(x) dx, where φ is continuous and strictly positive. Then there exists λ=λ(X)>0 such that for every Cartesian power X^N, N=1,2,...,∞, and every k≥0, wst(X^N→R^k,ε) ≥ γ_k(B(0,λ ε)) for all ε>0, where X^N has the product probability measure and the Cartesian/Pythagorean product metric, possibly taking value ∞ in the infinite product case. Equivalently, every continuous map X^N→R^k has a fiber whose ε-neighborhood has measure at least the standard Gaussian k-ball of radius λ ε.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Gromov uses domination of mm-spaces. If X=(X,dist,μ) and X'=(X',dist',μ') are mm-spaces, X is dominated by X' when there is a 1-Lipschitz map ψ:X'→X pushing μ' to μ. Waist lower bounds are monotone under domination, and domination is compatible with Cartesian products. The compact connected measured Riemannian manifold X is dominated by a scaled Gaussian space λ^{-1}Ga^n for all sufficiently large n, where λ depends only on X. Hence X^N is dominated by (λ^{-1}Ga^n)^N, which is a scaled Gaussian space. Applying the log-waist theorem to the Gaussian product and rescaling the radius gives the lower bound γ_k(B(0,λ ε)).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Convex Partition Theorem with centers (§4.4.A, operative form in §§5.7 and 7)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let μ be a Borel probability measure on S^n. Let c_k be a continuous center rule assigning to each exactly k-dimensional convex subset S of S^n, or to each convexly derived probability measure supported on such an S, a point c_k(S)∈S. For lower-dimensional limiting supports, let C(S)⊂S denote the compact set of all limit points obtained by applying c_k along approximating k-dimensional convex sets or measures. Then for every continuous map f:S^n→R^k there exist a value z∈R^k and a measurable convex partition Π of S^n into convex pieces S_π of dimension at most k such that f^{-1}(z) intersects C(S_π) for μ-almost every π. In the measure-theoretic formulation, μ disintegrates as μ=∫_Π μ_π dπ, where μ_π are convexly derived conditional probability measures on S_π, and the level meets the corresponding center set for almost every conditional measure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The theorem is first proved for finite dyadic partitions and continuous centers on full-dimensional convex pieces. The space of depth-i binary partitions is parameterized by choices of oriented hyperplanes at the vertices of a dyadic tree. A finite group of tree automorphisms acts freely on this parameter space. For a chosen center rule, one defines an equivariant map whose coordinates measure, at every vertex, the difference between the volume of the left and right descendants and the difference between their weighted f-center sums. The associated section of a vector bundle over the quotient must vanish by the Stiefel-Whitney nonvanishing lemma. A zero gives a partition with equal masses and common f-value at all chosen centers. To obtain pieces of dimension at most k, one restricts cutting hyperplanes to a dense sequence of lower-dimensional families of directions and passes to an inverse limit; any limiting piece with dimension greater than k is cut infinitely often in almost normal directions, forcing its width in that dimension to shrink to zero. For center maps defined only on k-dimensional limiting pieces, Gromov resolves ambiguity by a cohomological selection argument using ellipsoid/principal-axis approximations of measures; the possible choices form cycles whose projection remains mod-2 essential. This yields the asserted partition for general continuous f.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Non-Vanishing Lemma and Vanishing Corollary for partition bundles (§5.1-§5.2)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a positive integer i, let P_i be the space of depth-i binary convex partitions of S^n obtained by successively cutting pieces by oriented hyperplanes; P_i is naturally (H^n)^{2^i-1}, where H^n is the sphere of oriented hyperplanes. Let G_i be the automorphism group of the depth-i dyadic tree, acting freely on P_i, and let Q_i=P_i/G_i. Let L_i→Q_i be the vector bundle built as the Whitney sum of the tautological line-bundle contributions from all levels of the tree. For every m≤n, if N=m(2^i-1), the top Stiefel-Whitney class w_N(L_i^m) of the m-fold Whitney power of L_i is nonzero. Consequently, every continuous section Q_i→L_i^m has a zero; moreover, the zero set of a section supports a nonzero class in H_N(Q_i;Z2).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on the depth i. The quotient Q_i fibers over Q_{i-1}; the fiber is a Cartesian power (P^n)^{2^{i-1}} of real projective spaces, corresponding to the new hyperplanes at the last level. The top Stiefel-Whitney class of the last-level tautological summand restricts to the generator of the top cohomology of this fiber. By the induction hypothesis, the top Stiefel-Whitney class of the pullback of the previous-level bundle is nonzero on the base. Their cup product is therefore nonzero on Q_i. Equivalently, the dual cycles may be represented by a section corresponding to a monodromy-fixed diagonal point in the fiber and by a fiber over the base, and they intersect transversely in one point mod 2. The vanishing corollary is the standard obstruction-theoretic consequence: a nowhere-zero section would force the top Stiefel-Whitney class to vanish.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Finite equal-volume common-center partition corollary (§5.3)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S denote the space of n-dimensional geodesically convex subsets of S^n, each contained in a hemisphere, with the Hausdorff topology. Let c:S→S^n be continuous with c(S)∈S. If f:S^n→R^k is continuous and 1≤k≤n-1, then for every depth i≥1 there exists a dyadic convex partition Π∈P_i of S^n into 2^i convex pieces S_π such that all pieces have equal n-volume Vol(S^n)/2^i and f(c(S_π)) is the same point of R^k for every piece S_π in the partition.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each oriented final cutting hyperplane h, let S be the chosen side and S^⊥ its sibling. Define v(h)=Vol(S) and φ(h)=v(h) f(c(S)). For an internal hyperplane h^-, define v(h^-) and φ(h^-) as the sums of these quantities over all final leaves in the left subtree. The map F from the partition parameter space P_i to the direct sum over the 2^i-1 vertices of one scalar volume coordinate and k weighted f-coordinates sends each vertex to the difference between its left and right data: (v(h)-v(-h), φ(h)-φ(-h)). This map is equivariant for the tree automorphism group G_i, hence descends to a section of L_i^{k+1} over Q_i. Since k+1≤n, the non-vanishing lemma forces a zero. At a zero, every cut bisects the total volume of its descendants and their weighted f-center sums. Induction down the tree gives equal volumes for all leaves and equality of f(c(S_π)) for all leaves.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Restricted hyperplane and limiting low-dimensional partition corollaries (§5.6-§5.7)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix equatorial m-spheres H^m_j⊂H^n in the sphere H^n of oriented hyperplanes. If m≥k+1, then the finite partition corollary remains true with the additional constraint that every cutting hyperplane used at level j lies in H^m_j. If the sequence H^m_j is chosen dense in the space of all such m-spheres, then one obtains a limiting convex partition Π of S^n into convex sets S_π of dimension at most k such that, for a suitable limiting interpretation of c(S_π), all values f(c(S_π)) are equal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the finite constrained statement, the same equivariant-section construction is carried out with the parameter space P_i(S^m) rather than P_i(S^n); the Stiefel-Whitney nonvanishing lemma applies because m≥k+1. For the limiting statement, take a projective limit of finite constrained partitions with a dense sequence of allowed direction spheres. If a limiting convex piece had approximate dimension ℓ>k, then the density of the allowed cutting directions would force it to be cut infinitely often by hyperplanes almost normal to some ℓ-dimensional direction in the piece. Its ℓ-dimensional width would therefore tend to zero, a contradiction. Thus all limiting pieces have dimension at most k. The equality of f-values passes to the limit by continuity, using limit points of centers of approximating finite pieces.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Elementary one-dimensional comparison lemma and log-concave intersection concavity (§4.4 and §5.8.A)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The paper uses the following one-dimensional comparison. Let φ_0(t), 0≤t≤t_0≤∞, be smooth, positive, and monotone decreasing, with (log φ_0)''≤0 and (log φ_0)'' monotone decreasing. Let λ(τ)>0 for 0≤τ≤τ_0<t_0, and define ψ(t)=inf_{0≤τ≤τ_0} λ(τ) φ_0(t+τ) for 0≤t≤t_0-τ_0. Then for every ε, (∫_0^ε ψ(t) dt)/(∫_0^{t_0-τ_0} ψ(t) dt) ≥ (∫_0^ε φ_0(t) dt)/(∫_0^{t_0} φ_0(t) dt). The spherical application uses φ_0(t)=cos t on [0,π/2], and the log-concave application uses a Gaussian model density. Separately, if μ is a 1-log-concave measure on a convex subset of R^n and U_1,U_2 are convex domains, then the function x↦μ((U_1+x)∩U_2) is 1-log-concave on the set where it is positive.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the one-dimensional lemma, Gromov calls the proof straightforward: the lower envelope of scaled translates of the model density remains sufficiently dominated near the tail because of the monotonicity of the logarithmic curvature, and normalization then shows that the initial interval [0,ε] has at least the model fraction of total mass. This is the 1-dimensional input that turns sin-concavity or log-concavity of needle measures into the spherical or Gaussian waist functions. For the translated-intersection concavity, if y=(x_1+x_2)/2, then (U_1+y)∩U_2 contains the Minkowski half-sum of (U_1+x_1)∩U_2 and (U_1+x_2)∩U_2. The Brunn-Minkowski inequality for log-concave measures then gives the required 1-log-concavity of the intersection measure as a function of the translate.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Borsuk-Ulam partition theorem for families of cycles (§6.1-§6.2)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A Borsuk-Ulam family of codimension-k subsets in S^n is specified by a topological space X_hat, a continuous map p:X_hat→S^n such that H^n(S^n;Z2)→H^n(X_hat;Z2) is injective, and a continuous map f:X_hat→Z, where Z is a k-dimensional Z2-homology manifold. For z∈Z set Y_z=p(f^{-1}(z))⊂S^n. Given center data C_π as in the convex partition theorem and assuming k<n, there exist z∈Z and a convex partition Π of S^n into pieces of dimension at most k such that Y_z intersects C_π⊂S_π for almost every π. For k=n the paper says additional assumptions are needed, for example that Z splits as Z_0×R.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: In the smooth generic model, consider the pullback of the diagonal Δ_Z⊂Z×Z under f×f:X_hat×X_hat→Z×Z. This gives a relative (2n-k)-cycle in X_hat×X_hat minus the diagonal, equivariant under the transposition Z2-action. Next take a round k-sphere S^k⊂S^n and consider in X_hat×X_hat the pullback under p×p of the set of pairs of opposite points in S^k. The key intersection assertion is that, if p has nonzero mod-2 degree and k<n, then the quotients of these two cycles by Z2 have nonzero mod-2 intersection. For a small S^k this is exactly the homological content of the Borsuk-Ulam theorem; since k<n, all round k-spheres are homotopic in S^n, so the intersection number is invariant. This nonzero intersection replaces the single-valued zero of the finite partition section and allows the convex partition argument to be carried out for multivalued cycle families. General topological cases are handled by the same cohomological formulation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Volume-contracting Borsuk-Ulam fiber lower bound (§6.3.C)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X_hat be an n-dimensional Riemannian manifold and let p:X_hat→S^n have nonzero Z2-degree. Suppose p is (n-k)-volume contracting; the text gives as a smooth sufficient condition that the appropriate exterior-power norm of dp on the relevant vectors is at most one. Let f:X_hat→Z be continuous, where Z is a k-dimensional Z2-homology manifold. Then some fiber f^{-1}(z), z∈Z, has k-codimensional Minkowski volume at least the volume of the equatorial S^{n-k}⊂S^n. If the fibers are rectifiable, the paper says the (n-k)-dimensional Hausdorff measure may be used instead of Minkowski volume.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply the Borsuk-Ulam partition theorem to the family Y_z=p(f^{-1}(z))⊂S^n. The same argument as in the waist theorem gives a lower bound on the codimension-k Minkowski waist of the family {Y_z}: some Y_z has Minkowski volume at least Vol(S^{n-k}). Since p contracts (n-k)-dimensional volume, the image p(f^{-1}(z)) cannot have larger (n-k)-volume than f^{-1}(z). Therefore the corresponding fiber in X_hat has at least the same lower bound.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Parametric Borsuk-Ulam partition theorem (§7.1)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let α:X→B be an n-sphere bundle. Let C_k(X) be the space of exactly k-dimensional convex subsets in the spherical fibers X_b=α^{-1}(b), and let c_k:C_k(X)→X be a continuous center map with c_k(S)∈S. Let p:X_hat→X be Z2-onto, meaning that for every pair of compact subsets X_1⊂X_2⊂X the cohomology map H^*(X_2,X_1;Z2)→H^*(p^{-1}(X_2),p^{-1}(X_1);Z2) is injective. Let β:Z→B be a fibration whose fibers Z_b are k-dimensional Z2-homology manifolds, and let f:X_hat→Z be fiberwise over B. For each b, let Z'_b⊂Z_b be the set of z such that Y_z=p_b(f_b^{-1}(z))⊂X_b meets the center sets C_π of some convex partition of X_b. Then Z'=∪_b Z'_b maps to B by β' with β':Z'→B Z2-onto.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each fixed b, the nonparametric Borsuk-Ulam partition theorem shows that Z'_b is nonempty. The proof of that theorem is cohomological and functorial: the relevant diagonal-intersection and Stiefel-Whitney obstruction classes persist under restriction to compact pairs in the base. Therefore the collection of good values z varies homologically continuously with b. This gives injectivity on relative Z2-cohomology for the projection Z'→B, which is exactly the Z2-onto condition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Z2-parametric waist product and fibration inequalities (§3.3, §7.3-§7.4)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The paper defines Z2-parametric waists by requiring that the set of good parameters z, for which the fiber neighborhood bound holds, maps Z2-onto the base of every parameter family. With this strengthened notion, products and certain fibrations preserve waist lower bounds. In particular, for the sup product metric on X×X', the paper states the pointwise product inequality Z2-pawst(X×X'→R^k,ε) ≥ Z2-pawst(X→R^k,ε) · Z2-pawst(X'→R^k,ε). For the Gibbs-Laplace transform Gi(Y;β)=∫_X exp(-β dist(x,Y)) dμ(x), ordinary Cartesian or Riemannian products satisfy Z2-paGiwst(X×X'→R^k,β) ≥ Z2-paGiwst(X→R^k,β) · Z2-paGiwst(X'→R^k,β). The paper also describes the analogous statement for metric fibrations X'→X under suitable compatibility assumptions on the metric and conditional measures.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Suppose X' is fibered over X with conditional probability measures on fibers X'_x. A fiberwise Z2-waist lower bound gives, for each x, a homological selection of parameters z=z(x) for which the slice inside X'_x has large ε-neighborhood measure. Because the selection is generally multivalued, it is treated as a Z2-morphism X→Z. Applying the base Z2-waist bound to this Z2-morphism produces a large subset Y⊂X on which the selected fiber slices are large. Under the sup product metric, the ε-neighborhood of the total selected set contains the product of the base ε-neighborhood and the fiber ε-neighborhoods, so the measures multiply. For Gibbs waists, the exponential distance integrals factor or are bounded from below multiplicatively under the product/Riemannian distance structure. The parametric Z2 formulation is what guarantees that these choices can be made coherently over arbitrary bases.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Subdivision Lemma for Z2-waists (§7.5)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let μ be a measure with positive continuous density on S^n. Let H be the space of oriented hyperplanes h that divide S^n into two halves X_h^+ and X_h^- of equal μ-measure. These halves form two families X^+→H and X^-→H. If the Z2-waists of these half-sphere families over H have lower bounds w^+(h,ε) and w^-(h,ε), then the Z2-waist of (S^n,μ) satisfies Z2-wst((S^n,μ)→Z,ε) ≥ inf_{h∈H}(w^+(h,ε)+w^-(h,ε)). Iterating this subdivision reduces waist estimates to estimates for convexly derived measures on low-dimensional convex subsets.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each bisecting hyperplane h, one applies the parametric waist statement to the two half-sphere families. The Borsuk-Ulam theorem forces a choice of h for which the relevant parameter values on the two sides coincide in the target. The corresponding level in the whole sphere is the union of the two selected half-levels, and because the halves partition the measure, their ε-neighborhood measure contributions add. Repetition of the bisection process produces increasingly fine convex partitions; in the limit the conditional measures are convexly derived measures, which are then handled by one-dimensional comparison.
