key_theorems_lemmas.label: Degree-d faithful families, induced families, and semiadditivity (§8.1-§8.3)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A family {Y⊂X} is Z2-faithful in codimension k if it admits a Z2-faithful k-parametrization by a parameter space Z, meaning roughly that through every point of X there is a homologically essential choice of a member of the family. It is Z2-faithful of degree d in codimension k if the family {Y^d⊂X^d} is Z2-faithful in codimension kd. Examples include algebraic families where a generic d-tuple of points lies on a finite odd number of members. If {Y} is faithful in Z and g:X→Z is a continuous map, then the induced family {g^{-1}(Y)} in X is faithful with the same codimension and degree; this remains true for Z2-morphisms. For the corresponding degree-d waist invariant wst^d_{-k}, if X_1 and X_2 are disjoint open subsets of X and d_1+d_2=d, then wst^d_{-k}(X) ≥ wst^{d_1}_{-k}(X_1)+wst^{d_2}_{-k}(X_2).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Faithfulness is formulated in the Z2-parametric category, so it is stable under pullback by maps or Z2-morphisms. For semiadditivity, a degree-d family over X restricts to degree-d_1 and degree-d_2 data over the disjoint regions X_1 and X_2. Since the regions are disjoint, the relevant Minkowski or neighborhood measures add. The Z2-parametric definition ensures that the homological essentialness of the parameter choices on the pieces combines into the degree-d essentialness on X. Gromov notes that applying this repeatedly to a partition into d small pieces yields Weyl-type lower asymptotics for degree-d Minkowski waists, with constants depending only on n and k.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Convex localization isoperimetric inequality (§9.2.A) and spherical/cone corollaries (§9.2.B-§9.2.C)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let μ be a probability measure on S^n, and let dist be a metric on S^n. For every convexly derived measure μ' supported on a minimal geodesic segment S⊂S^n, consider the isoperimetric profile of the one-dimensional mm-space (S,dist|_S,μ'). Define v(s) as the supremum of the corresponding volume profiles and s(v) as the infimum of the corresponding boundary profiles over all such derived segment measures. Then the isoperimetric profile of X=(S^n,dist,μ) satisfies v_X(s)≤v(s) and s_X(v)≥s(v). As a corollary, for the standard round sphere, the distance to a point is isoperimetrically extremal; the same holds in every convex sector containing the point and its antipode. Passing to the scaling limit gives that in a convex cone X⊂R^n, the distance to the origin is isoperimetrically extremal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Given a domain U⊂S^n, one constructs a convex partition of S^n into geodesic segments S such that the conditional probability μ_S satisfies μ_S(U∩S)=μ(U) for almost every segment. The measures μ_S are convexly derived from μ. Applying the one-dimensional isoperimetric inequality on almost every segment and integrating over the partition gives the global profile bound. For the round sphere, convexly derived measures on geodesic segments are sin^{n-1}-concave and are iso-dominated by the normalized density sin^{n-1}(t) dt on [0,π], so the spherical cap profile follows. The Euclidean cone statement is obtained by rescaling large spheres and taking the limit.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Schwarz symmetrization product and fibration theorems (§9.4.C-§9.4.D)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X_i=(X_i,dist_i,μ_i), i=1,...,r, be Riemannian manifolds with smooth measures, and suppose each X_i is iso-dominated by a measure ν_i on R, meaning that the isoperimetric profile of X_i is no better than that of (R,ν_i) in the monotone rearrangement sense. Then the isoperimetric profile of the product X=X_1×...×X_r is restricted by that of X'=(R,ν_1)×...×(R,ν_r): v_X(s)≤v_X'(s) and s_X(v)≥s_X'(v). More generally, let p:X→X_bar be a smooth fibration of measured Riemannian spaces such that the total measure disintegrates over the base, the conditional fiber measures are invariant under normal parallel transport, and p is 1-co-Lipschitz in the sense that the Hausdorff distance between fibers is at most the base distance. If X_bar is iso-dominated by ν_1 and all fibers are iso-dominated by ν_2, then X is isoperimetrically restricted by (R,ν_1)×(R,ν_2).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof uses fiberwise Schwarz symmetrization. Given p:X→B and a comparison space X'=B×R with line fibers carrying measures μ'_b, replace each slice U∩X_b by an initial interval (-∞,t_b] in the line fiber with the same conditional measure. For sufficiently regular boundaries, the boundary measure can be written as an integral over the base of a fiber boundary term multiplied by a slope factor Δ. The hypotheses ensure that symmetrization preserves the relevant horizontal behavior and that the slope factor after symmetrization is bounded by a convex function of the averaged original slope. Jensen's inequality then gives Min_{n-1}(∂U')≤Min_{n-1}(∂U) while preserving total volume. Iterating over product factors reduces the product isoperimetric problem to the product of line models. The fibration version is the same argument with normal parallel transport replacing literal product translations and the 1-co-Lipschitz condition controlling base directions.

proof_techniques.name: Waist proof by convex needles and centers
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly reduces a global waist estimate to a family of one-dimensional or low-dimensional estimates by partitioning the source into convex pieces, or needles, and disintegrating the measure over the partition. A topological argument forces a single fiber or cycle Y_z to pass through the selected center of almost every needle. If the conditional measure on each needle has a density with suitable concavity, the measure of an ε-ball around the center has a universal lower bound. Integrating these lower bounds over all needles gives the global neighborhood-volume lower bound for Y_z.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For rectangle maps, one can try to build a sweepout of the domain or target by low-dimensional slices and prove that degree one forces some image/preimage cycle to hit selected centers in many fibers. The integration of local slice estimates is a plausible route to lower bounds involving side-length products.

proof_techniques.name: Equivariant dyadic partitions and Stiefel-Whitney obstruction
proof_techniques.description: Finite convex partitions are produced by iterated bisection with oriented hyperplanes. The parameter space is a product of spheres with a free action of the automorphism group of a dyadic tree. Volume and center-value imbalances define an equivariant section of a vector bundle over the quotient. A nonzero top Stiefel-Whitney class forces the section to vanish, yielding a balanced partition in which all centers map to the same point.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a robust way to manufacture balanced partitions without solving variational equations. In a rectangular setting, analogous cubical or hyperplane bisection schemes might be used to create sweepouts whose cells have controlled volumes and whose center data are forced into a common fiber by topology.

proof_techniques.name: Convex derivation and one-dimensional comparison
proof_techniques.description: When convex partitions degenerate, the conditional measures on limiting pieces are convexly derived measures. Their densities inherit concavity properties from the original measure, such as sin-concavity on the sphere or 1-log-concavity in the Gaussian setting. The paper then applies elementary one-dimensional comparison lemmas to show that balls about density maxima have at least the model mass.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Anisotropic rectangles have product structure rather than spherical symmetry, but the same localization principle suggests reducing estimates to intervals or coordinate rectangles where explicit side-length-dependent inequalities can be proved.

proof_techniques.name: Borsuk-Ulam families and Z2-morphisms for cycle sweepouts
proof_techniques.description: Gromov generalizes from literal fibers f^{-1}(z) to families of cycles Y_z=p(f^{-1}(z)) where p:X_hat→S^n is mod-2 essential. The proof uses the mod-2 intersection of a pulled-back diagonal cycle with an opposite-point cycle, which is the homological content of Borsuk-Ulam. This formulation tolerates multivalued selections and singular parameter spaces through the language of Z2-morphisms.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A degree-one map between manifolds with boundary naturally produces homologically essential families of preimage or image cycles. The Z2-cycle-family viewpoint may bypass regularity difficulties from piecewise smooth maps and allow sweepout lower bounds to be applied to essential cycle families rather than only smooth fibers.

proof_techniques.name: Volume-contraction transfer
proof_techniques.description: In §6.3.C, once a waist lower bound is known for the image family p(f^{-1}(z)) in a model space, a volume-contracting map p transfers the lower bound back to f^{-1}(z), because the image cannot have larger volume than the preimage in the relevant dimension. This converts topological essentialness plus metric contraction into a quantitative lower bound for some fiber.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original hypothesis Dil_2(f)≤1 is a metric contraction condition on two-dimensional areas. A useful strategy is to compose with projections or build auxiliary maps so that a waist or sweepout lower bound in a model space forces a large two-dimensional or codimension-two object, then compare via the 2-dilation bound.

proof_techniques.name: Parametric Z2-waists and homological selection
proof_techniques.description: The paper strengthens waist inequalities by requiring not just existence of a good fiber in each member of a family, but that the collection of good parameters maps Z2-onto the base. This makes good fibers selectable in a homological, possibly multivalued way over parameter spaces. Such parametric control is what permits product and fibration arguments.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Rectangle arguments often involve varying slices over one or more coordinate parameters. Parametric waist control could ensure that lower bounds persist coherently as slices vary, which is important when turning local slice estimates into global inequalities involving several side lengths.

proof_techniques.name: Product and fibration waist bootstrapping
proof_techniques.description: Using Z2-parametric waists, Gromov proves product inequalities: a lower waist bound in the base and a lower waist bound in the fibers combine multiplicatively for sup-product metrics, and Gibbsian analogues combine for ordinary product metrics. The proof first obtains a homological selection of good fiber parameters and then applies the base waist to that selection.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Rectangles are products of intervals with very different lengths. Product waist bootstrapping is a natural template for deriving anisotropic lower bounds, where different coordinate directions contribute different factors such as S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4.

proof_techniques.name: Subdivision into bisected halves
proof_techniques.description: The subdivision lemma cuts a measured sphere by a hyperplane that bisects the measure and relates the waist of the whole to the sum of waists of the two halves. Iterating this procedure leads to convexly derived measures on small convex pieces, providing a recursive proof of waist inequalities.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A rectangular proof may benefit from repeatedly bisecting along coordinate or adapted hyperplanes, producing smaller boxes or slabs where local inequalities are easier and then adding the resulting lower bounds.

proof_techniques.name: Degree-d semiadditivity
proof_techniques.description: For faithful families of degree d, the paper proves a semiadditivity inequality over disjoint open subsets: the degree-d waist of the union is at least the sum of degree-d_i waists on the pieces when the degrees add. This formalizes the idea that independent essential constraints in disjoint regions contribute additively to waist lower bounds.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a degree-one rectangle map can be decomposed into essential contributions over disjoint slabs or cells, a semiadditive waist principle could help convert many local lower bounds into a global volume or area obstruction.

proof_techniques.name: Schwarz symmetrization over fibers
proof_techniques.description: The isoperimetric part of the paper develops a fiberwise symmetrization method. One replaces each fiber slice of a set by an initial interval in a model line with the same conditional measure and proves that boundary measure does not increase, using a slope formula and convexity. Iterating over factors reduces product isoperimetric problems to products of one-dimensional models.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Many rectangle estimates require sharp or near-sharp filling and boundary-volume inequalities in products. Fiberwise symmetrization can reduce such estimates to one-dimensional side-length calculations, which may be useful for proving the specific rectangular alternatives.

other_useful_info: The paper emphasizes that ordinary waist lower bounds have Z2-parametric refinements, and those refinements are essential for stability under products, fibrations, and induced cycle families. The sphere waist theorem gives Minkowski-volume lower bounds for continuous maps; Gromov notes that Hausdorff-measure lower bounds for general continuous maps are unclear, although generic smooth maps can be handled by Almgren-Morse theory as cited in [Gr5]. Section 6.3.D gives a filling-radius application: if an (n-k)-cycle in R^{n+1} has Fill Rad at least 1, radial projection to a unit sphere and the Borsuk-Ulam partition theorem yield a 1-Lipschitz map to S^n whose image has ε-neighborhoods at least as large as the equatorial model, giving an elementary route to Gehring-type linking lower bounds. Section 3.5 records the standard concentration consequence: a waist inequality for X→R^k implies that every 1-Lipschitz pushforward measure to R^k is concentrated around some point with the same lower function. The OCR text supplied for §4.4 uses dimension/codimension terminology inconsistently; the operative formulation in §§5.7 and 7 uses exactly k-dimensional convex slices for maps to k-dimensional parameter spaces, which is the version used in the waist proofs. Related references explicitly cited by the paper that may matter for rectangle/sweepout problems include Gromov [Gr5] on filling Riemannian manifolds and systolic/filling inequalities, Gromov-Milman [GrM] on convex localization and isoperimetry, and Ledoux [L] on concentration of measure.
why_relevant: This paper develops waist and sweepout lower bounds for fibers/cycles of maps. Such estimates are central to proving that a degree-one, area-contracting map must have sufficiently large slices or cycle families in the target rectangle.

title: Filling Riemannian Manifolds
overall_summary: Gromov introduces the filling radius and filling volume of a Riemannian manifold by embedding it isometrically into the Banach space L^∞ of distance functions, and proves dimension-dependent filling inequalities for cycles in Banach spaces, especially L^∞. The central technical tools are Federer-Fleming cubical deformation, cone and coarea inequalities, decomposition of cycles into round pieces, and the injectivity/compressing properties of L^∞ and related mass* volumes. The paper also proves and generalizes Besicovitch-type inverse isoperimetric inequalities for cubes, giving lower bounds for volume in terms of distances between opposite faces. For the rectangle 2-dilation problem, the most useful content is likely the controlled filling of low-dimensional cycles, slicing/sweepout lower bounds via filling radius, and Besicovitch cube estimates connecting degree and side-face separation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 1.1, canonical embedding into L^∞ and extension property
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let V be a metric space, in particular a closed Riemannian manifold with its distance function. Let L^∞(V) be the Banach space of bounded Borel functions on V with the sup norm. The map I: V → L^∞(V) defined by I(v)(w) = dist(v,w) is an isometric embedding. Moreover, if Y is a subspace of a metric space X and φ: Y → L^∞(V) is distance-nonincreasing, then φ extends to a distance-nonincreasing map Φ: X → L^∞(V). Consequently, any distance-nonincreasing degree-one map V1 → V2 between closed manifolds does not increase filling radius in the sense that FillRad(V2) ≤ FillRad(V1), and for any isometric embedding V ⊂ X one has FillRad(V ⊂ X) ≥ FillRad(V).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The isometry of I follows from the triangle inequality: |dist(v1,w) − dist(v2,w)| ≤ dist(v1,v2) for all w, and equality is attained by taking w = v1 or w = v2. For the extension property, write an element of L^∞(V) as a function of v ∈ V and define Φ(x)(v) = inf over y ∈ Y of φ(y)(v) + dist(x,y). This formula agrees with φ on Y and is 1-Lipschitz in x by the triangle inequality. If f: V1 → V2 is 1-Lipschitz and degree one, the extension property gives a 1-Lipschitz map L^∞(V1) → L^∞(V2) extending f; applying it to any filling of V1 in an ε-neighborhood sends the fundamental class to a filling of V2 in an ε-neighborhood. The assertion for arbitrary isometric embeddings follows by extending the identity map V → L^∞(V) from V ⊂ X to X.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2.A, main filling-radius inequality
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every integer n there is a universal constant const'_n > 0 such that every closed connected n-dimensional Riemannian manifold V satisfies FillRad(V) ≤ const'_n Vol(V)^(1/n). The paper gives an explicit dimension-dependent upper bound for const'_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is postponed to Sections 4.2 and 4.3. Embed V isometrically in L^∞(V). First prove an isoperimetric inequality in L^∞: every n-cycle z has a filling volume bounded by C_n Vol(z)^((n+1)/n). Then take an almost volume-minimizing filling c of V and consider the distance function d from the support of V. By the coarea inequality, the volumes of slices c ∩ d^{-1}(t) control the derivative of the volume of c ∩ d^{-1}[0,t]. If every level up to radius R had non-negligible volume, the filling volume would be too large; hence before a controlled radius there is a very small residual cycle, which is filled separately by a Federer-Fleming small-volume filling. This produces a filling supported in a controlled neighborhood of V, giving the filling-radius bound.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1.2.B and inequality (1.2), systole controlled by filling radius
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let V be a closed connected Riemannian manifold, let K be an aspherical space, and let f: V → K be continuous. Suppose all closed curves in V of length less than l are mapped by f to contractible curves in K. Let P be a simplicial polyhedron containing V as a subpolyhedron and contained in the ε-neighborhood of V inside L^∞(V). If l > 6ε, then f extends continuously to P. Consequently, if V is an essential closed n-manifold, then sys_1(V) ≤ 6 FillRad(V), where sys_1(V) is the length of the shortest noncontractible closed curve.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Subdivide P so that every simplex has diameter less than l/2 − 2ε. For each vertex p of P choose a nearby point v(p) ∈ V at distance at most ε. Adjacent vertices in a simplex map to points of V at distance less than l/2, so join them by short segments in V; this gives a retraction of the 1-skeleton P^1 to V. The boundary of every 2-simplex maps to a loop in V of length less than l, hence its image under f is contractible in K. Since K is aspherical, extension over the 2-skeleton implies extension over all of P. If V bounds in an ε-neighborhood with ε below FillRad(V) plus a small error, one constructs such a P with the fundamental class killed in H_n(P). For an essential map f with f_*[V] nonzero, extension to P is impossible; therefore the hypothesis that all short loops are killed must fail for l just above 6 FillRad(V).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 2, definition and basic properties of filling volume
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a singular simplex σ: Δ^(n+1) → X in a metric space, its volume is defined as the infimum of the volumes of Riemannian metrics on Δ^(n+1) for which σ is distance-nonincreasing. The volume of a chain is the sum of the volumes of its simplices with absolute coefficients. The filling volume FillVol(z) of an n-cycle z in X is the infimum of Vol(c) over chains c with ∂c = z. For a closed manifold V with a metric, FillVol(V) means FillVol(V ⊂ L^∞(V)). If W is a compact Riemannian manifold with boundary, then FillVol(∂W) ≤ Vol(W), where ∂W carries the induced metric.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The definition is metric and works with integer, real, or Z_2 coefficients. For a compact Riemannian W with boundary, the isometric embedding ∂W → L^∞(∂W) extends to a distance-nonincreasing map W → L^∞(∂W) by the L^∞ extension property. The image of W is a chain filling ∂W and has volume no larger than Vol(W).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.1.A, volume-decreasing extension to Euclidean space
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Y be a subspace of a metric space X, and let f: Y → R^(n+1) be distance-nonincreasing. Then f extends to a Lipschitz map F: X → R^(n+1) such that dist(F(x1),F(x2)) ≤ sqrt(n+1) dist(x1,x2) for all x1,x2 ∈ X and such that F decreases the volumes of all (n+1)-dimensional chains. As a consequence, if W is a simply connected Riemannian-flat compact (n+1)-manifold with boundary, then FillVol(∂W) = Vol(W).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The extension is obtained by first extending f into an L^∞ space and then using the Banach-space compressing/projection result for mass* or volume in dimension n+1. The sqrt(n+1) Lipschitz loss comes from comparing the Euclidean norm to an extremal l^∞ norm, while the volume in dimension n+1 is not increased. For a flat simply connected W, a locally isometric map W → R^(n+1) is distance-nonincreasing on ∂W. Any filling of ∂W in L^∞(∂W) maps by the proposition to an (n+1)-chain in R^(n+1) spanning the Euclidean image of ∂W, so its volume is at least the Euclidean volume of W. The reverse inequality FillVol(∂W) ≤ Vol(W) is the basic boundary-filling inequality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.2.A, filling volume as minimal volume of Riemannian fillings
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let V be a connected oriented closed manifold of dimension greater than 2 with a metric dist_0. Let W be a manifold with ∂W = V, possibly noncompact but equipped only with complete Riemannian metrics g satisfying dist_g(v1,v2) ≥ dist_0(v1,v2) for all boundary points v1,v2 ∈ V. Let M(V,W) be the infimum of Vol(W,g) over such metrics. Then M(V,W) = FillVol(V), and in particular this infimum is independent of the topology of W, subject to the hypotheses.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: One inequality follows by extending the boundary embedding V → L^∞(V) to W: any admissible filling metric on W gives a distance-nonincreasing map W → L^∞(V), hence a filling chain of volume at most Vol(W,g). For the reverse inequality, developed in Appendix 2, a nearly minimizing filling chain in L^∞ is thickened or modeled by a Riemannian pseudomanifold/cylinder with boundary V, preserving boundary distances and with volume arbitrarily close to the chain volume. The dimension and connectedness assumptions avoid low-dimensional and disconnected counterexamples discussed in the paper.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.1.A, Federer-Fleming deformation to an (n−1)-skeleton
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let V be an n-dimensional submanifold of Euclidean space R^N. There exists a continuous map f from V into an (n−1)-dimensional subpolyhedron K^(n−1) of R^N such that dist(f(v),v) ≤ C_N Vol(V)^(1/n) for all v ∈ V, where C_N is a universal constant depending on N and n; the paper gives C_N < (1/2) sqrt(N) (N!/(n!(N−n)!))^(1/n). Consequently FillRad(V ⊂ R^N) and Rad_(n−1)(V ⊂ R^N) are bounded above by C_N Vol(V)^(1/n).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Decompose R^N into unit cubes. Repeatedly radially project each cube from its center to its boundary, then project faces to lower-dimensional skeleta, obtaining a map P_(n−1) to the (n−1)-skeleton which is continuous away from a dual grid of codimension n. Let Q(V) be the sum of the n-dimensional volumes of the coordinate projections of V. If Q(V) < 1, a translate of V avoids the dual grid, so P_(n−1) is continuous on that translate. The projection moves points by at most half the cube diameter. Scaling reduces the general volume case to Q(V) < 1. The filling-radius corollary is obtained by using the cylinder between the identity map on V and f.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3.2 and Remark 3.2.A, Federer-Fleming isoperimetric inequality with support control in R^N
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every N and n there is a constant C_N such that every n-dimensional cycle z in R^N satisfies FillVol(z) ≤ C_N Vol(z)^((n+1)/n). Moreover, the Federer-Fleming construction gives a filling chain contained in an ε-neighborhood of the support of z, with ε ≤ const_N Vol(z)^(1/n); equivalently, for this ε one has FillVol(z inside U_ε(supp z)) ≤ C_N Vol(z)^((n+1)/n).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the standard cubical grid and its n-skeleton. After translating z so that it avoids the dual grid in an averaged sense, project z to the n-skeleton by the Federer-Fleming radial projection P_n; the volume of the projected cycle is bounded by a constant times Vol(z), and the cylinder from z to P_n(z) has controlled volume and lies at controlled distance. The projected cycle in the cubical n-skeleton is homologous, with zero (n+1)-volume error, to a union of whole n-cubes. That cubical cycle has diameter bounded in terms of its number of cubes and hence in terms of Vol(z), so it is filled by a cone in the n-skeleton. Scaling gives the exponent (n+1)/n, and the construction keeps the support within the corresponding Vol(z)^(1/n)-neighborhood.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3.3, cone inequality and coarea inequality for cycles in Euclidean space
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For an n-dimensional cycle z in R^N, FillVol(z) ≤ (1/(n+1)) Diam(z) Vol(z), where Diam(z) means the diameter of the support of z. If d(x) = dist(x,H) for a subset H ⊂ R^N and b(T) = z ∩ d^{-1}[0,T], a(t) = Vol_(n−1)(z ∩ d^{-1}(t)), and β(T) = Vol_n(b(T)), then the coarea inequality gives β(T) ≥ ∫_0^T a(t) dt for all T ≥ 0, after the usual regularization of d or z.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The cone inequality fills z by the cone from a point of the support of z: the radial parameter contributes a factor 1/(n+1), while the radius is at most the diameter. The coarea inequality is the standard coarea formula applied to the 1-Lipschitz distance function d restricted to the rectifiable cycle z; for nonsmooth levels one approximates d and z by regular objects.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3.3, dimension-only Euclidean isoperimetric inequality
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every n there is a constant C_n, independent of the ambient dimension N, such that every closed n-dimensional submanifold, and more generally every n-dimensional cycle, in R^N satisfies FillVol(z) ≤ C_n Vol(z)^((n+1)/n). The paper obtains an explicit bound such as C_n < n^n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let C_max be the supremum of FillVol(z)/Vol(z)^((n+1)/n). Federer-Fleming gives finiteness but with ambient dependence. Consider an almost extremal cycle V. If Diam(V) is controlled by Vol(V)^(1/n), the cone inequality gives the desired bound. Otherwise slice V by distance levels from a suitable hyperplane H. By the coarea inequality, some level has small (n−1)-volume; by the inductive isoperimetric inequality in dimension n−1, fill this level by a small n-chain, decomposing V into two cycles. An analytic convexity lemma shows that such a decomposition strictly decreases the weighted sum Vol^((n+1)/n), contradicting almost extremality unless most of V lies in a ball of radius O(Vol(V)^(1/n)). Then a final spherical slice outside that ball has negligible volume and is filled by a cone, while the remaining tiny piece is filled by Federer-Fleming. Induction on n gives constants depending only on n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.4.A, Proposition 3.4.B, and Corollary 3.4.C, decomposition into round cycles
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a compact Riemannian manifold. Suppose every (n−1)-cycle y in X satisfies FillVol(y) ≤ C_(n−1) Vol(y)^(n/(n−1)). Then every n-cycle z can, up to an arbitrarily small residual term, be decomposed as z = z'_0 + Σ z'_μ so that (i) the weighted volume does not increase: Vol(z)^((n+1)/n) ≥ Weight(z'_0) + Σ Vol(z'_μ)^((n+1)/n), (ii) FillVol(z'_0) is arbitrarily small, and (iii) every z'_μ has controlled diameter Diam(z'_μ) ≤ D_n Vol(z'_μ)^(1/n), where D_n depends only on n and C_(n−1). Consequently, if all k-cycles in X for k = 1,...,n satisfy a cone inequality FillVol(y) ≤ C(k) Diam(y) Vol(y)/(k+1), then every n-cycle z satisfies FillVol(z) ≤ C_n Vol(z)^((n+1)/n).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Define Weight(z) as the infimum over decompositions z = Σ z_j of Σ Vol(z_j)^((n+1)/n). Choose a nearly minimizing decomposition. A component that is not sufficiently round can be sliced by a distance function d: X → R. Coarea gives a level with small boundary, and the inductive filling inequality fills that boundary cheaply; replacing the component by the two pieces capped by this filling strictly reduces the weighted volume by a fixed amount. Hence all components except a negligible residual must be round in a quantitative sense. Compactness and a small-cycle filling lemma, obtained by embedding X into R^N and applying Federer-Fleming plus normal projection, convert essential roundness to actual diameter control. Finally, fill each round piece using the cone inequality and sum the estimates; fill the negligible residual using its small filling volume.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 4.1, mass*, compressing property, and coarea in Banach spaces
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a finite-dimensional Banach space L_0, Gromov defines a Haar normalization called mass* by duality with the mass norm on the dual space. If L_0 is an n-dimensional subspace of a Banach space L, there exists a linear projection P: L → L_0 that is distance-nonincreasing relative to a mass*-extremal l^∞ norm on L_0, and hence P is mass*-nonincreasing on n-dimensional chains. Also, if V is an n-dimensional submanifold of a Banach space and d: V → R is distance-nonincreasing, then mass*(V) ≥ ∫ mass*(d^{-1}(t)) dt.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose a mass*-extremal l^∞ norm on L_0, equivalently a minimal-volume parallelepiped norm dominating the Banach norm. The coordinate linear functionals of this l^∞ norm extend from L_0 to L by Hahn-Banach without increasing norm, producing the projection P. Since the l^∞ norm is adapted to mass*, P decreases n-dimensional mass*. The coarea inequality reduces to a linear inequality for an n-parallelotope: its mass* is at least the first height times the mass* of the base. The height statement follows by duality and the same compressing projection argument. Integrating the linear inequality over tangent planes gives the coarea formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.2.A and Theorem 4.2.B, isoperimetric inequalities in Banach and L^∞ spaces
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every n there is a universal constant C_n such that every piecewise smooth n-dimensional cycle z in an arbitrary Banach space L satisfies Fill mass*(z) ≤ C_n mass*(z)^((n+1)/n). If L is isometric to an L^∞ space, then the hyper-Euclidean volume satisfies FillVol(z) ≤ C_n Vol(z)^((n+1)/n), again with C_n depending only on n and with an explicit bound in the paper.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Approximate z by a piecewise linear cycle contained in a finite-dimensional subspace of L. In that finite-dimensional space, some Euclidean-coordinate Federer-Fleming inequality gives an isoperimetric inequality with a finite ambient-dependent constant. To remove ambient dependence, repeat the Section 3.3 argument using the Banach-space coarea inequality for mass* and a cone inequality. For mass*, the cone inequality has an additional dimension-dependent factor comparing mass and mass*. For L^∞ and the chosen hyper-Euclidean volume, Gromov proves a sharper cone inequality by comparing the cone to a spherical cone and extending it distance-nonincreasingly to L^∞. The same extremal decomposition/rounding induction then yields constants depending only on n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.3.A and Theorem 4.3.B, filling radius of cycles in Banach and L^∞ spaces
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every n there is a universal constant const_n such that every n-dimensional cycle z in a Banach space L satisfies FillRad(z ⊂ L) ≤ const_n mass*(z)^(1/n). If L is an L^∞ space, then FillRad(z ⊂ L) ≤ const_n Vol(z)^(1/n). More precisely, in finite-dimensional L one can choose a filling c' of z with Vol(c') ≤ FillVol(z) + δ and support contained in the ε-neighborhood of supp(z), where ε is bounded by a dimension-dependent multiple of FillVol(z)^(1/(n+1)) and hence by a multiple of Vol(z)^(1/n).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume first L finite-dimensional and take an almost volume-minimizing filling c of z. Let d be the distance to supp(z). The slices c ∩ d^{-1}(t) are n-cycles for almost every t, and coarea relates their volumes to the growth of Vol(c ∩ d^{-1}[0,t]). The analytic lemma used in Section 3.3 implies that before a radius controlled by FillVol(z)^(1/(n+1)) there is a slice of arbitrarily small volume. The portion of c inside this radius plus a small Federer-Fleming filling of the residual slice gives a filling supported in the controlled neighborhood. The Banach and L^∞ inequalities from Section 4.2 convert FillVol to Vol(z)^((n+1)/n). Infinite-dimensional cases are obtained by approximating z in finite-dimensional subspaces.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.3.C, density lower bound for minimal fillings
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let V be a closed n-dimensional submanifold or sub-pseudomanifold in a finite-dimensional Banach space L, and let W be a minimal filling of V obtained as a Hausdorff limit of a volume-minimizing sequence of (n+1)-chains. Then for every w ∈ W and every R < dist(w,V), the weak-limit volume satisfies Vol(B_w(R) ∩ W) ≥ A_n R^(n+1), where A_n is a positive constant depending only on n and the isoperimetric constant from Theorem 4.2. The paper also states an analogous existence and inequality for submanifolds in L^∞ using abstract Hausdorff limits.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For an approximating minimizing chain c_i and points w_i → w in its support, set c_i(R) = c_i ∩ B_{w_i}(R) and z_i(R) = ∂c_i(R). Minimality implies that Vol(c_i(R)) is asymptotically no larger than the filling volume of z_i(R), otherwise one could replace c_i(R) by a cheaper filling. The isoperimetric inequality bounds FillVol(z_i(R)) by C_n Vol(z_i(R))^((n+1)/n), while coarea relates Vol(z_i(R)) to the derivative of Vol(c_i(R)). The resulting differential inequality gives the lower bound A_n R^(n+1), unless the volume tends to zero; in that exceptional case one fills a nearby boundary slice by a tiny chain, producing a minimizing sequence with a strictly smaller Hausdorff limit, contradicting minimality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 6.2, isoperimetric inequality for cubical δ-complexes
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every n there are positive constants μ_n and C'_n such that every singular n-dimensional cycle z with Vol(z) < μ_n δ^n in an arbitrary cubical δ-complex K bounds a chain c in K with Vol(c) ≤ C'_n Vol(z)^((n+1)/n), and c is contained in the ε'-neighborhood of supp(z) with ε' ≤ C'_n Vol(z)^(1/n).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If K is a subcomplex of the standard δ-cubical decomposition of an L^∞ space, first fill z in L^∞ using the Section 4 inequalities, then retract the ε-neighborhood of K to K by the coordinatewise map R_ε from Section 6.2, and add the cylinder over z under this retraction. For a general cubical complex, use that sufficiently small-diameter cycles satisfy a cone inequality inside the complex; then apply the decomposition method of Section 3.4. The small-volume condition ensures all constructed chains remain within the scale where the cubical cone estimates apply.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 7.1, Besicovitch lemma for cubes and Gromov's degree-d cube generalization
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let W be an n-dimensional 'cube' of degree d, meaning that ∂W is covered by 2n closed 'faces' F_i and F'_i and maps to the boundary of the standard cube with degree d, sending F_i and F'_i to opposite standard faces. If W is embedded in a Banach space L, then mass*(W) ≥ |d| Π_{i=1}^n dist(F_i,F'_i). In particular, for a Riemannian n-cube C with opposite faces F_i,F'_i, Vol(C) ≥ Π_i dist(F_i,F'_i). Also an (n−1)-dimensional boundary 'cube' V of degree d satisfies FillVol(V) ≥ Fill mass*(V) ≥ |d| Π_i dist(F_i,F'_i).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Set δ_i = dist(F_i,F'_i). Define coordinate functions h_i(w) = min(δ_i, dist(w,F_i)) and h = (h_1,...,h_n): W → Π_i [0,δ_i]. The map h sends the marked faces to the corresponding faces of the rectangular solid and has degree d. Relative to the l^∞ norm on the solid, h is distance-nonincreasing; by the compressing property of mass*, it cannot increase n-dimensional mass*. Since h has degree d, the mass of W is at least |d| times the mass* volume of the solid, which is Π_i δ_i. The classical Riemannian cube case is the degree-one case. The boundary filling-volume lower bound follows by applying the same argument to any filling W of the boundary cube.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 7.1.B, product Besicovitch inequality
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let W be an n-dimensional cube of degree d with opposite faces F_i,F'_i, and let W_0 be a closed oriented k-dimensional manifold or pseudomanifold. Suppose W × W_0 is embedded in a Banach space L. Let mass*(d[W_0]) denote the infimum of the k-dimensional mass* of cycles in W × W_0 that project to d times the fundamental class [W_0]. Then mass*(W × W_0) ≥ mass*(d[W_0]) Π_{i=1}^n dist(F_i × W_0, F'_i × W_0).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the same distance-to-faces map h_0: W × W_0 → Π_i [0,δ_i], with δ_i the distance between the product faces. It is distance-nonincreasing. For a generic point x in the rectangular solid, the fiber h_0^{-1}(x) is a k-cycle whose projection to W_0 represents d[W_0]. The coarea inequality for mass* gives mass*(W × W_0) ≥ ∫ mass*(h_0^{-1}(x)) dx, and every fiber has mass at least mass*(d[W_0]). Integrating over the solid gives the product of the δ_i times mass*(d[W_0]).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 7.2.A, conformal Besicovitch lemma
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let W be an n-dimensional Riemannian 'cube' of degree d. For two subsets A,B ⊂ W define dist^(n)(A,B) as the supremum of dist_φ(A,B) over nonnegative functions φ with ∫_W φ^n ≤ 1, where dist_φ is the path distance obtained by integrating φ along curves. Then the conformal distances between opposite faces satisfy Π_{i=1}^n dist^(n)(F_i,F'_i) ≤ |d|^{-1}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose nonnegative functions φ_i with L^n norm at most one and with dist_{φ_i}(F_i,F'_i) close to δ_i. For each i construct a coordinate function h_i: W → [0,δ_i] that is 1-Lipschitz for the φ_i-weighted path metric and sends F_i,F'_i to opposite endpoints. The combined map h = (h_1,...,h_n) has degree d onto the solid Π[0,δ_i]. Its Jacobian is bounded pointwise by Π φ_i. Integrating and applying Hölder gives |d| Π δ_i ≤ ∫ Π φ_i ≤ Π (∫ φ_i^n)^(1/n) ≤ 1. Taking suprema over φ_i yields the stated product bound.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Appendix 1, Lemma A and Proposition D, mapping diameters and k-radii
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a continuous map f: X → Y between metric spaces define Diam(f) = sup_{x1,x2∈X} [dist_X(x1,x2) − dist_Y(f(x1),f(x2))]. If Diam(f) = δ < ∞, there exists a continuous map Q: Y → L^∞(X) such that Q ∘ f is within δ/2 of the canonical embedding I: X → L^∞(X). Define Diam_k(X) as the infimum of Diam(f) over all k-degenerate maps f, i.e. maps factoring through a k-dimensional polyhedron; define Diam'_k using the supremum of diameters of fibers of maps X → k-dimensional polyhedra. For a complete piecewise Riemannian polyhedron V, Rad_k(V) = (1/2) Diam_k(V) = (1/2) Diam'_k(V).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Given f with Diam(f)=δ, set Q(y)(x) = δ/2 + inf_{x0∈X} [dist_X(x,x0) + dist_Y(y,f(x0))]. This is modeled on the L^∞ extension formula and places Q(f(x)) within δ/2 of I(x). Thus a k-degenerate map of small diameter gives a map into L^∞ close to the canonical embedding, proving Rad_k ≤ Diam_k/2. Conversely, any map V → L^∞(V) close to the canonical embedding produces a k-degenerate map whose distortion of distances is at most twice the closeness, giving the reverse inequality. For complete piecewise Riemannian polyhedra, Diam_k and Diam'_k agree because one may put a sufficiently large metric on the target polyhedron so that small fiber diameter implies small map diameter.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Appendix 1, Example E1, surface area controls one-dimensional width for genus zero
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If V is a connected complete Riemannian surface of genus zero, then Rad_1(V) = (1/2) Diam'_1(V) ≤ Area(V)^(1/2). More generally, for a complete surface of genus g < ∞, the same argument gives Diam'_1(V) ≤ 2(g+1) Area(V)^(1/2).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix v_0 ∈ V and take the distance function f(v)=dist(v,v_0). Replace f by its associated connected map to a tree: points in a level are identified by connected components of level sets. For genus zero the associated graph is a tree, because otherwise a loop in the graph would lift to a curve intersecting a level component nontrivially, contradicting the vanishing of the relevant intersection index. If a level component has diameter δ, then for 0 < p < δ/2 preceding level components have length at least δ − 2p. Coarea gives Area(V) ≥ ∫_0^{δ/2} 2p dp = δ^2/4, so every fiber has diameter at most 2 Area(V)^(1/2). Proposition D then gives the Rad_1 bound.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Appendix 1, F2 and F2'', slicing-volume lower bound through filling radius
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let V be an n-dimensional complete Riemannian manifold and let f: V → P be a smooth proper map to an (n−m)-dimensional manifold or polyhedron P, satisfying the transversality/triangulation regularity conditions stated in the paper, for instance f is real analytic or generic C^∞. Let Vol_m(f) = sup_{p∈P} Vol_m(f^{-1}(p)), with fibers counted with geometric multiplicity. Then FillRad(V) ≤ const (Vol_m(f))^(1/m), where the constant depends on the relevant dimensions and on the filling constants used in the construction. Equivalently, any such slicing of a manifold with large filling radius has at least one m-dimensional fiber of large volume.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Triangulate P finely and transversely to f so that the pullbacks of k-simplices have controlled volume in terms of Vol_m(f) and an arbitrarily small mesh parameter. First fill the fibers over vertices by (m+1)-chains using the filling-radius/filling-volume inequalities. For each edge, the pullback of the edge together with the two previously chosen vertex fillings forms a cycle; fill it by a small chain. Continue inductively over the skeleta of P: over each k-simplex, the pullback plus the fillings already constructed on its boundary forms a cycle, which is filled using the isoperimetric inequality. At the top dimension this constructs a filling of V lying within a distance controlled by (Vol_m(f))^(1/m), proving the filling-radius estimate.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Appendix 2, Theorems B1 and B2, contraction-radius bounds for polyhedra
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every n there is a universal constant const_n such that any compact n-dimensional polyhedron V with a piecewise Riemannian or Finsler metric satisfies Cont_{n−1}Rad(V) ≤ const_n Vol(V)^(1/n). Here Cont_{n−1}Rad(V) is the least radius ε for which the canonical embedding V ⊂ L^∞(V) becomes homotopic inside its ε-neighborhood to a map factoring through an (n−1)-dimensional polyhedron. A stronger version, denoted Cont'_{n−1}, also controls the (n+1)-volume of an explicit (n−1)-contraction W by Vol(W)^(1/(n+1)) ≤ ε.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof extends the filling inequalities from cycles to contractions of polyhedra. One first proves that small polyhedra in Euclidean space or a compact Finsler manifold have controlled contractions by a Federer-Fleming type argument. Then a decomposition lemma analogous to Section 3.4 breaks an n-polyhedron into round pieces plus negligible pieces, using distance-function slicing and lower-dimensional contractions of slice boundaries. If a space satisfies cone inequalities in all dimensions up to n, these decompositions imply contraction inequalities. Finally, since Banach spaces satisfy the needed cone inequalities, applying the result to the canonical embedding V ⊂ L^∞(V) gives the stated bound.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Appendix 2, Theorem C4, cone inequalities imply contraction inequalities
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a complete Finsler manifold. Suppose that for k = 1,...,n, every sufficiently small k-dimensional singular polyhedron in X satisfies a cone inequality allowing a (k−1)-contraction with volume controlled by diameter times k-volume. Then for k = 1,...,n the corresponding contraction inequalities hold: small k-dimensional singular polyhedra have (k−1)-contractions whose radius and filling volume are bounded by constants times Vol^(1/k), with constants depending only on the cone data and k.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The compact case follows from the decomposition argument: non-round pieces can be sliced and contracted in lower dimension, so only round pieces remain; round pieces are contracted by the cone inequality. For noncompact complete X, localize by taking a compact smooth region Y containing a large neighborhood of the given polyhedron and replacing the metric in the interior by a conformally blown-up complete metric f(y) = (1 − p^{-1} dist(y,∂Y))^{-1}. Cone inequalities transfer to this new compact region with modified constants, and the normal projection from the blown-up collar back to a compact sublevel is distance-nonincreasing. Applying the compact result and returning to the original metric gives a contraction supported within the desired radius.

proof_techniques.name: Canonical L^∞ embedding and 1-Lipschitz extension
proof_techniques.description: The paper systematically replaces an abstract metric or Riemannian manifold V by its canonical isometric embedding v ↦ dist(v,·) into L^∞(V). The crucial feature is injectivity: any 1-Lipschitz map into L^∞ from a subspace extends to the ambient metric space. This makes filling radius and filling volume intrinsic while allowing chains to live in a linear Banach space where Federer-Fleming and cone constructions can be performed.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a degree-one map between rectangles, this suggests encoding relative cycles or slices in an ambient injective space, then filling them with controlled volume without needing a Lipschitz bound in all directions. It is especially relevant if one constructs a sweepout of the target rectangle by images of lower-dimensional slices and then fills boundaries.

proof_techniques.name: Federer-Fleming cubical deformation to skeleta
proof_techniques.description: Gromov uses a cubical grid and radial projections from cube centers to push an n-dimensional cycle or submanifold toward an n- or (n−1)-skeleton. A suitable translate avoids the bad dual grid, and averaging over projections controls volume. The deformation has two simultaneous outputs: an isoperimetric filling and a support/radius control of order Vol^(1/n).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a template for discretizing a rectangle and replacing images of slices by chains in a skeleton while preserving degree information. In a k-dilation problem, cubical deformation can help convert analytic volume bounds on images of slices into combinatorial sweepouts.

proof_techniques.name: Cone inequality plus coarea slicing
proof_techniques.description: A basic cycle of small diameter is filled by coning, with volume at most diameter times boundary volume. If a cycle is not small in diameter, it is sliced by distance functions; coarea finds a low-volume slice, and an inductive filling of that slice decomposes the cycle into smaller parts. This is the backbone of the dimension-only isoperimetric inequality.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The rectangle theorem has natural distance functions given by coordinate directions and faces. Coarea-style slicing can find good lower-dimensional sections, while cone/filling inequalities can cap off their boundaries with controlled 2- or 3-volume.

proof_techniques.name: Weighted-volume decomposition into round pieces
proof_techniques.description: The paper introduces the weighted quantity Σ Vol(piece)^((n+1)/n) and shows that if a piece is not round, slicing it and filling the slice strictly decreases this weighted volume. Iterating yields a decomposition into pieces whose diameter is controlled by their volume. Round pieces are then filled by cone inequalities.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a proof needs to fill many image cycles arising from a sweepout, this method gives a way to localize fillings and prevent long thin cycles from causing uncontrolled filling radius. It may help when only 2-dimensional volumes are controlled by the dilation hypothesis.

proof_techniques.name: Filling radius from an almost-minimal filling via distance levels
proof_techniques.description: Given a near-minimal filling of a cycle, Gromov studies the distance-to-boundary function on the filling. Coarea forces a small residual slice before a radius comparable to Vol(boundary)^(1/n), otherwise the filling volume would exceed the isoperimetric bound. The residual slice is then filled separately by a tiny Federer-Fleming filling.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This converts upper bounds on volumes of cycles into upper bounds on how far their fillings need to go. In rectangle dilation arguments, such controlled fillings are needed to build extensions over cells without losing control of where chains lie.

proof_techniques.name: Besicovitch distance-to-faces map
proof_techniques.description: For a cube with marked opposite faces, define coordinates by distances to one face of each opposite pair, truncated at the face separation. This gives a degree-d map to a rectangular solid that is 1-Lipschitz for the l^∞ metric. Since it has nonzero degree, the domain volume is at least the product of the face separations.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem is about rectangles and degree-one maps. Besicovitch's lemma is the direct source of lower bounds that combine side lengths and degree, and it suggests constructing auxiliary metrics or chains whose distances between opposite target faces are known.

proof_techniques.name: Product Besicovitch and fiberwise coarea
proof_techniques.description: Gromov extends Besicovitch's lemma to products W × W_0 by projecting to the cube factor and applying coarea. Generic fibers over the cube represent a prescribed multiple of [W_0], so their minimal mass supplies an additional lower factor in the total volume.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The rectangle estimate involves mixed products of side lengths rather than only total volume. Product Besicovitch is a natural model for deriving inequalities where some directions are treated as a base cube and the remaining directions contribute through slice or fiber volumes.

proof_techniques.name: Slicing-volume lower bounds via filling radius
proof_techniques.description: Appendix 1 builds fillings over a triangulated base of a map f: V → P. If all m-dimensional fibers had small volume, one could fill fibers over vertices, then over edges, and inductively over higher-dimensional cells, ultimately producing a small-radius filling of V. Thus large filling radius forces a large fiber.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A common strategy for dilation estimates is to consider coordinate projections of the domain or target and estimate volumes of images of fibers. This result gives a mechanism for turning an assumed small 2-dilation into a contradiction with a necessary sweepout or filling-radius lower bound.

proof_techniques.name: mass* volume and l^∞ compressing projections
proof_techniques.description: In Banach spaces, Gromov uses the dual mass* volume because it satisfies both a coarea inequality and a projection/compressing property. Extremal l^∞ norms allow Hahn-Banach to build projections that do not increase the relevant dimension of volume.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Although the problem is Riemannian and Euclidean, k-dilation concerns exterior powers rather than ordinary Lipschitz constants. The mass*/projection formalism is a useful analogue when one needs maps that may not be 1-Lipschitz but control k-dimensional volumes.

proof_techniques.name: Inductive extension over skeleta by filling cell boundaries
proof_techniques.description: Several arguments in the paper construct a global filling or contraction by triangulating a parameter space and filling successively over vertices, edges, and higher-dimensional simplices. At each step the boundary cycle has already been built and is filled using an isoperimetric inequality, with coarea or volume assumptions controlling the size.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Degree-one rectangle maps can be analyzed by subdividing the target rectangle and trying to lift or fill images of cell boundaries. This skeletal induction is a plausible way to convert local 2-volume bounds into global homological information.

proof_techniques.name: Minimal filling density lower bound
proof_techniques.description: A minimal filling cannot have too little volume in any ball away from its boundary. Otherwise one could cut out that part and replace it by a cheaper isoperimetric filling of its boundary slice, contradicting minimality. This gives uniform lower density estimates depending only on dimension.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a contradiction proof constructs a minimal or near-minimal chain spanning part of a rectangle, density lower bounds can force a certain amount of volume in balls or slabs, which may combine with 2-dilation bounds on slices.

proof_techniques.name: Width/radius equivalence through maps to lower-dimensional complexes
proof_techniques.description: Appendix 1 identifies k-radius in L^∞ with half of a k-dimensional mapping diameter. Thus being close to a k-dimensional complex, being sliced into small fibers, and having small contraction radius are quantitatively related.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Rectangle side-length inequalities often have a width interpretation. This framework suggests proving that small 2-dilation would force a target or sweepout to have too small a width, contradicting degree or face-separation constraints.

other_useful_info: The paper explicitly treats piecewise smooth and Finsler settings in many arguments, so its filling and slicing estimates are robust under polyhedral approximation. Section 7 records Besicovitch's cube inequality as an inverse isoperimetric inequality and extends it to degree-d 'cubes' and products, which is particularly close in spirit to rectangle side-length estimates. Section 3 cites Federer-Fleming [28] for cubical deformation and Section 4 adapts it to Banach/L^∞ spaces; Section 2.3 mentions Michael-Simon [58] as another source of dimension-only isoperimetric inequalities. Appendix 1 and Appendix 2 are valuable for sweepout arguments because they convert bounds on fiber volumes into filling-radius or contraction-radius bounds.
why_relevant: Gromov's paper is a foundational source for filling radius, Federer-Fleming-type isoperimetric inequalities, sweepouts, and systolic/filling arguments. Guth's width-volume and rectangle dilation estimates build directly on this circle of ideas.

title: The homotopy groups of the integral cycle groups
why_relevant: Almgren's paper is the foundational source for the topology of spaces of cycles and the discrete complexes-of-cycles formalism later used by Gromov and Guth. It is relevant for rigorously encoding the sweepouts induced by a degree-one map of pairs.
